Skills prioritários: fluência com frases prontas (pattern-based), vocabulário de alta frequência, produção oral guiada com scaffolding progressivo e redução da dependência do português.

O objetivo geral declarado no documento é 'desenvolver segurança na comunicação em inglês, aumentando vocabulário e reduzindo a dependência do português'.

perfil.resumoExecutivo: Gabriela é uma adolescente de 15-16 anos, estudante em São Paulo, com uma personalidade comunicativa, leve e curiosa sobre o mundo. Seu inglês está no início da jornada — nível A1+ — mas ela já demonstra uma base fonética sólida e, o mais importante, disposição genuína para tentar falar mesmo quando não sabe tudo. Seu grande motivador é concreto: uma viagem para a Europa, com foco na França, prevista para fevereiro de 2027. Esse evento funciona como âncora emocional de toda a trilha. Gabriela se anima com séries, celebridades, fofoca leve e cultura pop — tópicos que o programa deve usar como combustível para o aprendizado. Sua principal barreira é a dependência do português: ela ainda processa em PT e tenta traduzir para EN, o que trava a fluência. O desafio pedagógico central é criar um ambiente de baixo filtro afetivo, alta exposição e alta relevância pessoal para que ela reduza esse padrão progressivamente e chegue à viagem com autonomia comunicativa real.

necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoNivel: Nível A1+ (regras do rulebook A1 com sensibilidade à transição para A2): tradução PT-BR obrigatória em todo o material. Mínimo de 4-5 palavras novas por aula (expandindo para 5-7 conforme progresso). Frases-modelo de 2-6 palavras com drilling intenso. CCQs em português. Practice: matching imagem+palavra, repetição guiada, fill-in-blanks simples. Production muito guiada, máx 5-10 min, com script de suporte e scaffolding explícito. Mídias: vídeos curtos com legendas PT-BR e EN simultâneas. Foco em pattern-based learning (frases prontas reutilizáveis). Proporção geral/foco: 70% geral / 30% foco temático (viagem, séries, pop culture).
necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoIdade: Adolescente 13-17 anos (Regra 136 — peer-like): professor deve adotar tom de parceiro, não de autoridade. Temas obrigatórios: redes sociais, escola, tecnologia, séries, celebridades. Mídias: séries teen e pop culture (Gossip Girl, Friends, Gilmore Girls — todos já na trilha). Ritmo acelerado com variações frequentes. Gamificação e micro-desafios entre aulas. Feedback via mensagem curta e motivacional (não relatório formal). Evitar gramática como tópico central — gramática apenas como suporte pontual.
necessidadesPedagogicas.estiloAprendizagem: Aprendiz cinestésico com componente lúdico: materiais devem priorizar fazer sobre memorizar. Flashcards físicos (presencial), jogos de role play, simulações de situações reais, atividades de movimento quando possível. Drilling com variação de contexto (não mecânico). Frases prontas aplicadas imediatamente em situações simuladas. Revisão espaçada integrada a novos contextos — nunca revisão isolada.
necessidadesPedagogicas.ritmoIdeal: Ritmo acelerado com variações a cada 10-12 minutos. Estrutura sugerida por aula de 60 min: 5 min aquecimento (pergunta pessoal ou mini-jogo), 10 min vocabulário novo com contexto visual, 15 min prática guiada (drilling + matching), 20 min produção oral em pares ou simulação, 8 min feedback e recast suave, 2 min preview do próximo tema. Nunca mais de 12 minutos na mesma atividade.
tiposExercicio: Matching imagem + palavra com flashcards físicos
Drilling de frases prontas com variação de contexto (pattern drills)
Role play guiado com script parcial — preencher lacunas
True/false e múltipla escolha com imagens (CCQ visual)
Recontar cena de série com frases-modelo fornecidas
Mini simulações de viagem (comprar ingresso, pedir direção, fazer check-in)
Jogos de vocabulário (Taboo simplificado, Word Association)
Fill-in-the-blanks com banco de palavras para produção escrita mínima
Listen and repeat com áudio ElevenLabs para ajuste fonético
'Hot seat' — aluno responde perguntas pessoais simples cronometrado

Gilmore Girls (série) — vocabulário rico em contexto natural (já na trilha)

necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoModalidade: Presencial: explorar ao máximo o contato físico com o material. Flashcards impressos para drilling de vocabulário. Post-its para atividades de categorização (agrupar palavras por tema). Dados ou cartas para role plays aleatórios (sortear situação de viagem, sortear personagem). Quadro branco para mind maps de vocabulário construídos juntos. Objetos da sala como gatilhos de vocabulário (cadeira, janela, mochila — naming games). Movimento físico em atividades de resposta corporal total (TPR) para vocabulário concreto.

indicacaoProfessor.perfilIdeal: PERFIL ACADÊMICO: Professor com sólida formação em abordagem comunicativa (CLT) e experiência comprovada com adolescentes em nível A1-A2. Certificação CELTA ou equivalente preferível. Especialidade em conversação e inglês para viagens. Inglês Americano como padrão (Regra 143), sem sotaque regional marcado. Experiência com técnicas de scaffolding progressivo, pattern-based learning e recast — capacidade de corrigir sem interromper o fluxo da fala. Conhecimento de cultura pop contemporânea (séries, celebridades) é diferencialmente importante para este perfil. PERFIL DE PERSONALIDADE: Professor altamente energético, com senso de humor genuíno e capacidade de criar clima de descontração sem perder o foco pedagógico. Tom peer-like — parceiro, não professor-autoridade. Feedback gentil e motivacional (nunca formal ou corretivo em excesso). Alta tolerância ao caos criativo de uma aluna adolescente que vai misturar PT e EN constantemente. Capacidade de variar o ritmo da aula a cada 10-12 minutos e de usar o interesse genuíno dela (séries, fofoca, viagem) como alavanca de aprendizado. Professor que genuinamente assiste séries ou se interessa por cultura pop terá vantagem significativa de conexão com Gabriela.
justificativa: Gabriela tem alta tolerância ao erro e energia constante — professor excessivamente formal ou centrado em gramática vai matar o engajamento que é, segundo o próprio diagnóstico, o principal fator de sucesso do programa

Estilo de aprendizagem cinestésico e preferência por aulas dinâmicas exigem professor que domine técnicas ativas (role play, jogos, simulações) e não dependa de explicação gramatical como estrutura da aula

A dependência do português é a barreira central — professor precisa ter paciência estratégica para não punir o uso do PT mas gradualmente reduzir o scaffolding em português ao longo das 47 aulas, mantendo a confiança da aluna intacta

perfil.justificativaPrograma: O programa de 47 aulas de 60 minutos, 3 vezes por semana, foi desenhado especificamente para o perfil de Gabriela: adolescente A1+ com viagem marcada para a Europa em fevereiro de 2027. O prazo é concreto e motivador — funciona como âncora emocional da jornada inteira. A frequência de 3x/semana é ideal para criar o ambiente de 'high-input' que o diagnóstico recomenda, garantindo exposição constante ao idioma sem sobrecarregar a rotina escolar. A trilha em 9 fases respeita a lógica do desenvolvimento adolescente: começa em território seguro (apresentação pessoal, rotina), passa pelos temas de maior engajamento emocional (séries, pop culture, celebridades), atravessa temas de médio prazo (carreira, saúde) e converge nas Fases 8 e 9 para o objetivo final — intercâmbio, viagem e simulações integradas. A modalidade presencial favorece as técnicas cinestésicas necessárias para este perfil. As 2+ horas de estudo autônomo disponíveis fora das aulas devem ser direcionadas para consumo de séries e conteúdos de interesse pessoal, transformando o lazer em imersão. O resultado esperado ao final das 47 aulas é uma aluna capaz de se comunicar com autonomia em situações reais de turismo — o exato cenário que a espera na França.
perguntasValidacao: Gabriela, você tem 15 ou 16 anos? O documento da consultoria registra 15, mas o cadastro indica 16 — precisamos confirmar para o material de aula.
Você já teve aulas de inglês antes, na escola ou em curso? Queremos entender se existe alguma base gramatical anterior ou se é um começo do zero.
Quando você pensa em chegar na França em 2027, qual é a situação que mais te preocupa — aquela que você imagina e pensa 'espero saber falar inglês nessa hora'?
Você usa o inglês de alguma forma hoje? Legenda de série, jogo, letra de música — qualquer coisa conta.
Tem alguma série ou filme que você assistiria mesmo sem ser tarefa — que você já ama? Queremos garantir que o material usa exatamente o que você curte de verdade.
conflitosIdentificados: CONFLITO DE IDADE: Gestor declarou 16 anos no Bloco 1; diagnóstico pedagógico da transcrição registra 15 anos. Diferença pequena mas relevante para adequação de material (regra de faixa etária). Requer confirmação antes da Aula 1.

CONFLITO DE QUANTIDADE DE AULAS: Gestor declarou 47 aulas no Bloco 2; a trilha da transcrição foi estruturada em 48 aulas. Diferença de 1 aula — verificar se a Aula 48 (Simulação final) está incluída no contrato ou é aula extra.

AUSÊNCIA DE STAKE E VITÓRIA DECLARADOS: Bloco 4 não foi preenchido pelo gestor. Os campos foram inferidos a partir da transcrição e observações do professor. Recomenda-se validação direta com a aluna e/ou responsável para garantir que a promessa do programa ressoa emocionalmente.

Pronúncia descrita como 'boa', sem travas articulatórias, o que sugere alguma exposição a conteúdo em inglês (possivelmente via séries ou cultura pop) mesmo que informal.
historico.evidencia: Transcrição registra 'pronúncia boa — sem travas articulatórias, base sólida para a fluência futura' e 'disposição para tentar se comunicar mesmo com apoio do português'. Esses sinais indicam exposição informal prévia, mas sem histórico formal declarado.

valor.stake: Não ter autonomia comunicativa mínima para navegar situações reais de viagem (turismo, transporte, interações sociais) em um país de língua não nativa, dependendo de terceiros ou ficando em silêncio em situações básicas.
valor.vitoria: Chegar à França capaz de se comunicar em inglês com confiança em situações de viagem — pedir informações, interagir com pessoas, descrever experiências — sentindo-se independente e segura.

eventoAlvo.evidencia: Bloco 3 confirma viagem para Europa/França em fevereiro de 2027. A transcrição reforça com as Aulas 43-44 ('França — cultura' e 'França — diálogo simples (turismo)') e Aula 46 ('Role play — viagem'). Professor reportou animação explícita no tópico de viagem.

perfil.transcricao:
OBJETIVO GERAL
Desenvolver segurança na comunicação em inglês, aumentando vocabulário e reduzindo a dependência do português, com foco em engajamento e consistência ao longo de toda a trilha.
Documento confidencial —uso pedagógico interno
Diagnóstico Pedagógico
Gabriela Castro Pires —A1+
PERFIL GERAL
Aluna jovem (15 anos) em transição A1 → A2, com potencial comunicativo claro, mas ainda construindo seu sistema linguístico em inglês (interlanguage). Tem dependência forte do português e precisa de exposição constante, repetição espaçada e estímulocontínuo. Para esse perfil, o engajamento é o principal fator de sucesso.
PONTOS DE FORÇA
PONTOS DE ATENÇÃO
Pronúncia boa
Sem travas articulatórias —base sólida para a fluência futura.
Disposição para tentar se comunicar
Mesmo com apoio do português —sinal positivo de evolução.
Forte dependência do português
Reduzir esse padrão é o primeiro objetivo do plano.
Necessidade de exposição alta e variada
High-input environment com repetição estruturada para sustentar o engajamento.
Fator etário e motivacional
Adolescente, engajamento é variável crítica. Aulas com clima leve (baixo affective filter) e alta relevância pessoal rendem muito mais.
IMPLICAÇÃO PEDAGÓGICA
Sugere-se uma abordagem comunicativa, leve e sem pressão, com apoio gradual (scaffolding) constante e ensino por padrões (pattern-based learning) —frases prontas que ela reutiliza. A correção pode ser feita de forma suave, por reformulação (recast,sem interromper a fala), e os temas podem ser escolhidos a partir dos interesses pessoais dela. A gramática entra como apoio,não como protagonista.
FORMATO DE ESTUDO
PRE-CLASS
Material leve, visual e dinâmico, com alto apelo de interesse pessoal. Introdução ao vocabulário do tema com objetivo de dar segurança para participação ativa em aula.
IN-CLASS
Aula altamente interativa e leve, com conversação guiada e atividades dinâmicas —jogos, perguntas pessoais, simulações. Menos teoria, mais prática. Correções naturais e gramática pontual como suporte.
POST-CLASS
Feedback leve e motivacional, com reforço de pontos-chave e sugestões simples (vídeos, séries, conteúdos curtos) que mantêm contato com o inglês entre as aulas.

temas.tema: Who Is Gabriela? — Diagnostic Session + Personal Introduction Baseline
temas.focoLinguistico: Diagnostic focus: present simple (to be + have), personal pronouns, basic personal information vocabulary; Key patterns: 'My name is…', 'I'm from…', 'I'm … years old', 'I live in…', 'I like…', 'I want to…'; Vocabulary: name, age, city, school, hobby, travel, dream, favorite; Diagnostic targets: pronunciation quality, hesitation patterns, L1 interference, comfort with speaking English spontaneously
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Ice-breaker: Teacher introduces themselves fully in English using a visual mind map on paper, then invites Gabriela to point to any bubble she understands. This reduces pressure and activates receptive vocabulary. Phase 2 (20 min) — Diagnostic speaking task: Teacher asks open questions ('Tell me about yourself', 'What do you like doing?', 'Why do you want to learn English?') and records notes on fluency, vocabulary range, pronunciation, and L1 switch frequency — NOT correcting, just observing. Phase 3 (20 min) — Guided self-introduction: Teacher provides a printed 'My Profile Card' with blanks (Name / Age / City / School / Hobbies / Dream Trip / Favorite Series) and Gabriela fills it in English with scaffolded support. She then reads it aloud. Phase 4 (15 min) — Goal-setting ritual: Teacher and Gabriela co-create a 'Victory Wall' poster listing her 3 big English goals anchored to Paris 2027. Written in English together. Posted visually. This creates emotional investment. Phase 5 (10 min) — Debrief + preview: Teacher shares one genuine strength observed (e.g., pronunciation) and one focus area. Gabriela records a 60-second baseline audio: 'Hi, my name is Gabriela. I'm from São Paulo. I like…' — this becomes her benchmark for progress.
temas.homework: 1) Record a 60-second audio in English saying: your name, your age, your city, your school, 2 hobbies, and one sentence about your dream trip to France. Save it — you'll compare this to your final recording in Aula 48. (15 min) | 2) Write your 'My Profile Card' cleanly in your English notebook with illustrations or stickers — make it personal and fun. (10 min) | 3) Watch the first 5 minutes of Friends S01E01 with English subtitles. Write down 3 words or phrases you understood. (15 min)

temas.tema: Hello, Nice to Meet You! — Greetings, Introductions & the Alphabet in Real Contexts
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb 'to be' (am/is/are) — affirmative, negative, question forms; personal pronouns (I, you, he, she, we, they); subject-verb agreement at sentence level; Vocabulary (10+ words): hello, hi, good morning, good afternoon, good evening, nice to meet you, what's your name, how are you, fine, great, spell, last name, first name; Expressions: 'Nice to meet you too', 'How do you spell that?', 'My last name is Pires — P-I-R-E-S'; Phonics focus: English alphabet pronunciation, especially letters often confused by Portuguese speakers (A, E, G, H, I, J, R, W, Y)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up: Teacher and Gabriela greet each other in 5 different ways (formal, casual, energetic, sleepy, excited) using the target expressions. Repetition through role-play variation keeps it fun. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces 'to be' using Gabriela's Profile Card from Aula 1. 'You are Gabriela. You are 15. You are from São Paulo.' Then flips: 'I am your teacher. I am…' Visual grammar on paper with color-coded pronoun-verb pairs. Phase 3 (20 min) — Alphabet drilling with a purpose: Teacher explains that in France, Gabriela will need to spell her name at hotels, airports, and ticket counters. Practice spelling G-A-B-R-I-E-L-A and P-I-R-E-S aloud, then spell 5 celebrity names she knows (e.g., Blake Lively, Penn Badgley from Gossip Girl). Makes drilling feel relevant. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play 'Check-in at a French hotel': Teacher plays receptionist. 'Good evening! Can I have your name please?' Gabriela responds, spells her last name, says her city, answers 'How are you?' This simulates a real Paris scenario using today's grammar. Phase 5 (15 min) — Drilling + wrap-up: Gabriela writes 5 'to be' sentences about herself and 3 about a Gossip Girl character. Read aloud. Teacher gives immediate positive corrective feedback.
temas.homework: 1) Practice spelling your full name, your best friend's name, and the name of your favorite café or place in São Paulo — aloud, in English letters. Record yourself doing it. (10 min) | 2) In your notebook, write 8 sentences using 'to be': 4 about yourself, 2 about a Friends character, 2 negative sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'English Alphabet Pronunciation' (any clear American English version, ~5 min). Note the 3 letters that sound most different from Portuguese.

temas.tema: This Is My World — Talking About People, Places & Things Around Me
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: demonstrative pronouns (this, that, these, those); indefinite articles (a/an); definite article (the) — introduction; plural nouns (regular -s/-es); 'to be' in questions and short answers ('Is this…?' 'Yes, it is / No, it isn't'); Vocabulary (12 words): friend, family, school, class, teacher, book, phone, city, neighborhood, street, building, place; Expressions: 'This is my friend…', 'That's my school', 'These are my things', 'Is this your…?'; Context: Gabriela's daily environment in São Paulo + objects she carries
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up with real objects: Teacher asks Gabriela to put 5 personal items on the table (phone, notebook, pen, water bottle, etc.). Practice 'This is my phone', 'That is your pen' — physical, tactile, zero abstract grammar. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces demonstratives and articles using a printed visual with Gabriela's name and photos of São Paulo landmarks (Paulista Avenue, MASP, her school neighborhood). 'This is São Paulo. That is Paris.' Grammar emerges from context, not a table. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'My World Map' activity: Gabriela draws a simple map of her week on paper — home, school, her favorite place in SP. Labels each in English using target vocabulary + articles. Teacher circulates and prompts: 'Is this your neighborhood? What's that?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Gossip Girl scene adaptation: Teacher creates a simple printed dialogue between Blair and Serena introducing their world ('This is my school. That's my best friend.') Gabriela reads Blair's lines, teacher reads Serena's. Then they swap characters. Debrief: which sentences did she feel confident with? Phase 5 (15 min) — Sentence construction race: Teacher calls out a Portuguese phrase, Gabriela writes/says the English version as fast as possible. Focus on 'this/that/these/those' + articles. 10 rounds.
temas.homework: 1) Take 5 photos of places or things in your daily life in São Paulo (school corridor, your bedroom, your lunch, etc.) and write 1 English sentence per photo using 'this is' or 'these are'. Save them in a note on your phone. (20 min) | 2) Write 10 sentences in your notebook — 5 affirmative, 3 negative, 2 questions — using 'to be' + demonstratives + articles. (15 min) | 3) Rewatch a scene from Gossip Girl (any scene from S01) and count how many times you hear 'this' or 'that'. Write the number in your notebook.

temas.tema: My Daily Life — Describing Routines in São Paulo with the Present Simple
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple — affirmative (I/you/we/they + base verb; he/she/it + verb-s); frequency adverbs (always, usually, sometimes, never, every day); time expressions (in the morning, at night, on weekdays); Vocabulary (12 words): wake up, get up, go to school, have breakfast, study, watch, sleep, start, finish, eat, take the bus/metro, exercise; Expressions: 'I usually wake up at…', 'I always have breakfast before school', 'On weekdays, I…', 'I never…'; Context: Gabriela's actual São Paulo school-day routine
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Routine brainstorm: Teacher asks 'What's the first thing you do every day?' in English, gestures, mimes. Gabriela guesses and responds. Build a quick list of her actual routine verbs — not invented, HERS. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Using a printed timeline (6am to 11pm), teacher models 'I wake up at 7am. I usually take the metro to school.' Then maps Gabriela's real routine onto the timeline using her own information from the brainstorm. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Gabriela's Day vs. Rory Gilmore's Day': Teacher creates a simple split chart. Left side: Gabriela's routine (student fills in). Right side: Rory Gilmore from Gilmore Girls routine (teacher provides in English). Gabriela compares: 'Rory always studies in the morning. I usually study at night.' Genuine comparison using real character she loves. Phase 4 (20 min) — Oral production: Gabriela describes her full day in English without looking at notes — teacher prompts with timeline images. Goal: 8+ sentences. Teacher notes hesitation and L1 switches for later feedback. Phase 5 (15 min) — Grammar focus: Third person singular -s. Teacher introduces: 'She wakes up. She studies. She watches Gilmore Girls.' Gabriela writes 5 sentences about Rory in third person. Debrief on the -s rule.
temas.homework: 1) Write your full daily routine in English in your notebook — at least 10 sentences, mixing frequency adverbs (always, usually, sometimes, never). Include morning, afternoon, and night. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'This is my day in São Paulo.' Describe your routine naturally, as if talking to a new friend abroad. (15 min) | 3) Watch the first breakfast scene in any Gilmore Girls episode. Write down 3 routine verbs you hear in English.

temas.tema: Do You Like It? — Expressing Likes, Dislikes & Preferences (Pop Culture Edition)
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for expressing preferences — 'I like / I love / I enjoy / I don't like / I hate + noun/gerund (-ing)'; question forms with 'do': 'Do you like…?', 'What do you like?'; short answers: 'Yes, I do / No, I don't'; Vocabulary (12 words): series, episode, season, character, actor, celebrity, romantic, drama, comedy, thriller, celebrity, favorite; Expressions: 'I'm obsessed with…', 'I can't stand…', 'I'm a huge fan of…', 'What's your favorite…?', 'Have you seen…?' (receptive only); Context: Gossip Girl, Friends, Gilmore Girls, Notting Hill, celebrities Gabriela knows
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Like or hate?' warm-up: Teacher holds up printed images of shows/movies (Gossip Girl, Friends, a horror movie, a soap opera, action film) — Gabriela reacts in English only: 'I love this!', 'I don't like that.' Pure oral reaction, no writing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher draws the 'Preference Ladder' on paper: hate → don't like → it's okay → like → love → obsessed. Gabriela places her shows on the ladder using full sentences. Grammar emerges naturally. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Hot Takes' game: Teacher reads out celebrity or show names. Gabriela gives a 2-3 sentence opinion in English using target structures. 'I love Blair Waldorf because she is smart and stylish. I don't like Chuck Bass in season 1. He is mean.' Teacher drills sentence structure after each response, not interrupting flow. Phase 4 (20 min) — Roleplay: Gabriela meets a French student at an international school. Teacher plays the French student: 'Do you like French films? What series do you watch?' Gabriela must respond and ask back. 5-minute dialogue, then debrief. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes 10 preference sentences — 5 likes, 3 dislikes, 2 questions she would ask a new international friend.
temas.homework: 1) Write a 'My Favorites List' in English in your notebook: favorite series (3 sentences), favorite movie (3 sentences), favorite celebrity (3 sentences). Use 'I love', 'I like', 'I don't like', 'I hate'. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio: 'My top 3 favorite series and why.' Speak naturally — no reading, just talk. (10 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of Friends with English subtitles. Count how many times you hear 'I like', 'I love', or 'I don't like'. Write the sentences in your notebook.

temas.tema: Numbers, Time & Dates — The Tools You Need to Survive in Paris
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: cardinal numbers (1–1000+); ordinal numbers (1st–31st); telling time in American English ('It's three fifteen', 'It's half past two', 'It's a quarter to five'); dates in American English format (March 15th, February 3rd, 2027); question forms: 'What time is it?', 'When is your flight?', 'How much is it?'; Vocabulary (12 words): o'clock, quarter, half past, noon, midnight, flight, arrival, departure, ticket, price, total, change (money); Expressions: 'Excuse me, what time is it?', 'My flight is on February 3rd', 'How much does this cost?', 'Here's my ticket'; Paris 2027 anchor throughout
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Number warm-up: Teacher calls out numbers in random order (up to 1000), Gabriela writes them in digits. Then reverses: teacher shows digits, Gabriela says aloud. Fast pace, fun. Phase 2 (15 min) — Clock drilling: Teacher draws analog clocks on paper with different times. Gabriela says the time aloud in American English. Focus on quarter past, half past, quarter to — often confused. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Paris Schedule' simulation: Teacher creates a printed 'Trip Itinerary for Gabriela Pires — Paris, February 2027' with 6 fake events (museum visit 10:30am, lunch 1:15pm, Eiffel Tower 4:45pm, etc.). Gabriela reads the itinerary aloud, answers questions: 'What time is the museum visit?', 'When does she have lunch?' Real-world task anchored to her trip. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play at a Paris ticket counter: Teacher is the ticket seller at the Louvre. 'Hello! One ticket please. How much is it?' Gabriela buys a ticket, asks the time of the next guided tour, and confirms her date of visit. Drills 'How much?', 'When?', 'What time?' in context. Phase 5 (15 min) — Date dictation: Teacher dictates 8 important dates (including February 3rd 2027 as her 'departure date'). Gabriela writes in American English format and reads back. Debrief: What felt easy? What needs more practice?
temas.homework: 1) Write your 'Dream Paris Itinerary' for 5 days in English — at least 2 activities per day with times and dates. Use American English date format. (25 min) | 2) Practice saying times aloud: set a timer for 5 minutes, look at a clock every 30 seconds and say the time in English. (5 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'Telling Time in English — American English' (~7 min). Note any time expression you hadn't heard before.

temas.tema: Where Are You From? — Countries, Nationalities & the World Around Gabriela
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'to be' for origin and nationality; prepositions of place for countries (from, in, at); question forms: 'Where are you from?', 'Where is she from?', 'What nationality are you?'; adjective-noun agreement awareness (no agreement in English vs. Portuguese); Vocabulary (12 words): country, nationality, capital, language, culture, Brazilian, French, American, European, citizen, passport, international; Target countries: Brazil, France, USA, UK, Italy, Spain, Japan, Australia; Expressions: 'I'm from Brazil', 'I'm Brazilian', 'She's French', 'Is he American?', 'Where in France are you from?'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Flag quiz' warm-up: Teacher shows 10 printed flag images. Gabriela names the country AND nationality adjective in English. Fun, fast, zero pressure. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces nationality adjectives using a printed world map. Focus on patterns: Brazil → Brazilian, France → French, USA → American, Italy → Italian. Highlights the difference from Portuguese (no gender agreement in English). Gabriela repeats and internalizes 10 key nationalities. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Celebrity Nationality Game': Teacher gives clues about a celebrity without saying their name. 'She is American. She is a famous actress. She was in Gossip Girl.' Gabriela guesses and responds: 'Is she Blake Lively? She's American!' Then Gabriela creates 3 clues for teacher to guess. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: At a Paris youth hostel, Gabriela meets international travelers. Teacher plays 3 different characters (one American, one Italian, one Japanese). Gabriela introduces herself and asks where they're from. Full dialogue: 5+ exchanges per character. Focuses on 'Where are you from? / I'm from… / What language do you speak?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Written production: Gabriela writes a short paragraph (6-8 sentences) introducing herself to a potential international pen pal: name, nationality, city, language, and why she's learning English.
temas.homework: 1) Write 10 sentences about 10 different celebrities or fictional characters: their name, nationality, country, and one fact. Use 'She is French. She is from Paris.' format. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio: 'Hi! I'm Gabriela. I'm Brazilian. I'm from São Paulo, Brazil. I speak Portuguese and I'm learning English because…' Speak naturally. (10 min) | 3) Look up the nationalities of the main cast of Gossip Girl (Blake Lively, Penn Badgley, Leighton Meester, Ed Westwick) — find out where they're REALLY from and write it in your notebook in English.

temas.tema: Can You Do That? — Abilities, Possibilities & Asking for Help
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verb 'can' — affirmative ('I can speak English'), negative ('I can't drive'), questions ('Can you help me?', 'Can I have…?', 'Can you repeat that?'); short answers: 'Yes, I can / No, I can't'; very basic 'could' for polite requests (receptive + formulaic production: 'Could you help me, please?'); Vocabulary (12 words): speak, understand, help, repeat, write, read, find, show, open, carry, call, need; Survival expressions: 'Can you help me?', 'I can't understand', 'Can you speak slower, please?', 'Can you repeat that?', 'I don't speak French, but I can speak English'; Travel survival focus throughout
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Can you…?' survey: Teacher asks Gabriela a rapid list of questions: 'Can you swim? Can you cook? Can you speak French? Can you play guitar?' She answers in full sentences. Fun, personal, fast. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces 'can' using a printed 'Skill Card' with Gabriela's name. 'Gabriela Pires can speak Portuguese. She can understand some English. She can't speak French.' Grammar is immediate and personal. Introduces can/can't in affirmative, negative, question with clear visual. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Survival Situations in Paris' activity: Teacher presents 5 printed mini-scenarios (lost in the metro, can't find the hotel, doesn't understand the menu). Gabriela must choose the right 'can' sentence to use in each situation. Discuss together. Focus on 'Can you help me?', 'Can you speak English?', 'Can you show me on the map?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Teacher plays a French passerby who speaks very fast. Gabriela must use survival English: 'Excuse me, can you help me? I can't find my hotel. Can you speak slower, please? Can you show me on the map?' Run the role-play 3 times with increasing complexity. Phase 5 (15 min) — Reflection + written output: Gabriela writes 8 'can/can't' sentences: 4 about her actual abilities, 4 survival phrases she would use in France. Read aloud with confidence.
temas.homework: 1) Write a list in your notebook: 5 things you CAN do in English, 5 things you CAN'T do yet in English, 3 survival phrases using 'can' for your Paris trip. (15 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio scenario: 'I am in Paris and I am lost. I see a person and I ask for help.' Use 'can' at least 5 times. (15 min) | 3) Watch the Friends episode 'The One Where Nobody's Ready' (S03E02) — listen for the word 'can' and count how many times you hear it. Note 3 sentences.

temas.tema: Where Is It? — Directions, Locations & Getting Around Paris on Foot
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: prepositions of place (in, on, at, next to, across from, between, in front of, behind, near, far from); imperatives for directions (turn left, go straight, take the first right, walk past); question forms: 'Where is the…?', 'How do I get to…?', 'Is it far?'; Vocabulary (12 words): street, corner, block, turn, left, right, straight, cross, map, station, exit, landmark; Paris-specific vocabulary: metro, arrondissement, boulevard, rue (as receptive — teacher explains it's French for 'street'); Expressions: 'Turn left at the corner', 'It's next to the museum', 'Walk straight for two blocks', 'You can't miss it'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Directional warm-up: Teacher gives rapid direction commands in the classroom. 'Turn left. Walk straight. Stop. Turn right.' Gabriela follows physically — gets her moving and makes language embodied. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher draws a simple hand-drawn map of a Paris neighborhood on paper — names streets, adds landmarks (Eiffel Tower, a café, a metro station, a hotel labeled 'Hotel Pires'). Introduces prepositions of place and direction verbs with arrows on the map. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Find Gabriela's Hotel' map task: Teacher places Gabriela at 'Point A' on the hand-drawn Paris map. Reads aloud a set of directions. Gabriela follows on the map with her finger and marks where she ends up. Then reverses: Gabriela picks a destination and gives teacher directions. 3 rounds each. Phase 4 (20 min) — Outdoor or hallway simulation (if possible): Teacher sets up 4 'landmarks' in the room or hallway using labels (museum, café, hotel, pharmacy). Gabriela gives directions from one to another as if speaking to a tourist. If outdoor not possible, use the drawn map role-play. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes directions from the Eiffel Tower to the Louvre on the hand-drawn Paris map — at least 6 direction sentences. Read aloud.
temas.homework: 1) Draw your own simple map of your neighborhood in São Paulo — label at least 6 places in English. Write 5 directions from your home to your school in English. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Excuse me, how do I get to the Eiffel Tower from the Louvre?' — give the full answer yourself using the map from class. (15 min) | 3) Google Maps exercise: search 'walking directions, Paris, France' for any two famous landmarks. Read the directions aloud in English, adapting to your vocabulary.

temas.tema: What Do You Look Like? — Describing People, Characters & First Impressions
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: adjectives for physical description — order with 'have/has' and 'to be' ('She has long brown hair', 'He is tall and slim'); third-person singular review in descriptive context; question forms: 'What does she look like?', 'What does he look like?'; basic comparison: 'She is taller than…' (receptive, with limited production); Vocabulary (14 words): tall, short, slim, curvy, young, old, long, short (hair), curly, straight, dark, blonde, blue/brown/green eyes, stylish; Character vocabulary: confident, shy, funny, kind, mean, smart, dramatic; Expressions: 'She looks like…', 'He reminds me of…', 'She has a very strong personality'; Context: Gossip Girl cast + Gabriela's own description
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Guess who?' warm-up: Teacher gives 3 physical descriptions of Gossip Girl characters. Gabriela guesses who it is. Then Gabriela gives a description, teacher guesses. Pure listening + speaking activation. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces descriptive adjectives using printed photos of Gossip Girl cast (Blair, Serena, Chuck, Dan). Creates a simple 2-column chart: LOOK (physical) and PERSONALITY. Models sentences: 'Blair has dark hair. She is elegant and confident. She looks like a queen.' Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Character Profile Cards': Gabriela receives 4 blank cards and fills in descriptions for 4 characters using target vocabulary. She then reads each card aloud while teacher shows the photo — class 'confirms' if description matches. Teacher corrects word order issues gently. Phase 4 (20 min) — Self-description + comparison: Gabriela describes herself in English (appearance + personality) — at least 5 sentences. Then compares herself to a character: 'I have dark hair like Blair. But I'm more relaxed than her. I think I'm more like Rory from Gilmore Girls because…' Phase 5 (15 min) — 'Describe and draw': Teacher describes a mystery fictional character (not from a show Gabriela knows). Gabriela draws the character based on the English description only. Compare and laugh. Reinforces listening comprehension of adjectives.
temas.homework: 1) Write a full physical + personality description of your favorite character from Gossip Girl OR Friends — at least 8 sentences. Use 'has' for physical features and 'is' for personality. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio describing yourself in English — appearance and personality. Pretend you're introducing yourself to a new friend in France. (10 min) | 3) Watch 5 minutes of any Gossip Girl episode. Write down 3 adjectives you hear used to describe a person.

temas.tema: Let's Eat! — Food, Menus & Ordering at a Restaurant (Paris Simulation)
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: countable vs. uncountable nouns (a sandwich / some water / a piece of cake); 'would like' for polite ordering ('I would like…', 'I'd like…'); basic quantity expressions (a cup of, a glass of, a piece of, some, any); question forms in restaurant context: 'Can I have…?', 'What do you recommend?', 'What's in the…?'; Vocabulary (14 words): menu, order, waiter, bill, appetizer, main course, dessert, vegetarian, allergic, recommend, delicious, awful, tip, receipt; Food vocabulary: bread, water, coffee, salad, chicken, pasta, cake, juice; Expressions: 'I'd like the…, please', 'Can I see the menu?', 'What do you recommend?', 'The check, please', 'It's delicious!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Food reaction warm-up: Teacher shows 10 food images. Gabriela reacts in English: 'I love this!', 'I don't like that', 'I've never tried this' (introduce as fixed phrase). Fast, fun, expressive. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates a printed 'Café de Paris Menu' in English with Gabriela Pires as the customer name on the mock reservation. Introduces 'would like' as the polite way to order — explains it's more sophisticated than 'I want' and more common in restaurants. Models full ordering dialogue. Phase 3 (25 min) — Full restaurant simulation: Room is set up as a Parisian café. Teacher plays the waiter (with optional prop — apron or notepad). Gabriela is seated, receives the menu, orders a starter, main, and dessert, asks for recommendations, and asks for the bill. Run 2 full simulations — first with scaffolding (menu visible), second without notes. Focus on natural flow and politeness markers. Phase 4 (15 min) — Difficult moments drill: Teacher introduces 3 common problems: 'I'm allergic to nuts', 'I don't eat meat, I'm vegetarian', 'I ordered the salad, not the pasta.' Gabriela practices each complaint/request in English. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes her ideal 3-course Paris meal with full sentences: 'For my starter, I'd like… For my main course, I'd like… For dessert, I'd like… because it looks delicious.'
temas.homework: 1) Write a full restaurant dialogue in your notebook — at least 10 lines total (5 as customer, 5 as waiter). Include: greeting, ordering starter, main, dessert, a question about the menu, and asking for the bill. (25 min) | 2) Record the dialogue as if you're at a Parisian café — say BOTH parts, changing your voice. (15 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'How to Order Food at a Restaurant in English' (~8 min). Write 3 new expressions you didn't know.

temas.tema: Shopping in the City — Buying Clothes, Asking Prices & Making Choices
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'How much does it cost?', 'How much is it?'; this/that/these/those in shopping context (recycled from Aula 3); 'too + adjective' ('It's too expensive', 'It's too small'); 'not + adjective + enough' (receptive); comparative adjectives introduction: 'bigger', 'cheaper', 'nicer'; Vocabulary (14 words): size, price, discount, try on, fit, exchange, receipt, expensive, cheap, brand, style, cash, credit card, sale; Clothing vocabulary: dress, skirt, blouse, jacket, sneakers, accessories; Expressions: 'Can I try this on?', 'Do you have this in a smaller size?', 'It's too expensive', 'I'll take it!', 'Do you accept credit cards?'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Shopping or not?' warm-up: Teacher describes 5 items with prices (€5 souvenir vs. €500 dress). Gabriela reacts: 'That's cheap!', 'That's too expensive!', 'I love it but it's not in my budget!' Gets opinion vocabulary flowing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates a printed 'Paris Boutique' mini-catalog with 6 items, prices in euros, and sizes. Introduces 'how much', 'too expensive', 'try on', comparative adjectives. Models: 'Excuse me, how much is this dress? It's €120. Oh, that's too expensive. Do you have something cheaper?' Phase 3 (25 min) — Shopping role-play simulation: Teacher plays shop assistant in a Parisian boutique. Gabriela browses the 'catalog', picks items, asks prices, tries to negotiate, decides to buy or not. Three complete shopping interactions: 1) souvenir shop, 2) clothing boutique, 3) bookstore. Each builds in complexity. Phase 4 (15 min) — Problem-solving in shops: Teacher introduces 3 scenarios — wrong size, broken item, no cash payment. Gabriela must navigate each in English. 'Excuse me, I think this is broken. Can I exchange it?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a postcard-style note to a friend: 'I went shopping in Paris today! I bought… I didn't buy… because it was too… The best deal was…' (using present simple — 'I buy / I find' as narrative for now)
temas.homework: 1) Write a shopping dialogue in your notebook — at least 12 lines. Include: asking for prices, trying things on, asking for a different size, and making a decision to buy or not. (25 min) | 2) Look at an online shop website (in English — ASOS, for example). Find 5 items you like and write their description, size, and price in your notebook using full English sentences. (15 min) | 3) Record a 60-second audio: 'Today I went shopping in Paris. Here's what happened…' Describe a fictional shopping trip using your new vocabulary.

temas.tema: What Were You Doing? — Past Continuous & Storytelling About Yesterday in SP
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple — introduction with 'to be' (was/were) as stepping stone; past continuous — 'was/were + verb-ing' ('I was watching TV', 'She was studying'); time expressions: at + time, when, while; contrast: past simple vs. past continuous at sentence level (receptive: 'I was watching TV when my phone rang'); Vocabulary (12 words): yesterday, last night, this morning, ago, earlier, suddenly, then, after that, before, during, interrupted, while; Action verbs review: watch, study, eat, walk, talk, listen, wait, sleep; Expressions: 'Last night I was watching…', 'Yesterday I was studying when…', 'What were you doing at 8pm?'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Where were you?' game: Teacher asks Gabriela about specific times yesterday: 'At 7am, were you sleeping or eating breakfast? At 3pm, were you in school?' Gabriela answers with was/were. Activates the new form in a fun interrogation format. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces past continuous using a printed 'Yesterday's Timeline' with Gabriela's name. 'At 8am, Gabriela was having breakfast. At 10am, she was studying at school. At 9pm, she was watching Gossip Girl.' Visual timeline makes the progressive action concept clear. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Alibi game': Teacher says there was a 'mystery event' (a famous celebrity posted something controversial) and asks Gabriela to prove she wasn't involved by describing exactly what she was doing at each hour yesterday. Gabriela must create a believable past continuous alibi — 6 sentences minimum. Teacher cross-examines. Phase 4 (20 min) — Gossip Girl storyline retelling: Teacher provides 4 printed screenshots (or descriptions) of a Gossip Girl episode scene. Gabriela narrates what each character was doing: 'Blair was walking into school when she saw Serena. Chuck was sitting in his limo. Dan was reading a book.' Production in third person + past continuous. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a 6-8 sentence description of her evening yesterday using past continuous. Read aloud and teacher gives form-focused feedback.
temas.homework: 1) Write a paragraph (8-10 sentences) about what you were doing at various times yesterday — include morning, afternoon, and evening. Use 'was/were + -ing' throughout. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Let me tell you about my Sunday. At 9am, I was…' Describe your whole day in past continuous. (15 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of Friends and write down 3 sentences where a character describes what they were doing ('I was just…', 'She was talking about…'). Note the exact sentences.

temas.tema: What Happened? — Past Simple for Telling Stories & Talking About the Weekend
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple — regular verbs (-ed: watched, studied, visited, walked, talked, listened); irregular verbs (high-frequency list: went, saw, had, ate, drank, met, bought, took, came, said, felt, got); negative: 'didn't + base verb'; questions: 'Did you…?', 'Where did you go?', 'What did you do?'; short answers: 'Yes, I did / No, I didn't'; Vocabulary (14 words): weekend, last week, last year, ago, visit, trip, museum, party, concert, festival, adventure, boring, amazing, unforgettable; Irregular verb list (receptive + active): go→went, see→saw, have→had, eat→ate, buy→bought, meet→met, take→took; Expressions: 'Last weekend I went to…', 'It was amazing!', 'I didn't expect that', 'Have you ever been to…?' (formulaic, receptive)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Did you…?' rapid fire: Teacher asks 10 yes/no questions about last weekend. 'Did you watch a series? Did you go to school? Did you eat pizza?' Gabriela answers with 'Yes, I did / No, I didn't.' Fast and activating. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates two printed 'Story Cards' — Gabriela's weekend and Blair Waldorf's imaginary weekend. Models: 'Last Saturday, Blair went to a fancy party. She wore a beautiful dress. She met a new boy. She didn't like him at first.' Introduces regular and irregular past forms in story context. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'My Weekend Story': Gabriela narrates her actual last weekend — teacher asks guiding questions and Gabriela produces the story. Teacher writes key verbs on paper as she speaks (correctly conjugated) for her to refer to. Goal: 10+ sentences in past simple. Teacher notes errors in past form without interrupting flow, addresses in Phase 5. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Story swap': Teacher narrates a fictional weekend story about Rory Gilmore in 8 sentences. Gabriela listens and answers 5 comprehension questions: 'Where did Rory go? Who did she meet? What did she eat?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Verb form focus: Teacher reviews the irregular verbs from today's story. Gabriela writes them in base + past form on a dedicated 'Verb Wall' page in her notebook. Drills 5 sentences with different irregular verbs.
temas.homework: 1) Write a 'Weekend Story' in your notebook — what you did last weekend, in at least 10 past simple sentences. Include: where you went, who you saw, what you ate, what you watched, how you felt. (25 min) | 2) Make a flashcard set (on paper or phone) with 15 irregular verbs: base form on one side, past simple on the other. Quiz yourself. (15 min) | 3) Record a 2-minute audio: 'Let me tell you about my last weekend in São Paulo.' Use at least 8 past simple sentences. No reading — just talk.

temas.tema: Have You Ever…? — Experiences, the Present Perfect & Talking About Life So Far
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present perfect — 'have/has + past participle' for life experiences; 'Have you ever…?', 'Yes, I have / No, I haven't'; 'never' with present perfect ('I've never been to France'); contrast with past simple (experience vs. specific time): 'I've been to Rio' vs. 'I went to Rio last year' (awareness level); Vocabulary (12 words): experience, ever, never, already, yet, before, once, twice, amazing, unforgettable, regret, bucket list; Past participles focus: been, seen, eaten, tried, visited, met, traveled, watched, read, heard; Expressions: 'Have you ever been to…?', 'I've never tried…', 'I've always wanted to…', 'It was my first time!', 'I've seen it twice!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Have you ever?' bingo: Teacher gives Gabriela a printed bingo card with 9 experiences ('eaten escargot', 'been to another country', 'met a celebrity', 'tried a new sport', etc.). Teacher asks questions: 'Have you ever…?' Gabriela marks the ones that apply and answers in full sentences. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces present perfect using a printed 'Gabriela's Life Experiences' visual. 'You have studied English. You have watched Gossip Girl. You have never been to France — but you're going in 2027!' Creates emotional hook. Explains form clearly: have + past participle. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Bucket list vs. Done it' activity: Gabriela creates two columns in her notebook — 'I've already done this' and 'I've never done this but I want to.' She writes 6 items per column using present perfect. Shares with teacher. Teacher prompts follow-up questions: 'Have you ever tried sushi? How many times?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Gabriela meets a French student at a cultural exchange event. Teacher plays the student: 'Have you ever been to France? Have you ever eaten a croissant? Have you ever watched a French film?' Gabriela answers and asks back. 8-exchange dialogue. Focus on natural rhythm of question + answer. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes her 'English Experience List' — 8 present perfect sentences: 5 things she has done, 3 things she has never done but wants to. Read aloud with pronunciation focus.
temas.homework: 1) Write 10 'Have you ever' questions you would ask a new French friend — then answer all 10 yourself. Use present perfect throughout. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Things I've done and things I've never done.' Include at least 6 present perfect sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch the 'The One Where Everyone Finds Out' episode of Friends (S05E14). Count how many times you hear 'have' + past participle (e.g., 'I've told you', 'I haven't seen'). Write 3 examples.

temas.tema: Tell Me About Your Plans! — Future with 'Going To' & Talking About the Paris Trip
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: future with 'going to' — affirmative ('I'm going to visit Paris'), negative ('I'm not going to miss the Eiffel Tower'), questions ('What are you going to do in France?'); present continuous for future arrangements (receptive awareness: 'I'm flying to Paris on February 3rd'); time expressions: next year, in 2027, this summer, soon, in + month; Vocabulary (12 words): plan, trip, flight, hotel, book, visit, sightseeing, museum, tour, passport, luggage, itinerary; Paris-specific vocabulary: Eiffel Tower, the Louvre, Notre-Dame, Montmartre, croissant, boulangerie, arrondissement; Expressions: 'I'm going to…', 'We're planning to…', 'I can't wait to…', 'It's going to be amazing!', 'I'm so excited about…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Dream trip' activation: Teacher asks in English: 'Close your eyes. You're in Paris. What do you see? What do you do first?' Gabriela responds freely. Teacher notes vocabulary she doesn't know and teaches those 3-4 words immediately — just-in-time vocabulary learning. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates 'Gabriela Pires — Paris Trip 2027' planned itinerary and introduces 'going to' in context. 'On February 3rd, Gabriela is going to fly from São Paulo to Paris. On February 4th, she's going to visit the Louvre. She's not going to waste a single day!' Every example uses HER name and HER trip. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'Plan your Paris week': Gabriela creates her own 5-day Paris itinerary in English using 'going to' — at least 2 activities per day. Shares with teacher as an oral presentation: 'On Monday, I'm going to visit Montmartre in the morning. In the afternoon, I'm going to eat at a French café.' Teacher asks follow-up questions to extend production. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Gabriela calls a friend (teacher) to tell them about her Paris plans. 'Guess what? I'm going to go to Paris next year! I'm going to stay for 5 days. I'm going to visit…' Friend asks questions, expresses jealousy, asks for recommendations. Natural conversation flow. Phase 5 (10 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes 8 'going to' sentences about her Paris trip — 6 positive, 2 negative ('I'm not going to forget my camera!'). Read aloud.
temas.homework: 1) Write your complete Paris Trip Plan in English — 7 days, at least 2 activities per day with full 'going to' sentences. Include: transport, accommodation, food, sightseeing, shopping. (30 min) | 2) Record a 2-minute audio: 'Let me tell you about my plans for Paris 2027!' Use 'going to' at least 10 times. Speak with excitement — this is YOUR trip! (15 min) | 3) Watch the trailer of Notting Hill on YouTube (in English) and write 3 sentences about what the characters are going to do, based on what you see.

temas.tema: Emergency Mode — Health Vocabulary, Asking for Help & Staying Safe Abroad
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'to feel' + adjective for health ('I feel sick', 'I feel dizzy'); 'have' for symptoms ('I have a headache', 'I have a fever'); 'need' + noun/infinitive ('I need a doctor', 'I need to sit down'); modal 'should' introduction (formulaic: 'You should see a doctor', 'You should rest'); imperatives for emergency ('Call an ambulance!', 'Don't move!'); Vocabulary (14 words): sick, headache, fever, dizzy, hurt, pain, allergic, doctor, hospital, pharmacy, emergency, medicine, insurance, lost; Expressions: 'I don't feel well', 'I need help', 'I'm allergic to…', 'Please call a doctor', 'Where's the nearest pharmacy?', 'I have travel insurance'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'How do you feel?' charades: Teacher mimes symptoms (dizzy, sick, in pain, tired, hungry). Gabriela guesses in English: 'You feel sick! You have a headache!' Then reverses. Embodied learning — makes health vocabulary physical and memorable. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces health vocabulary through a printed 'Emergency Card' titled 'Gabriela Pires — Emergency Information' with key sentences she'd need in France. Models each sentence aloud with clear pronunciation. Explains that this is one of the most important vocabulary sets for solo travel. Phase 3 (25 min) — Emergency simulation scenarios: 3 printed scenario cards. Scenario 1: Gabriela feels sick in a Parisian café — talks to the waiter. Scenario 2: She needs to find a pharmacy — asks a passerby for directions + explains symptoms. Scenario 3: She has a headache and goes to a pharmacy to buy medicine — speaks to the pharmacist. Each role-play runs for 4-5 minutes with teacher playing the other person. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'What should you do?' discussion: Teacher presents 5 situations (lost phone, fever, allergic reaction, missed flight, stolen wallet). Gabriela responds: 'I should call the embassy', 'I should go to the hospital', 'I should contact my travel insurance.' Introduces 'should' in a purposeful, high-stakes context. Phase 5 (15 min) — Emergency card creation: Gabriela creates her own 'Emergency Card' in English — her name, nationality, any allergies, emergency contact, insurance number (fictional). She writes it and reads it aloud.
temas.homework: 1) Create your personal 'Travel Emergency Card' in English in your notebook — include: name, nationality, blood type, allergies, emergency phrases, and 5 sentences for different emergency situations. (25 min) | 2) Record a 90-second role-play audio: 'I'm in a pharmacy in Paris. I don't feel well. Let me explain my symptoms.' Play both roles. (15 min) | 3) Research (in English): 'What to do if you get sick abroad?' Find 3 tips online and write them in your notebook in your own words.

temas.tema: Let's Compare! — Comparative & Superlative Adjectives Through Culture & Travel
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: comparative adjectives — short adjectives + -er ('bigger', 'cheaper', 'nicer', 'older'); 'more + long adjective' ('more expensive', 'more beautiful', 'more interesting'); 'than' in comparisons ('Paris is more romantic than São Paulo'); superlative adjectives — 'the + -est' ('the biggest', 'the most famous', 'the best', 'the worst'); irregular: good→better→best, bad→worse→worst; Vocabulary (12 words): famous, popular, crowded, expensive, modern, ancient, romantic, exciting, dangerous, peaceful, cosmopolitan, iconic; Expressions: 'In my opinion…', 'I think… is better than…', 'The most beautiful city I know is…', 'She's the best character in the show'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Which is better?' rapid reactions: Teacher shows pairs of images (São Paulo vs. Paris; Blair vs. Serena; Friends vs. Gossip Girl; Brazilian food vs. French food). Gabriela reacts immediately: 'Paris is more beautiful than São Paulo' / 'Blair is more dramatic than Serena.' Gets comparatives flowing without grammar explanation first. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces comparative and superlative structure using a printed chart with Gabriela's own comparisons from Phase 1. Adds the grammar labels AFTER she's already using the forms. Focuses on short vs. long adjective rule and irregular forms. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'São Paulo vs. Paris vs. New York' comparison activity: Teacher provides a printed fact sheet with 3 cities (population, famous landmarks, food scene, weather, cost of living). Gabriela writes 8 comparison sentences using comparatives and superlatives. Reads aloud and debates with teacher. Phase 4 (20 min) — Opinion debate: Teacher asks comparative opinion questions: 'Is Rory Gilmore a better character than Blair Waldorf? Is Gossip Girl more interesting than Friends? Is Paris more romantic than London?' Gabriela must defend opinions with complete sentences using comparatives. Teacher pushes back to generate more production. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a 'My Favorites Comparison List' — 6 comparative sentences and 3 superlative sentences about things she loves. Example: 'Friends is funnier than Gossip Girl, but Gossip Girl is more dramatic. The best character in Friends is…'
temas.homework: 1) Write 10 comparison sentences about cities, shows, food, or celebrities you know — use 5 comparatives and 5 superlatives. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'São Paulo vs. Paris — a comparison.' Compare the two cities using at least 6 comparative and superlative sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of any travel vlog on YouTube about Paris (in English). Write down 3 comparison sentences you hear (e.g., 'Paris is more…', 'The best…').

temas.tema: Making Plans & Inviting People — Social English for International Friendships
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'Would you like to…?' for invitations; 'Let's + verb' for suggestions ('Let's go to the museum!'); 'What about + noun/-ing?' and 'How about + noun/-ing?' for alternatives; accepting: 'That sounds great!', 'I'd love to!'; declining politely: 'I'd love to but…', 'Maybe next time', 'I can't, I have to…'; 'have to' introduction (obligation): 'I have to be back by 10'; Vocabulary (12 words): invitation, suggestion, plan, meet, hang out, free, available, busy, cancel, confirm, venue, appointment; Expressions: 'Are you free this weekend?', 'Would you like to grab a coffee?', 'What are you doing tonight?', 'Let's meet at the Eiffel Tower at noon!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Accept or decline?' game: Teacher makes rapid invitations. 'Would you like to eat at McDonald's?' / 'Would you like to visit a museum at 6am?' / 'Would you like to get coffee?' Gabriela accepts or declines with full natural sentences. Immediate and energizing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces invitation and suggestion structures using a printed 'Weekend in Paris' scenario. Models: 'Would you like to visit the Louvre tomorrow? What about lunch at a café near the Seine? Let's take a boat tour!' Introduces accepting/declining responses in pairs. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'Paris social weekend' role-play: Gabriela plays herself as a traveler in Paris. Teacher plays 3 different characters: a French student she met at the hostel, a Brazilian traveler she bumped into, and a local guide. Each character extends an invitation or suggestion. Gabriela must respond appropriately — accept, decline with reason, counter-suggest. 3 full mini-dialogues. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Making plans by message': Teacher gives Gabriela a printed 'fake WhatsApp exchange' in English between two travelers making plans. Gabriela reads aloud, then creates her own 8-line message exchange with a fictional French friend making plans for the next day in Paris. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a social invitation email (4-6 sentences) to a fictional French classmate: 'Hey! Would you like to…? We could… What about…? Let me know if you're free!' Uses all target structures.
temas.homework: 1) Write 3 different invitation scenarios in your notebook: one accepted, one declined politely, one with a counter-suggestion. Each scenario should have at least 6 exchanges. (25 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'I'm going to invite my new French friend to spend the day with me in Paris.' Plan the full invitation conversation and say it aloud. (15 min) | 3) Watch a Friends episode focused on social plans (e.g., 'The One with the Prom Video' S02E14). Note 3 expressions characters use to make or cancel plans.

temas.tema: Block 1 Review & Progress Check — Gabriela's Mid-Journey Milestone Celebration
temas.focoLinguistico: Review of ALL key structures from Aulas 1-19: to be (present + past), present simple, past simple, past continuous, present perfect, going to future, can/can't, comparatives/superlatives, modals (should, would like, have to), prepositions, articles; Vocabulary review: 120+ words introduced across 19 aulas — recycled through communicative tasks; Self-assessment focus: What can Gabriela do now that she couldn't do in Aula 1? What still needs work? Progress measurement against Paris 2027 goals; Expressions recycled: all key expressions from restaurant, directions, shopping, health, social plans
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Remember this?' warm-up quiz: Teacher reads out 10 sentences with gaps from across the 19 aulas. Gabriela fills in from memory. Not graded — just diagnostic. Fun, light, confidence-building. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Paris Survival Mega Role-Play': A 20-minute immersive simulation that chains together 5 mini-scenarios from the block: arriving at the hotel (greetings, to be, spelling name) → asking for directions to a restaurant → ordering a 3-course meal → shopping for a souvenir → asking for help when she feels unwell. Teacher plays all characters. Gabriela stays in English throughout. Teacher notes what flows naturally and what breaks down. This is the primary assessment tool. Phase 3 (15 min) — Progress comparison: Teacher plays back (or reads aloud) Gabriela's Aula 1 baseline audio description. Then Gabriela records a NEW 90-second self-introduction in English. Together, they compare: vocabulary range, sentence complexity, confidence, L1 switches. Celebrate concrete progress. Phase 4 (20 min) — 'Vocabulary Victory Lap': Gabriela has 10 minutes to create a personal 'vocabulary map' on paper — all the topic areas covered (greetings, food, directions, health, travel) with her 5 favorite words per area. Then reads her map aloud to teacher. This is her personalized vocabulary reference for Block 2. Phase 5 (15 min) — Goal reset + Block 2 preview: Teacher and Gabriela review the Victory Wall from Aula 1. What has she already achieved? Teacher previews Block 2 themes (storytelling, opinions, media, intercâmbio language, more Paris prep). Gabriela writes one personal goal for Block 2 in English: 'In the next 16 lessons, I want to…' Ends with a celebration moment — Gabriela's choice (favorite scene from a series, a fun English game).
temas.homework: 1) Re-record your Aula 1 baseline audio — 2 full minutes this time. Talk about: who you are, your life in São Paulo, your hobbies, your plans for Paris, things you've done, what you're going to do. Compare it to your original recording and write 3 differences you notice. (25 min) | 2) Create your 'Block 1 Vocabulary Book' — go through your notebook from Aulas 1-19 and write all new words you learned organized by theme on a new clean page. Aim for 80+ words. (30 min) | 3) Choose your favorite English series moment from the past weeks and watch it again — this time WITHOUT any subtitles. Write what you understood (even if partial). Celebrate how far you've come!

temas.tema: Will You or Won't You? — Future with 'Will' for Predictions, Promises & Spontaneous Decisions About Life After High School
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: future with 'will' — affirmative ('I will study abroad'), negative ('I won't stay home'), yes/no questions ('Will you travel in 2027?'), short answers ('Yes, I will / No, I won't'); contrasting 'will' vs. 'going to' — spontaneous vs. planned decisions; vocabulary: probably, maybe, definitely, I think, I hope, I'm sure, I doubt, I promise; topic vocab: exchange program, gap year, scholarship, abroad, apply, enroll, deadline, semester; expressions: 'I'll call you back', 'I'll take it!', 'I think it will be amazing', 'I promise I'll practice every day'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows Gabriela a timeline of her life from now to 2027 (school graduation → Paris trip → possible exchange) and asks 'What do you think WILL happen?' She makes 5 spontaneous predictions using 'will'. Presentation (15 min): Contrast 'going to' (from Aula 16) vs. 'will' using real scenarios — 'I'm going to visit the Eiffel Tower' (planned) vs. 'I think the weather will be cold in February' (prediction). PPT/whiteboard with 3 categories: predictions, promises, spontaneous decisions. Practice (20 min): Drilling round — teacher says a situation card, Gabriela responds with 'will'. Ex: 'Your phone is ringing!' → 'I'll answer it!'; 'Someone is cold in Paris' → 'I'll get a jacket!'. Then gap-fill exercise: complete 10 sentences about Gabriela's Paris trip using will/won't. Production (20 min): Role-play — Gabriela is on the phone with her future host family in France. Teacher plays the French host mom asking about Gabriela's plans, habits, and what she will/won't do during the stay. Gabriela must use at least 8 'will' structures naturally across the conversation. Debrief (10 min): Discuss the difference will/going to — spot-check 3 sentences the student produced and correct together.
temas.homework: Write a short paragraph (8–10 sentences) titled 'My 2027 Predictions' — what Gabriela thinks will and won't happen during the Paris trip. Use: will, won't, probably, maybe, I think, I hope. Also, record a 90-second audio: 'A message to my future host family' — introduce yourself and say 3 things you will do and 2 things you won't do during the stay. Send the recording. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

temas.tema: At the Airport — Check-In, Security, Gates & Surviving Gabriela's First International Flight
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: review of modal verbs 'can/can't' (Aula 8) + new: 'may I…?' and 'could you…?' for polite airport requests; imperative for instructions ('Please remove your shoes', 'Place your bag on the belt'); vocabulary: passport, boarding pass, check-in counter, carry-on luggage, checked baggage, gate, departure, arrival, terminal, security checkpoint, customs, declaration form, flight attendant, overhead bin, seat belt, window seat, aisle seat, delay, on time; expressions: 'May I see your passport?', 'Could you open your bag, please?', 'Where is gate B12?', 'Is this seat taken?', 'My flight is delayed', 'How long is the layover?', 'I have nothing to declare'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher lays out airport props on the desk — printed boarding pass with Gabriela Pires's name, a 'passport', a luggage tag — and asks 'Where are you right now? What do you need to do?' Eliciting vocabulary. Presentation (15 min): Airport journey map on the board — each step labeled: arrive → check in → drop luggage → security → find gate → board. Introduce vocabulary at each stage with pronunciation drilling. Focus on polite request forms: 'May I…?' vs. 'Could you…?' Practice (20 min): Listening simulation — teacher reads 3 airport announcements aloud (gate change, boarding call, delay announcement) and Gabriela must answer comprehension questions. Then, written practice: fill in a simulated check-in form as Gabriela Pires (name, passport number, destination: Paris CDG, seat preference). Production (25 min): Full airport simulation role-play in 3 rounds — (1) check-in desk: teacher as agent, Gabriela checks in her bag and gets boarding pass; (2) security checkpoint: answer questions about items in her bag; (3) gate interaction: ask a fellow traveler about the gate and chat about the destination. Debrief: identify 2 moments where she hesitated and rehearse those lines again.
temas.homework: Listen to 2 real airport announcement recordings on YouTube (search 'airport announcements English') and write down 5 sentences you understood. Also, practice saying the full check-in dialogue alone at home and record yourself playing BOTH roles — agent and passenger Gabriela Pires. Recording should be 2–3 minutes. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

temas.tema: On the Plane & Arriving in Paris — Talking to Flight Attendants, Customs & Your First Moments in France
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'would like' for polite requests on the plane (review from Aula 11) + 'could you' + infinitive; present continuous for current actions ('We are landing in 20 minutes', 'The captain is speaking now'); vocabulary: turbulence, life jacket, emergency exit, tray table, recline, headphones, landing, customs officer, immigration, visa, purpose of visit, length of stay, I'm here as a tourist, accommodation; expressions: 'Could you bring me a blanket, please?', 'Excuse me, is there a vegetarian option?', 'I'm here for tourism', 'I'll be staying for 10 days', 'I'm staying at a hotel in Paris', 'Do I need to fill in a form?'; cultural note: difference between Schengen entry and non-EU passport protocols
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Recap of airport vocabulary from Aula 22 — quick flashcard quiz: teacher holds up a card, Gabriela says the word + a sentence. 5 words reviewed. Presentation (15 min): New scenario — inside the plane and at the immigration counter. Teacher presents vocabulary with a visual 'flight map' (boarding → takeoff → in-flight → landing → customs → exit). Pronunciation focus: 'turbulence', 'accommodation', 'immigration'. Practice (20 min): Dialogue completion — Gabriela receives a printed dialogue between a passenger and flight attendant with 6 blanks; she fills in using the target vocabulary, then reads it aloud with expression. Second activity: teacher reads a customs officer script and Gabriela answers as herself (purpose of visit, length of stay, where she's staying). Production (25 min): Two-part role-play. Part 1 — Gabriela is on the plane and needs to: ask for water, report her tray table is broken, ask what time they land. Part 2 — immigration at CDG airport: customs officer (teacher) asks all standard questions, Gabriela answers naturally using real information about her trip. Coach pacing and confidence. Debrief: highlight 2 strong answers and 1 area to improve.
temas.homework: Write a journal entry (8–10 sentences) as Gabriela on her first night in Paris — 'Today I arrived in Paris. My flight was…' Use: past simple + present perfect ('I have finally arrived!'). Also, find one YouTube video of a real immigration interview in English and note 3 questions the officer asked. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

temas.tema: Milestone Check-In — Gabriela at the Halfway Point: What She Knows, What She Can Do & Where She's Going
temas.focoLinguistico: Review + integration of ALL major structures from Aulas 1–23: to be (present/past), present simple, past simple, present perfect, present continuous, future (going to + will), modals (can, could, would, may), comparatives/superlatives, prepositions, demonstratives, articles; vocabulary review across all topics: personal info, travel, health, food, shopping, directions, emotions, invitations, airport, Paris; self-assessment language: 'I can…', 'I'm still working on…', 'I need more practice with…', 'I feel confident when…', 'I get confused when…'; expressions for reflecting: 'Looking back…', 'I've improved in…', 'My goal is to…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher reads aloud 10 sentences from previous aulas (one from each major topic) — Gabriela rates her confidence on each from 1–5. This creates a live heat map of strengths and gaps. Presentation/Review (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Gabriela's Journey' visual timeline (Aulas 1–23) — each grammar point marked. Together they identify: what was easy, what was hard, what needs recycling. Practice (25 min): Integrated assessment — 3 tasks: (1) Oral task: Gabriela introduces herself fully in English — name, city, school, hobbies, future plans, Paris trip — without stopping for 90 seconds; (2) Grammar spot-check: 10 written sentences with targeted errors for her to correct; (3) Vocabulary recall: 15 flashcards from aulas 1–23, Gabriela defines or uses in a sentence. Production (20 min): Gabriela records a 'Progress Report Video' (teacher films on phone or she does at home): she speaks directly to camera in English for 2 minutes — what she has learned, what she can do now that she couldn't do in Aula 1, and what she wants to achieve by Aula 48. Debrief (10 min): Celebrate concrete wins; set 3 personal targets for Block 3.
temas.homework: Watch your own recording from Aula 1 (if available) and compare it to today's recording. Write 5 sentences in English: 'In Aula 1, I couldn't… but now I can…'. Also, review your personal vocabulary notebook and mark 10 words you feel 100% confident with and 5 you want to practice more. Estimated time: 25–30 minutes.

temas.tema: Let's Talk About Series! — Describing TV Shows, Giving Opinions & Using Gossip Girl and Friends as Grammar Tools
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for habitual actions + adverbs of frequency (review) + NEW: relative clauses with 'who' and 'that' for describing characters — 'Blair is a character who is very manipulative', 'Friends is a show that takes place in New York'; opinion language: 'I think…', 'In my opinion…', 'I believe…', 'I feel like…', 'What I love about it is…'; vocabulary: episode, season, plot, character, storyline, cliffhanger, binge-watch, streaming, drama, romantic subplot, villain, protagonist, supporting character, dialogue, scene, spin-off; expressions: 'Have you seen…?', 'You should watch it!', 'It's about…', 'The main character is…', 'It gets better after season 2', 'I've been watching it non-stop'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows stills from Gossip Girl, Friends, and Gilmore Girls (printed or shown on screen) and asks 'Tell me everything you know about this show — IN ENGLISH.' Gabriela free-talks for 2 minutes while teacher notes vocabulary gaps to address. Presentation (15 min): Introduce relative clauses with 'who' and 'that' using real show descriptions. Build on board: 'Gossip Girl is a show THAT follows the lives of wealthy teenagers in New York. Blair Waldorf is a character WHO is ambitious and fashionable.' Gabriela underlines the relative pronoun and the clause it introduces. Practice (20 min): Guided writing + speaking — Gabriela writes 6 sentences describing characters from her favorite shows using 'who' and 'that'. Then oral drill: teacher says a character name, Gabriela describes them with a relative clause in under 5 seconds. Round 2: opinion marathon — teacher asks 'What do you think of Serena van der Woodsen?' and Gabriela must answer with a full opinion sentence using target structures. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is recommending Gossip Girl to a new French friend she met in Paris who doesn't know the show. She must: describe the show (what it's about), introduce 3 characters using relative clauses, give her opinion, and convince her friend to watch it. Teacher plays the skeptical French friend. Debrief: review 3 relative clauses she produced and fine-tune.
temas.homework: Choose ONE episode of Friends or Gossip Girl (any episode in English with subtitles). Watch 10 minutes. Write a mini-review (6–8 sentences): describe the plot, one character using a relative clause ('who' or 'that'), and give your opinion. Also, make a list of 5 new English words you heard in the episode. Estimated time: 45–55 minutes.

temas.tema: Getting Around Paris — Metro, Maps, Bus & Asking for Directions Like a Local
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: imperative for giving directions (review from Aula 9) + NEW: 'You need to…', 'You should…', 'You have to…' for advice and instructions in navigation contexts; prepositions of movement: through, past, along, across, toward, away from; vocabulary: metro line, platform, ticket machine, single ticket, monthly pass, timetable, bus stop, transfer, interchange, exit, north/south/east/west, crosswalk, roundabout, landmark, map, neighborhood — Marais, Montmartre, Saint-Germain; expressions: 'How do I get to…?', 'Which line do I take?', 'Where do I change trains?', 'Is it far from here?', 'It's about a 10-minute walk', 'Get off at…', 'Take line 4 toward…', 'You can't miss it'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed Paris metro map on the table and asks Gabriela to find 5 famous landmarks she already knows (Eiffel Tower, Louvre, Notre-Dame, etc.) — in English only. Presentation (15 min): Introduce prepositions of movement with physical demonstration in the classroom — teacher walks 'through' the door, 'past' the desk, 'across' the room, narrating each move. Then: 'You should take line 1. You need to get off at Châtelet. Walk past the fountain and turn left.' Practice (20 min): Map drill — teacher gives Gabriela a 'You are here' starting point on the Paris map and a destination. She must give directions using imperatives + 'you need to / you should'. Two rounds with different starting points. Then: gap-fill dialogue between a tourist and a Parisian — complete 8 lines with direction vocabulary. Production (25 min): Full role-play simulation. Teacher sets up two chairs as 'metro seats'. Gabriela has just arrived at Gare du Nord and needs to get to the Eiffel Tower.
