Teacher plays: (1) a metro ticket agent — she must buy the right ticket; (2) a local Parisian she stops on the street for directions. Gabriela navigates both interactions without switching to Portuguese. Debrief: map any confusion about prepositions of movement.
temas.homework: Using Google Maps (or a printed Paris map), plan a route from the Eiffel Tower to Montmartre. Write the directions in English (6–8 steps) using imperatives and 'you need to / you should'. Then, record yourself giving those directions aloud as if you're explaining to a friend — aim for 60–90 seconds. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

temas.tema: Talking About Feelings & Emotions — How Gabriela Feels About School, Travel & Life in a New Place
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'feel + adjective' (review from Aula 17) + NEW: 'I feel like + noun/gerund' ('I feel like crying', 'I feel like a stranger'); cause and effect with 'because', 'so', 'that's why'; linking words: although, however, but, on the other hand; vocabulary: excited, nervous, homesick, overwhelmed, relieved, proud, embarrassed, frustrated, confident, lonely, curious, anxious, grateful, nostalgic, motivated; expressions: 'I'm really excited about…', 'To be honest, I feel nervous when…', 'That makes me feel…', 'I feel more confident now', 'I was overwhelmed at first, but…', 'I miss home, but I love being here'; cultural vocabulary: culture shock, adjustment period, comfort zone, homesickness
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 10 'emotion cards' face-down on the table (each with an adjective). Gabriela picks one, acts it out without words — teacher guesses. Then switch. Vocabulary activated playfully. Presentation (15 min): Introduce the new emotion vocabulary in context: 'Imagine you're in Paris on day 1. You're at the hotel alone. How do you feel? Why?' Build sentences on the board: 'I feel overwhelmed because everything is in French. However, I'm excited because it's my dream.' Introduce linking words as connectors between emotions. Practice (20 min): Speaking chain — teacher gives a scenario card ('Your flight was delayed 4 hours'), Gabriela makes 3 sentences: how she feels + why + what she will do. 5 scenarios total. Written task: Gabriela fills in a 'Feelings Diary' — 5 situations from her life (school exam, talking in English, watching a new series) matched with 3 emotion words each + a sentence explaining why. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela has been in Paris for 3 days and video-calls her mom in São Paulo. Teacher plays the mom asking: 'How are you feeling? Are you homesick? Is it hard?' Gabriela must respond with full emotion sentences using linking words — no single-word answers. Debrief: celebrate emotional vocabulary range; highlight 2 especially strong sentences.
temas.homework: Write a 'Paris Day 3 Diary Entry' (10–12 sentences) in English — describe how you feel about being in Paris, what makes you nervous and what makes you excited, using emotion vocabulary and linking words (because, although, however). Also, look up one English song that talks about emotions and write down 3 lyrics that include an emotion word. Estimated time: 35–40 minutes.

temas.tema: At the Hotel — Checking In, Asking for Help & Handling Room Problems Like a Confident Guest
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'There is / There are' (affirmative, negative, questions) for describing room amenities; 'I'd like to…' + 'Could you…?' for polite requests; reporting a problem: 'The [noun] isn't working', 'There's no…', 'I think there's a problem with…'; vocabulary: reception desk, receptionist, reservation, room key, check-in/check-out, floor, elevator, single/double room, suite, Wi-Fi password, room service, minibar, air conditioning, heating, shower, towels, pillow, wake-up call, lobby, valet; expressions: 'I have a reservation under the name Pires', 'Could I have a room with a view?', 'The air conditioning isn't working', 'Could you send someone to fix it?', 'What time is breakfast?', 'Is there a safe in the room?', 'Can I have a late check-out?'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 8 small hotel-related objects or picture cards on the desk (towel image, key card, Wi-Fi sign, etc.) and Gabriela names each one and makes a sentence: 'There is a key card on the desk.' Quick-fire round — 60 seconds. Presentation (15 min): Hotel check-in dialogue on the board — full model conversation between Gabriela Pires and a Paris hotel receptionist. Teacher reads both roles with exaggerated politeness to show register. Highlight: 'There is/are', polite requests, problem-reporting structure. Practice (20 min): Gabriela rewrites 5 'rude' sentences into polite hotel English: 'Give me a towel' → 'Could you bring me a towel, please?' Then, gap-fill hotel dialogue — she completes a check-in script for a 4-star Paris hotel, including her name, reservation details, room preference, and one complaint. Production (25 min): Three-round hotel simulation — Teacher as hotel receptionist: Round 1: smooth check-in (Gabriela has a reservation, everything works); Round 2: problem scenario — the room smells, the Wi-Fi doesn't work, there are no towels; Round 3: check-out — pay the bill, ask for a receipt, request a taxi to the airport. Debrief: note 3 polite phrases that sounded natural and 1 area to polish.
temas.homework: Write 2 short emails (5–6 sentences each): Email 1 — before the trip, confirming your hotel reservation as Gabriela Pires. Email 2 — a complaint to the hotel after your stay about a problem with your room. Use 'There was no…', 'The [noun] wasn't working', 'I would like to…'. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

temas.tema: Eating Out in Paris — Full Restaurant Experience: Menus, Orders, Allergies & Paying the Bill
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'would like' vs. 'want' in restaurant register (review + expansion); 'Could I have…?' / 'I'll have…' for ordering; quantifiers with food: some, a piece of, a glass of, a bottle of, a slice of (review countable/uncountable from Aula 11) + NEW: too much / too many / not enough; vocabulary: appetizer, main course, dessert, side dish, the specials, sparkling/still water, medium/well-done/rare (steak), vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, allergy, bill, tip, receipt, service charge, split the bill; expressions: 'I'm allergic to…', 'Does this contain…?', 'Could we have the menu, please?', 'I'll have the salmon, please', 'Could I get this without onions?', 'Can we get the check?', 'Is service included?', 'It was delicious, thank you!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher hands Gabriela a printed French restaurant menu (in English) — she has 2 minutes to choose a starter, main, and dessert and tell the teacher her choices with reasons: 'I'd like the French onion soup because…' Presentation (15 min): Expand on Aula 11's restaurant vocab with the full dining experience — from arrival to payment. Introduce 'too much / too many / not enough' with food context: 'There's too much salt', 'There aren't enough vegetables', 'I don't have enough money for the dessert'. Practice (20 min): Roleplay script reading — teacher and Gabriela do a waiter/customer dialogue from a printed script, switching roles. Focus on intonation and politeness. Then: allergy scenario drill — teacher announces 'You're lactose intolerant!' and reads 5 menu items; Gabriela must ask about each one: 'Does this contain dairy?' Production (25 min): Full restaurant simulation — teacher sets the scene (2 chairs, printed menu, pretend bill). Gabriela is dining with a French classmate (teacher plays both the friend and the waiter). She must: greet the waiter, order a full meal, handle an issue (wrong dish brought), ask about an allergy, and pay the bill. Debrief: role-play 'what went well' + replay 1 awkward moment more naturally.
temas.homework: Find a real Paris restaurant menu online (many have English versions) and write your ideal 3-course meal order as a complete dialogue — from greeting the waiter to paying the bill (10–12 lines). Record yourself speaking the customer's lines aloud. Also, write 3 sentences using 'too much/too many/not enough' about food from your day. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

temas.tema: Visiting Museums & Monuments — The Louvre, Versailles & Talking About Art, History & Beauty in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple for narrating visits + present simple for general facts about places ('The Louvre is the world's largest museum', 'Napoleon built Versailles'); NEW: passive voice — simple introduction: 'The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci', 'The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889', 'It was designed by Gustave Eiffel'; vocabulary: painting, sculpture, gallery, exhibition, guided tour, audio guide, admission fee, masterpiece, artifact, portrait, landscape, architecture, medieval, Renaissance, Impressionism; expressions: 'It was absolutely breathtaking', 'I was amazed by…', 'Did you know that…?', 'The most impressive part was…', 'I can't believe how old it is!', 'It took my breath away'; cultural note: major Paris landmarks + brief context for each
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 images of Paris landmarks/artworks (Mona Lisa, Eiffel Tower, Versailles, Notre-Dame, Arc de Triomphe) and asks 'What do you know about this? Tell me ONE thing — in English!' Gabriela free-talks, teacher notes gaps. Presentation (15 min): Introduce the passive voice with the 5 images: 'The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889. It was designed by Gustave Eiffel. It was originally intended to be temporary.' Keep it accessible — no full passive grammar lesson, just the was/were + past participle pattern in context. Contrast with active: 'Gustave Eiffel designed it' → 'It was designed by Gustave Eiffel.' Practice (20 min): Sentence transformation — 10 active sentences about Paris monuments → Gabriela rewrites in passive. Then, oral quiz: teacher gives a passive sentence with a blank ('The Mona Lisa was painted by ___') and Gabriela completes it. 8 questions. Cultural knowledge + grammar combo. Production (25 min): Gabriela is a tour guide leading a group of Brazilian tourists through the Louvre. Teacher plays a clueless tourist asking basic questions ('Who painted this?', 'When was this built?', 'Why is it famous?'). Gabriela must answer using both active and passive structures confidently. Finish by having her describe her 'favorite thing she saw today' in 5 sentences. Debrief: coach 2 passive constructions that sounded unnatural.
temas.homework: Choose ONE Paris landmark or artwork. Research 5 facts about it in English. Write a 'Museum Card' description (8–10 sentences) using at least 4 passive voice sentences ('It was built in…', 'It was designed by…', 'It is visited by…'). Also, record yourself reading it aloud as if you're the museum audio guide. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

temas.tema: Making Friends Abroad — Starting Conversations, Small Talk & Keeping a Friendship Going in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: question formation review — all tenses (present simple, past simple, present perfect, future) in question form; question tags for natural conversation: 'It's beautiful here, isn't it?', 'You've been to France before, haven't you?', 'You're not from here, are you?'; vocabulary: small talk, break the ice, common ground, get along (with), have a lot in common, keep in touch, follow on Instagram, exchange numbers, hang out, casual conversation; expressions: 'What brings you here?', 'How long have you been in Paris?', 'Where are you from originally?', 'Have you been to the Louvre yet?', 'We should hang out again!', 'Let's keep in touch!', 'I'll follow you on Instagram'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): 'Small talk blitz' — teacher and Gabriela stand up, teacher pretends to be a stranger at a Parisian café. Without preparation, Gabriela must start a conversation and keep it going for 90 seconds. Teacher notes where it breaks down. Presentation (15 min): Introduce question tags as 'conversation glue' — they keep dialogue flowing and show engagement. Board: affirmative + negative tag / negative + affirmative tag. Examples from Gossip Girl / Friends contexts: 'Blair is so dramatic, isn't she?', 'They haven't seen each other in years, have they?' Practice (20 min): Written question tag exercise (10 sentences to complete) — then oral practice: teacher makes 8 statements about Paris / the trip / series, Gabriela adds the correct question tag without hesitation. Then: 'Small talk bingo' — teacher gives Gabriela a bingo card with small talk topics (weather, hometown, first time in Paris, favorite food, series, plans). She must get a stranger (teacher) to mention each topic naturally through questions. Production (25 min): Full conversation simulation — Gabriela meets a British exchange student (teacher) at a Paris youth hostel common room. They must have a 4-minute natural conversation covering: where they're from, why they're in Paris, what they've done so far, and making plans to meet again. Debrief: highlight 3 especially smooth exchanges; work on 1 question tag that felt awkward.
temas.homework: Write a 'conversation script' for meeting someone new in Paris — create both sides of the dialogue (Gabriela + an exchange student from Australia). Use at least 5 question tags and 6 different question types (present simple, past simple, present perfect, future). Script should be 15–18 lines. Then read both parts aloud and record. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

temas.tema: Shopping in Paris — Boutiques, Souvenirs, Bargaining & Handling Money in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: quantifiers + comparative forms (review from Aula 18) for shopping comparisons — 'This one is more expensive than that one', 'Which one is cheaper?'; conditional type 1 introduction: 'If I buy this, I won't have money for dinner', 'If it's on sale, I'll get two'; vocabulary: boutique, souvenir, receipt, refund, exchange, fitting room, size (S/M/L/XL), sold out, in stock, discount, sale, price tag, cash, credit card, contactless payment, window shopping, browse; expressions: 'I'm just browsing, thanks', 'Do you have this in a different size?', 'Can I try this on?', 'Is this on sale?', 'Do you accept card?', 'Can I get a receipt?', 'I'd like to return this', 'Could I get a bag, please?'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 6 items (or item photos) on the table with price tags in euros. Gabriela has a 'budget' of €50 (a play-money envelope). She must decide what to buy and say why: 'I'll buy the scarf because it's cheaper than the bag and I love the color.' Activates comparatives and decision language. Presentation (15 min): Introduce Type 1 Conditional in shopping context: 'If I spend all my money on clothes, I won't be able to eat out.' Build 5 examples together on the board. Keep it practical — not abstract. Contrast with 'going to' and 'will' (already known). Practice (20 min): Gabriela has 3 scenarios (printed cards): (1) she finds a dress she loves but it's too expensive; (2) she wants to return a bag that broke; (3) she's looking for a specific souvenir and the shop doesn't have it. She prepares what to say for each and then role-plays with teacher. Written task: complete 5 Type 1 conditional sentences about shopping decisions. Production (25 min): Boutique simulation — teacher plays a Parisian shop assistant. Gabriela enters to shop for souvenirs and one item for herself. She must: browse, ask about sizes, try something on, handle a problem (wrong size), decide using a conditional, and pay. Teacher deliberately creates one 'complication' (card declined — she must handle it). Debrief: what vocabulary felt automatic? What needed prompting?
temas.homework: Imagine you have €100 to spend on souvenirs in Paris. Write a shopping list and for each item write one conditional sentence about your decision ('If I buy the perfume, I won't have money for the keychain'). Then, write a short dialogue (8–10 lines) between you and a shop assistant in a Paris boutique — include one problem. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

temas.tema: Telling Stories with Confidence — Narrative Tenses & How to Share What Happened Like a Natural Storyteller
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: integration of narrative tenses — past simple (main events), past continuous (background actions), present perfect (connection to now); NEW: past perfect — 'I had never seen anything like it before', 'By the time I arrived, the museum had already closed'; time expressions for storytelling: first, then, after that, suddenly, eventually, by the time, as soon as, while; vocabulary: storytelling signals — 'So, the thing is…', 'You won't believe this but…', 'And then, out of nowhere…', 'Long story short…', 'The funniest/scariest/weirdest part was…', 'And that's when I realized…'; narrative vocabulary: incident, adventure, unexpected, hilarious, awkward, embarrassing, terrifying, unforgettable
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher tells a short, dramatic 2-minute story about a (fictional) travel mishap in Paris — getting on the wrong metro, ending up in the wrong neighborhood — using all narrative tenses. Gabriela listens and identifies: past simple (main events), past continuous (background), past perfect (before the story started). Presentation (15 min): Focus on past perfect — the 'flashback' tense. Board examples anchored in Gabriela's world: 'I had already visited the Eiffel Tower when I realized I had left my phone at the hotel.' Concept check: what happened first? Timeline drawing on the board. Practice (20 min): Story reconstruction — teacher gives Gabriela 8 jumbled sentence strips from a travel story. She puts them in order, identifies the tense in each, and reads the full story aloud with natural pacing and emphasis. Then: fill in 5 past perfect sentences in a gap-fill about a Paris adventure. Production (25 min): 'Story Time' — Gabriela tells a 3-minute story (real or imagined) about something that happened to her during the week OR a scene from one of her favorite series, using all three narrative tenses plus at least 2 past perfect sentences. Teacher gives a story prompt if needed: 'Tell me about a time when something unexpected happened to you.' Record the story. Debrief: teacher plays back the recording and highlights 3 natural storytelling moments.
temas.homework: Write a story (12–15 sentences) titled 'The Day Everything Went Wrong in Paris' — fictional but believable. Use: past simple (main events), past continuous (background), past perfect (what happened before). Underline each tense in a different color. Also, find one 2-minute YouTube video of someone telling a travel story in English and note 5 time expressions they use. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

temas.tema: Talking About Health & Lifestyle — Eating Well, Exercising & Discussing Healthy Habits in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for habits (review) + NEW: present perfect continuous — 'I've been exercising more lately', 'I've been eating healthier since I started school'; 'should / shouldn't' for advice: 'You should drink more water', 'You shouldn't skip breakfast'; vocabulary: balanced diet, nutrition, protein, carbohydrates, hydration, vitamins, portion size, junk food, processed food, meal prep, workout routine, cardio, strength training, stretching, sleep hygiene, mental health, stress, well-being; expressions: 'I've been trying to…', 'I cut down on…', 'I'm trying to eat cleaner', 'I work out 3 times a week', 'I've been feeling more energetic', 'I'm thinking about…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): 'Healthy or not?' — teacher reads 10 habits aloud ('Eating pizza every day', 'Walking 30 minutes daily', 'Sleeping 5 hours'). Gabriela gives a thumbs up/down AND a sentence: 'You shouldn't eat pizza every day because it has too much fat.' Activates 'should/shouldn't' and health vocabulary. Presentation (15 min): Introduce present perfect continuous using Gabriela's own interest in health: 'I've been working out more since January', 'I've been drinking more water lately.' Contrast with present perfect simple: 'I've exercised this week' (result) vs. 'I've been exercising for a month' (ongoing process). Board timeline with both. Practice (20 min): Conversation questions drill — teacher asks health-related questions, Gabriela must answer using present perfect continuous: 'How long have you been interested in healthy eating?' → 'I've been thinking about my diet for about a year.' 8 questions. Then: advice card activity — Gabriela receives 5 'patient profiles' with bad habits and writes a 'should/shouldn't' prescription for each. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is being interviewed for a YouTube health channel called 'Teen Wellness Talk'. Teacher is the interviewer asking about her routine, habits, advice for other teens, and what she's been doing differently lately. Gabriela must speak for at least 3 minutes total, using PPC and should/shouldn't naturally. Debrief: how did the language feel? 2 strong moments + 1 correction.
temas.homework: Write your 'Weekly Health Report' in English (10–12 sentences): what you've been eating, how you've been exercising, how you've been feeling. Use present perfect continuous throughout. Also, write 5 health tips for a Brazilian friend going on a long trip to Europe — use 'you should / you shouldn't'. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

temas.tema: Talking About the Future — Dreams, Ambitions & Life After High School with Real Conditional Language
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: Type 1 Conditional review ('If I study hard, I'll get a scholarship') + NEW: Type 2 Conditional — hypothetical/imaginary: 'If I lived in Paris, I would study French every day', 'If I were fluent in English, I would apply to an international university'; 'I wish + past simple': 'I wish I spoke French', 'I wish I had more time'; vocabulary: ambition, career path, degree, major, apply, scholarship, gap year, exchange student, fluent, bilingual, achieve, goal, dream, aspiration, opportunity, university entrance, future plans; expressions: 'In the future, I'd love to…', 'My dream is to…', 'If everything goes well, I'll…', 'Ideally, I would…', 'I'm planning to…', 'I wish I could…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher gives Gabriela 3 dream scenario cards ('You are fluent in English', 'You live in Paris', 'You have a scholarship to a UK university') — she must say one sentence for each using 'If I… I would…' without preparation. Activates hypothetical thinking. Presentation (15 min): Contrast Type 1 (realistic) and Type 2 (hypothetical) on the board using Gabriela's real goals: 'If I practice English every day, I'll be fluent by 2027' (Type 1 — real possibility) vs. 'If I were already fluent, I would apply to Sciences Po in Paris' (Type 2 — imaginary, not yet real). Introduce 'I wish' as a shortcut for Type 2 feelings. Practice (20 min): Sentence transformation — 8 prompts to complete: 4 Type 1, 4 Type 2. Then: 'Dream Interview' warm-up — teacher asks questions, Gabriela responds mixing both conditional types: 'If you could study anywhere in the world, where would you go and why?' Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is being interviewed by a journalist writing an article about 'Ambitious Brazilian Teenagers and Their International Dreams.' She must talk about her future plans, wishes, and hypothetical scenarios for 4 minutes, mixing Type 1, Type 2, and 'I wish' naturally. Teacher creates two 'hard questions' that push her to think on her feet. Record the interview. Debrief: highlight 2 conditional sentences that were especially fluent.
temas.homework: Write an essay titled 'My Future: Plans, Dreams & Wishes' (14–16 sentences). Include: 3 Type 1 Conditionals (realistic plans), 3 Type 2 Conditionals (dreams), 3 'I wish' sentences, and 5 future-oriented expressions. Also, find one famous person's interview in English where they talk about their dreams/plans — note 3 sentences they use about the future. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

temas.tema: Handling Problems & Complaining Politely — When Things Go Wrong in Paris and You Need to Fix It in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'I'd like to + verb' for formal complaints; 'The problem is that…', 'It seems that…', 'I was told that…' for explaining issues; past simple to narrate what happened before the complaint; Type 1 Conditional for requests: 'If you can fix this today, I'd really appreciate it'; vocabulary: complaint, issue, refund, replacement, compensation, manager, supervisor, misunderstanding, reservation error, overcharged, faulty, damaged, missing, unacceptable, inconvenient, resolve, apologize; expressions: 'Excuse me, I think there's been a mistake', 'I'd like to speak to the manager, please', 'I was charged the wrong amount', 'This isn't what I ordered', 'I'd like a refund', 'Could you look into this for me?', 'I appreciate your help with this'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher reads 5 'bad situations' aloud (wrong hotel room, overcharged at a restaurant, broken luggage from the airline, incorrect order at a café, lost reservation). Gabriela rates how confident she'd feel handling each from 1–5 in English — then the teacher says 'Today we fix ALL of those.' Energy-building intro. Presentation (15 min): Complaint language spectrum on the board — from aggressive ('This is unacceptable!') to assertive ('I'd like to resolve this, please') to passive (saying nothing). Focus on assertive, polite but firm register. Board: model complaint structure — (1) state the problem, (2) explain what happened, (3) request a solution. Practice (20 min): Written practice — Gabriela rewrites 4 aggressive complaints into polite English. Then: response drill — teacher plays someone who won't cooperate ('Sorry, there's nothing I can do') and Gabriela must escalate politely without getting rude ('In that case, could I speak to your supervisor, please?'). Production (25 min): 'Problem Day in Paris' simulation — 3 consecutive complaint scenarios: (1) restaurant overcharged her by €15; (2) hotel gave her a smoking room when she requested non-smoking; (3) a souvenir she bought is broken and she wants a replacement. Teacher plays a different unhelpful staff member each time, creating realistic friction. Gabriela must resolve each one in English. Debrief: which scenario was hardest? Replay the hardest one with coaching.
temas.homework: Write a formal complaint email (10–12 sentences) to a Paris hotel about a problem during your stay — choose a realistic issue. Use: past simple to explain what happened, 'I was told that…', 'I'd like a refund/replacement', formal closing. Also, write 3 complaint sentences and 3 polite request sentences practicing today's expressions. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

temas.tema: Social Media, Pop Culture & Celebrity Talk — Discussing Fame, Trends & Digital Life in Fluent English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present perfect continuous for ongoing trends ('People have been talking about this for weeks'); reported speech — introduction: 'She said (that) she was going on tour', 'He told reporters that he was leaving the show'; vocabulary: influencer, viral, trending, content creator, follower, engagement, post, reel, algorithm, streaming platform, season finale, cancel culture, comeback, fandom, stan, aesthetic, collaboration, brand deal, premiere; expressions: 'Have you heard about…?', 'Apparently, she said that…', 'According to…', 'People are saying that…', 'I can't believe she posted that!', 'It went completely viral', 'The internet is obsessed with…', 'I've been following this story for weeks'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 3 printed 'celebrity news headlines' (real or fictional, based on shows/celebrities Gabriela follows) and asks 'What do you know about this? Tell me everything!' She free-talks for 2 minutes — teacher notes spontaneous vocabulary and gaps. Presentation (15 min): Introduce reported speech in the context of celebrity gossip — this is the most natural context for it. 'Blake Lively said she loved filming Gossip Girl' → Direct: 'I loved filming it'. Reported: 'She said (that) she loved filming it.' Focus only on say/tell + that + past tense shift. Keep it conversational. Practice (20 min): 'Celebrity News Feed' activity — teacher gives Gabriela 6 celebrity 'direct quote cards'. She rewrites each in reported speech, then reads it aloud as if reporting to a friend. Gossip-style: 'She said that she was NOT happy with the season finale!' Then: conversation drill — teacher says a direct quote, Gabriela reports it back immediately. Production (25 min): Gabriela hosts a 4-minute 'Pop Talk' segment — she's a social media host reviewing this week's biggest celebrity and pop culture news. She uses reported speech to share what celebrities 'said', what fans 'are saying', and gives her own opinions. Teacher plays a co-host who asks follow-up questions. Record the segment. Debrief: highlight 3 natural reported speech uses.
temas.homework: Find ONE real celebrity interview on YouTube (any English-speaking celebrity Gabriela likes — from Friends, Gossip Girl, or pop music). Watch 3–4 minutes. Write 6 sentences in reported speech about what the celebrity said: 'She said that she…', 'He told the interviewer that he…'. Also, write a 5-sentence Instagram caption in English about an imaginary celebrity event you 'attended'. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

temas.tema: Talking About School & Study Life — Academic English for Describing School, Subjects & Learning Experience
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for routines + present perfect for experiences + 'used to' for contrasting past and present school habits: 'I used to hate math, but now I find it interesting'; vocabulary: subject (biology, literature, history, chemistry, math, PE, philosophy), semester, grade, essay, assignment, deadline, presentation, exam, final exam, study group, extracurricular activity, elective, school system, report card, GPA equivalent, tutor; expressions: 'I'm in my second year of high school', 'My favorite subject is…', 'I'm struggling with…', 'I used to find it boring, but…', 'We have exams coming up', 'I study best when…', 'I'm thinking of pursuing…'; cultural comparison: Brazilian vs. French/American school systems briefly
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): 'School speed round' — teacher points at Gabriela and says a subject in Portuguese. She says it in English + one sentence about it: 'That's biology. I used to find it really hard, but now I like it.' 8 subjects in under 3 minutes. Presentation (15 min): Introduce 'used to' structure — the grammar of contrasting past habits with now. Board: 'I used to study very little. Now I study every day.' / 'I didn't use to like English. Now I love it.' Concept check: is it happening now? No — it was a past habit. Drilling: teacher says a prompt ('school in 7th grade') and Gabriela makes 2 'used to' sentences. Practice (20 min): Written task — Gabriela writes 6 'used to vs. now' sentences about her school life. Then: 'School System Compare' — teacher presents 3 differences between Brazilian and French school systems. Gabriela comments using comparatives: 'In Brazil, we have 3 years of high school, but in France they have the Baccalauréat which is more…' Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is talking to her future exchange host family (teacher) in France. They ask all about her school in São Paulo — what subjects she takes, what she used to find hard, what she's planning to study, what school is like in Brazil. Gabriela must maintain the conversation for 4+ minutes without switching to Portuguese. Debrief: review 2 'used to' sentences she produced; coach any present simple errors.
temas.homework: Write a 'School Profile' paragraph in English (12–14 sentences) describing your school life in São Paulo — include: current subjects, your favorites and least favorites, 4 'used to' sentences comparing middle school to high school, and your future study plans. Also, research in English: 'What is the French Baccalauréat?' and write 3 sentences explaining what you found. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

temas.tema: Real Conversations, Real Confidence — Fluency Drills, Hesitation Strategies & Sounding Natural in English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: no new grammar — fluency integration of all structures from Aulas 21–38; Focus: conversation management strategies — hesitation fillers: 'Well…', 'Let me think…', 'That's a good question…', 'How do I put this?', 'What I mean is…'; repair strategies: 'Sorry, what I meant to say was…', 'Let me rephrase that…'; asking for clarification: 'Could you say that again?', 'Sorry, I didn't catch that', 'What do you mean by…?'; turn-taking: 'Actually, I think…', 'That's interesting, but…', 'I see what you mean, but…'; active listening: 'Really?', 'Oh wow!', 'I know, right?', 'That makes sense', 'Go on…'; pronunciation focus: word stress in multi-syllable words, linking sounds (gonna, wanna, kinda, I'd, we'll)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher starts a casual conversation with NO clear topic — just talks. Gabriela must keep the conversation going using fillers, active listening, and turn-taking. Timer: 3 minutes. After: debrief — how many times did she go silent? What did she use to buy time? Presentation (15 min): 'Conversation Toolkit' — teacher presents all hesitation, repair, and clarification strategies on a card that Gabriela keeps. Demonstrate each with exaggerated examples. Pronunciation focus: natural contractions and reductions in fast speech — 'gonna', 'wanna', 'I'd love to', 'we'll see'. Practice (20 min): 'Hot Seat' — Gabriela sits in the 'hot seat' and teacher asks rapid-fire questions on random topics (Paris, series, school, food, future, music, health). She cannot be silent for more than 2 seconds — she must use a filler or strategy. 15 questions. Then: clarification drill — teacher mumbles or speaks very fast; Gabriela practices asking for clarification without apologizing excessively. Production (25 min): 30-minute Free Conversation (real-time fluency test) — teacher and Gabriela have a completely natural, unscripted conversation about any topic that comes up. Topics can include: Paris trip, series, school, dreams, pop culture. No grammar exercises — just talk. Teacher notes: hesitation use, repair strategies, turn-taking quality, and vocabulary range. Debrief: 3 moments she sounded TRULY natural + 1 area to focus on going into the final block.
temas.homework: Every day this week: record a 2-minute 'daily thought' audio in English — pick any topic (what you watched, what you ate, how you felt, what you're planning). Do not stop to think in Portuguese. Use hesitation fillers instead of silence. Send ONE recording at the end of the week. Also, watch one episode of Friends with English subtitles and note 5 hesitation fillers or conversation strategies the characters use naturally. Estimated time: 15 min/day + 30 min for the Friends episode.

temas.tema: Block 2 Closing & Preview — Gabriela's Halfway-Home Celebration, Language Audit & Vision for the Final Stretch
temas.focoLinguistico: Review + integration of ALL key structures from Aulas 21–39: future with 'will', passive voice, Type 1 and Type 2 conditionals, reported speech, past perfect, present perfect continuous, used to, relative clauses, question tags, narrative tenses; vocabulary audit: travel (airport, hotel, restaurant, transport, monuments), emotions, health, pop culture, school, shopping, social; self-assessment language: 'I've improved in…', 'I'm now able to…', 'I used to struggle with… but now I can…'; preview language for Block 3 targets: 'By Aula 48, I will be able to…', 'My goal for the next 8 weeks is…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Gabriela walks in to find the room decorated with a mini 'halfway to Paris' theme — printed Eiffel Tower on the board, a 'Bienvenue' sign, a mini French flag. Teacher says: 'Let's celebrate what you've built.' Energy check-in: Gabriela rates her confidence in 8 skill areas from 1–10 (speaking fluently, asking for help, telling stories, handling problems, talking about feelings, future plans, pop culture discussion, understanding English) — creates a live profile of Block 2 outcomes. Presentation/Review (20 min): Visual 'Block 2 Grammar Map' on the board — all 20 aulas summarized in a single visual. Teacher highlights 5 structures Gabriela handled best and 2 that need more reinforcement. Mini spot-check: 10 rapid oral questions across all topics — she answers immediately using the correct structure. Production/Celebration (30 min): 'Block 2 Showcase' — Gabriela performs 3 mini-tasks without preparation: (1) 2-minute monologue: 'Imagine it's February 2027. You just landed in Paris. What do you do first? How do you feel? What will you do?' — using multiple tenses and conditionals; (2) Role-play: she meets a British girl at the Paris youth hostel and makes a friend (using question tags, small talk, invitation language); (3) Quick storytelling: 90-second story about 'the best/worst day of my imaginary Paris trip' — using narrative tenses. Record everything. Debrief + Goal-Setting (10 min): Celebrate genuine wins. Set 3 concrete personal goals for Block 3. Teacher writes them on a card that Gabriela keeps: 'By Aula 48, Gabriela will be able to ___.' Preview: what's coming in Block 3 — more complex grammar, longer conversations, more independence.
temas.homework: Watch your Block 2 recordings (especially Aulas 24 and 40) and write a 'Progress Letter to Future Gabriela' in English (12–15 sentences): describe what you've learned, how you've grown, what surprised you about your progress, and 3 things you want to conquer in Block 3. This letter will be read again in Aula 48. Also, create a personal 'Paris Survival Vocabulary List' — the 30 most important words and expressions you've learned in Block 2 that you'll definitely use in France. Estimated time: 45–55 minutes.

temas.tema: Giving Your Opinion Like a Native — Agreeing, Disagreeing & Hedging in Real Conversations About Series, Travel & Life
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: expressing opinion with hedged language — 'I think…', 'I believe…', 'In my opinion…', 'I'm not sure, but…', 'It seems to me that…'; agreeing — 'I totally agree', 'Exactly!', 'That's a good point', 'You're right'; disagreeing politely — 'I see your point, but…', 'I'm not sure I agree', 'Actually, I think…', 'That's interesting, but…'; softeners — 'kind of', 'sort of', 'a little bit', 'maybe'; vocabulary for opinions on pop culture and travel: fascinating, overrated, underrated, disappointing, worth it, highly recommend, not my thing, a must-see, iconic, hyped. Proportion: 50% general (opinion structures) + 50% focus (series, Paris, school life contexts). Level: A2 moving toward B1.
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Teacher presents 6 bold statements on flashcards — 'Paris is the most beautiful city in the world', 'Gossip Girl is better than Friends', 'School is more stressful than work', 'Traveling alone is scary', 'Social media is bad for teenagers', 'Learning English is fun'. Gabriela physically moves to a 'AGREE' or 'DISAGREE' corner of the room. She must say ONE sentence per card using the target structures. Teacher models first. PRESENTATION (15 min): Teacher introduces the 3-layer opinion model on the board — CLAIM ('I think Paris is amazing') + REASON ('because it has incredible food and history') + EXAMPLE ('For instance, the Louvre has over 35,000 works of art'). Gabriela drills the structure orally, building 3 opinion sentences about things she loves. PRACTICE (20 min): Card debate game — teacher places 4 topic cards face down (Gossip Girl vs. Friends, Paris vs. London, cooking at home vs. eating out, studying abroad vs. staying in Brazil). Gabriela flips a card, gets 20 seconds to prepare, then gives a 60-second opinion using the 3-layer model. Teacher plays devil's advocate, forcing use of disagreement phrases. PRODUCTION (25 min): Mini debate simulation — 'Should Gabriela study in Paris or in London for her exchange program?' Teacher takes the London side. Gabriela must defend Paris using opinion language, facts from previous lessons (Aulas 26, 30), and hedging phrases when unsure. Product: a 2-minute recorded opinion monologue on the topic of her choice from the session. WRAP-UP (10 min): Gabriela self-evaluates — 'Which opinion phrase felt most natural? Which felt weird?' Teacher introduces 'conversation fillers' as bridge to Aula 42: 'Well…', 'You know…', 'I mean…'
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Watch one scene from Friends or Gossip Girl (any episode Gabriela knows well) and pause every time a character gives an opinion. Write down 5 opinion phrases used — note if they agree, disagree, or hedge. Task 2 (15 min): Record a 90-second audio on the topic: 'What do I think about my trip to France in 2027?' Use at least 4 different opinion structures from today's lesson. Send the recording. Task 3 (10 min): Write 3 sentences in the notebook — one thing she agrees with about school life in Brazil, one thing she disagrees with, one she's not sure about — all in English.

temas.tema: Fluency Boosters — Conversation Fillers, Hesitation Strategies & Sounding Confident Even When You Don't Know the Word
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: no new grammar — fluency layer built on ALL structures from Aulas 1–41; Focus: conversation management strategies — fillers ('Well…', 'Let me think…', 'You know…', 'I mean…', 'Actually…', 'So basically…'); hesitation sounds ('Hmm…', 'Uh…', 'Um…'); circumlocution — describing a word you don't know ('It's like a…', 'It's the thing you use to…', 'It's similar to…', 'The opposite of…', 'What's the word…?'); repair strategies ('Sorry, I mean…', 'What I'm trying to say is…', 'Let me rephrase that…'); turn-taking — 'What about you?', 'Do you know what I mean?', 'Right?', 'Doesn't it?'; vocabulary for conversation: fluent, confident, hesitate, rephrase, clarify, describe, explain, natural. Proportion: 50% general (fluency strategies) + 50% focus (real scenarios from Paris trip and daily life).
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Teacher speaks in Portuguese for 30 seconds about a complex topic — asks Gabriela: 'Did you notice what I did to keep talking even when I paused?' Introduces the concept of 'keeping the ball in the air' — fluent speakers fill silence with strategies, not silence. Shows a short clip (YouTube: native English speaker fumbling in conversation) — what did they say to keep going? PRESENTATION (15 min): Teacher introduces the 'Fluency Toolkit' on a visual poster — 4 tools: (1) Fillers, (2) Hesitation bridges, (3) Circumlocution, (4) Repair. Each tool gets 2–3 examples drilled chorally. Gabriela repeats, records herself saying each phrase, listens back. PRACTICE 1 — Circumlocution Drill (15 min): Teacher draws a word on a card (umbrella, departure gate, souvenir, museum guide) — Gabriela must describe it in English WITHOUT saying the word. Teacher tries to guess. Switch roles. 10 words total. This simulates 'I don't know the word in English' situations she'll face in Paris. PRACTICE 2 — Hot Seat (15 min): Gabriela is in the 'hot seat'. Teacher asks rapid-fire questions about her life, Paris trip, series, opinions. Gabriela MUST answer within 3 seconds — no silence allowed. She can use any filler or hesitation strategy but must keep talking for at least 20 seconds per answer. If she goes silent for 5+ seconds, she loses a point. PRODUCTION (20 min): 3-minute unscripted conversation simulation — Teacher plays a French student Gabriela meets at a youth hostel in Paris. Topics emerge naturally: where they're from, what they're doing in Paris, what they think of the city, recommendations. Gabriela must use at least 3 fillers, 2 hesitation bridges, and 1 circumlocution organically. Product: full conversation recorded for self-review. WRAP-UP (5 min): Gabriela identifies her 'comfort filler' — the phrase she'll use most in Paris when she needs time to think.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Listen to a 3-minute podcast clip or YouTube video (native English, any topic she finds interesting). Count how many times the speaker uses fillers or hesitation phrases. Write them in the notebook with timestamps. Task 2 (15 min): Record a 2-minute audio describing 3 objects from around her bedroom WITHOUT saying the object's name — use circumlocution. Ex: 'It's the thing you use to…', 'It's like a small…'. Send the recording. Task 3 (10 min): Have a 5-minute WhatsApp voice note conversation with herself — pretend she's telling a friend about her day in Paris. Use at least 5 filler phrases from today's lesson.

temas.tema: Talking About the Past With Confidence — Gabriela's Story: Childhood Memories, School Moments & 'Back in the Day' English
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'used to + verb' for past habits and states (review from Aula 38, now as ACTIVE production focus) — 'I used to live near school', 'I didn't use to like studying English', 'Did you use to watch cartoons?'; contrast with present: 'I used to… but now I…'; 'would + verb' for repeated past actions (introduction) — 'Every weekend, my family would go to the park'; past simple for specific events (review): 'When I was 10, I started…'; time expressions: back then, when I was younger, as a child, in those days, a few years ago, at the time, ever since; vocabulary for childhood and school: grow up, primary school, best friend, neighborhood, used to be, change, realize, remember, miss (saudade), childhood memory, embarrassing moment. Proportion: 50% general (used to / would for past) + 50% focus (Gabriela's own life story, school in SP).
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Teacher shows 5 nostalgic images — old cartoon characters, a playground, a school cafeteria, a birthday party, a family road trip. Asks: 'What does this remind you of?' Gabriela responds in English using whatever language she has. Teacher notes gaps — these become the lesson's target structures. PRESENTATION (15 min): Teacher draws a simple timeline on the board — PAST vs. NOW. Introduces 'used to' with examples from Gabriela's context: 'When I was in 6th grade, I used to wake up at 6 AM. Now I wake up at 7.' Drills affirmative, negative, and question forms chorally and individually. Introduces contrast phrase 'I used to… but now I…' with 5 personal examples. Gabriela creates 5 sentences about her own life. PRACTICE 1 — Memory Map (15 min): Gabriela draws a simple 'memory map' of her childhood — 4 bubbles: home, school, free time, food. For each bubble, she writes 2 sentences using 'used to'. Teacher monitors and reformulates errors. Gabriela reads each sentence aloud with target pronunciation. PRACTICE 2 — Interview Simulation (15 min): Teacher plays a French pen pal video-calling for the first time and asking about Gabriela's childhood in São Paulo. Gabriela answers using 'used to', past simple, and time expressions. 8 questions total — 'Did you use to live in a different neighborhood?', 'What did you use to do on weekends?', 'Did you use to like school?' etc. PRODUCTION (20 min): 'My Story' oral presentation — Gabriela prepares and delivers a 2-minute spoken narrative: 'My life before and after I started learning English.' Must include: 3 'used to' sentences, 2 contrast sentences (used to… but now…), 1 'would' sentence, and at least 5 vocabulary items from today. Product: recorded and played back for self-correction. WRAP-UP (5 min): Teacher highlights most natural sentence from Gabriela's production — celebrates progress. Preview Aula 44.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Write a short paragraph (8–10 sentences) in the notebook titled 'When I Was a Kid in São Paulo' — use at least 5 'used to' sentences, 2 contrast sentences, and 3 time expressions from today's lesson. Task 2 (15 min): Record a 2-minute audio: 'Interview with my past self' — Gabriela asks herself questions (as the interviewer) and answers them (as her 10-year-old self). Use 'used to' and past simple naturally. Send the recording. Task 3 (optional, 10 min): Find one scene in Friends or Gilmore Girls where a character talks about their past. Write down 2 phrases they use to refer to the past.

temas.tema: Making It Count — Phrasal Verbs for Everyday Life: The 20 You Actually Need in Paris, at School & in Real Conversations
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: phrasal verbs — structure review (verb + particle; verb + two particles); meaning categories: (1) Travel & movement: check in, check out, get on, get off, take off, pick up, drop off, set off; (2) Social & daily: hang out, catch up, go out, come over, find out, look up; (3) Problem-solving: figure out, sort out, run out of, deal with, give up; (4) Communication: bring up, speak up, point out; key patterns: transitive vs. intransitive ('pick it up' vs. 'take off'); position of object pronoun with transitive phrasal verbs ('pick it up' NOT 'pick up it'); pronunciation focus: stress on the PARTICLE in phrasal verbs; vocabulary in context: the 20 phrasal verbs above used in sentences about Paris, school, and daily life in São Paulo. Proportion: 60% general (phrasal verb structure + core list) + 40% focus (Paris trip, daily routine, social life with friends).
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Teacher writes 5 sentences on the board using phrasal verbs WITHOUT explaining them — 'We need to check in by 3 PM', 'I ran out of euros', 'Let's hang out near the Eiffel Tower', 'Did you figure out how to use the metro?', 'Don't give up — you can do this!' Gabriela guesses meaning from context. This activates prior exposure and reduces overwhelm. PRESENTATION (15 min): Teacher introduces 'The Paris Survival Phrasal Verb Pack' — a physical card set of 20 phrasal verbs (hand-drawn or printed), each with: verb, particle, example sentence, and a small icon. Gabriela sorts them into 4 piles: Travel / Social / Problem-solving / Communication. Drills pronunciation of each — stress on particle ('check IN', 'find OUT', 'give UP'). PRACTICE 1 — Phrasal Verb Stories (20 min): Teacher reads a mini story (8 sentences) about Gabriela's first day in Paris — every sentence contains one phrasal verb. Gabriela listens and identifies the phrasal verbs. Then re-tells the story in her own words. Next: Gabriela replaces underlined words with the correct phrasal verb from the card set. PRACTICE 2 — Sentence Auction (15 min): Teacher writes 8 sentences on the board — some grammatically correct ('I need to pick it up'), some incorrect ('I need to pick up it', 'She gave up on'). Gabriela 'bids' by saying CORRECT or INCORRECT and corrects the wrong ones. Fast-paced and energetic. PRODUCTION (15 min): Role-play — Gabriela's friend from Brazil texts her (teacher plays the friend via written cards) with 8 questions about her Paris trip: 'Did you check in okay?', 'Did you hang out with anyone?', 'Did you run out of money?', etc. Gabriela responds verbally in full sentences using the target phrasal verbs. Product: a recorded 90-second 'Paris update' voice message using at least 8 phrasal verbs. WRAP-UP (5 min): Gabriela picks her TOP 5 most useful phrasal verbs for Paris. She writes them on a personal card to keep in her wallet.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Create a 'Phrasal Verb Journal' page in the notebook — draw or print the 20 phrasal verbs from today in a visual map. For each one, write your own original example sentence about YOUR life (not the ones from class). Task 2 (15 min): Record a 2-minute audio: 'My morning routine using phrasal verbs' — describe waking up, getting ready, going to school using as many phrasal verbs as possible. Send the recording. Task 3 (15 min): Watch any 5-minute clip from Friends or Gossip Girl and hunt for phrasal verbs — write down at least 3 with the timestamp and context sentence.

temas.tema: Real Talk — Passive Voice in Context: How the World Describes Things (Museums, News, Travel Guides & Pop Culture)
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: passive voice — review of present simple passive ('The Louvre is visited by millions of people every year') and past simple passive ('The painting was stolen in 1911') from Aula 40 context; NEW focus: using passive voice naturally in context — when we don't know the agent, when the agent is obvious, when the object is more important than the subject; passive with modals: 'can be seen', 'should be visited', 'must be booked in advance'; common passive collocations in travel and culture: 'is known for', 'is located in', 'was built in', 'is considered', 'is recognized as', 'was designed by', 'is surrounded by', 'is open to the public'; vocabulary: monument, exhibit, entrance fee, guided tour, architecture, masterpiece, landmark, heritage, renovate, restore, preserve. Proportion: 50% general (passive voice structure + modal passive) + 50% focus (Paris landmarks, travel guide language, museum culture).
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Teacher gives Gabriela a short tourist paragraph about the Eiffel Tower — 5 sentences, all in active voice. 'Gustave Eiffel designed the tower in 1889. Millions of tourists visit it every year. They built it for the World Fair.' Gabriela reads aloud. Teacher asks: 'How would a travel guide say this differently?' Introduces passive transformation as the 'official/formal' voice of travel, news, and museums. PRESENTATION (15 min): Teacher builds the passive construction visually: OBJECT → BE (correct tense) → PAST PARTICIPLE (+ by + agent, optional). Drills present and past passive with 10 sentences about Paris landmarks. Introduces modal passives ('should be visited', 'can be seen') with real travel guide examples. Gabriela sorts 10 sentences into ACTIVE / PASSIVE and rewrites 5 active → passive. PRACTICE 1 — Paris Travel Guide Writing (20 min): Gabriela receives a 'blank travel guide template' for 3 Paris attractions (Versailles, Notre-Dame, Montmartre). Teacher provides 5 facts per attraction in active voice. Gabriela transforms them into passive voice sentences and creates a mini travel guide paragraph for each. Read aloud with teacher feedback on natural flow. PRACTICE 2 — Pop Culture Passive (10 min): Teacher reads 8 statements about Gossip Girl and Friends in active voice. Gabriela transforms them into passive: 'They filmed Gossip Girl in New York' → 'Gossip Girl was filmed in New York.' Fast-paced, fun, uses familiar content to reinforce the structure. PRODUCTION (20 min): 'Gabriela's Paris Guide' oral presentation — Gabriela presents a 2-minute spoken audio guide for ONE Paris attraction of her choice (from Aulas 26, 30 or new research). Must use: 3 present passive, 2 past passive, 1 modal passive, and 4 vocabulary items from today. Product: recorded audio guide, simulating a real museum audio tour. WRAP-UP (5 min): Debrief — when does passive feel natural? Teacher highlights 3 phrases Gabriela can use spontaneously in Paris conversations.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Find a real English-language travel guide article about one Paris landmark (TripAdvisor, Lonely Planet, or BBC Travel). Underline every passive voice sentence you find. Write 5 of them in the notebook and explain WHY passive was used (agent unknown / object more important / formal register). Task 2 (15 min): Record a 90-second audio tour of your bedroom or your school — describe it as if it were a famous place. Use at least 4 passive sentences. 'This room is used for…', 'The desk was bought in…', 'The window can be seen from…'. Send the recording. Task 3 (10 min): Write 3 sentences in passive voice about São Paulo to use when explaining your city to French students in Paris.

temas.tema: Putting It All Together — Full Simulation: Gabriela's 48-Hour Paris Immersion (Arrival, Exploration, Social & Emergency Scenarios)
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: NO new grammar — full integration simulation using ALL structures from Aulas 1–45; target structures in active use: greetings + introductions (Aula 2), present simple + continuous (Aulas 4, 13), past simple + continuous (Aulas 14, 33), present perfect (Aula 15), future will + going to (Aulas 16, 21), modals (Aulas 8, 22, 23), conditionals (Aula 35), passives (Aula 45), phrasal verbs (Aula 44), opinion language (Aula 41), fillers + fluency (Aula 42), hotel + restaurant + airport language (Aulas 22, 23, 28, 29), directions (Aula 26), complaints (Aula 36), health emergencies (Aula 17); vocabulary recycled from ALL thematic units: travel, food, culture, emotions, daily life, pop culture. Proportion: 40% general (grammar integration) + 60% focus (complete Paris simulation scenarios). This is the DRESS REHEARSAL for Aula 48.
temas.atividadeEmAula: This is a 90-minute FULL IMMERSION SIMULATION structured as a narrative day in Paris. Teacher stays in character as different people throughout. SCENE 1 — Arrival at Charles de Gaulle (15 min): Teacher plays customs officer. Gabriela handles passport, declares purpose of visit, asks where to get the metro. Switches to taxi driver — she negotiates the ride, asks about traffic, makes small talk. Target: modals, polite requests, directions vocab. SCENE 2 — Hotel Check-In + Problem (15 min): Teacher plays hotel receptionist. Gabriela checks in using her full name, confirms booking, asks about amenities. Problem introduced: 'I'm sorry, your room isn't ready yet.' Gabriela must complain politely and find a solution. Target: there is/are, I'd like, complaints language, passive ('The room hasn't been prepared yet'). SCENE 3 — Café & Museum (20 min): Teacher plays café server and then museum guide. Gabriela orders breakfast, asks about the menu, handles a dietary issue (no gluten), pays. At the museum, she asks about the exhibits, understands a passive-heavy audio guide, asks questions about the art. Target: food vocabulary, would like, passive voice, questions. SCENE 4 — Making a Friend (15 min): Teacher plays an English-speaking student from Australia also visiting Paris. They meet at a museum. Gabriela makes small talk, shares opinions about Paris (Aula 41), uses fillers (Aula 42), discusses her life in São Paulo and plans for the exchange. Target: present perfect, used to, opinion language, fluency strategies. SCENE 5 — Emergency! (10 min): Teacher introduces a crisis: Gabriela's phone is stolen. She must go to a police station (teacher plays officer), describe what happened, what it looks like, when it was taken. Target: past simple, passive, description vocabulary. DEBRIEF (15 min): Teacher and Gabriela review the simulation — what flowed? What blocked? Rate confidence in each scene 1–5. Teacher notes gaps to address in Aula 47. Product: a personal 'Gap List' of 5 things Gabriela wants to improve before Aula 48.
temas.homework: Task 1 (30 min): Write a 'Day in Paris' journal entry (10–15 sentences) in the notebook describing the 5 scenes from today's simulation as if they really happened. Use past simple as the main tense, with at least 2 passive sentences, 1 conditional, and 2 phrasal verbs. Task 2 (20 min): Record a 3-minute audio: 'What I would do differently if I did the simulation again.' Use conditional language ('If I were in that situation again, I would…') and opinion phrases. Send the recording. Task 3 (10 min): Review your personal Gap List from class. For each gap, write one sentence in English that you WISH you could have said during the simulation — bring it to Aula 47.

temas.tema: The Final Push — Targeted Refinement: Closing Gabriela's Gaps, Polishing Her English & Building Unshakeable Confidence for Paris
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: fully personalized to Gabriela's Gap List from Aula 46 — teacher selects top 3–5 weak areas identified in the simulation and targets them with intensive, focused drilling; likely candidates based on level trajectory: (1) question formation across tenses — 'Where was it made?', 'Have you ever been to…?', 'What were you doing when…?'; (2) modal nuance — 'should have', 'could have', 'might have' for reflecting on the past; (3) conditional fluency — 'If I were you, I would…', 'If that happens, I'll…'; (4) linking language for storytelling — 'As soon as', 'By the time', 'While', 'Until', 'After that'; (5) polite register switching — formal vs. informal; vocabulary: any gaps identified in Aula 46; PLUS new confidence phrases: 'I'd like to clarify…', 'Just to make sure I understand…', 'Could you repeat that more slowly, please?', 'I'm not sure how to say this in English, but…'. Proportion: 50% general (gap structures) + 50% focus (Paris scenarios rerun with improvements).
temas.atividadeEmAula: WARM-UP (10 min): Gabriela shares her Gap List sentences from homework — teacher reads them aloud, models the corrected version, drills the corrected form 3 times. No shame — this is a 'power-up' session. TARGETED DRILLING BLOCK (25 min): Based on actual gaps from Aula 46, teacher runs 3 focused micro-drills: (1) Question Formation Drill — teacher gives a statement, Gabriela converts to a question in the correct tense at speed (20 questions, timed); (2) Modal Past Reflection — teacher describes a situation, Gabriela responds with 'I should have / could have / might have': 'You arrived at the wrong hotel entrance.' → 'I should have checked the address more carefully.'; (3) Linking Language Storytelling — Gabriela retells Scene 5 (the stolen phone) from Aula 46 using 5 linking phrases. CONFIDENCE PHRASE INTEGRATION (15 min): Teacher introduces the 'Lifeline Kit' — 6 phrases Gabriela will use in Paris when she's stuck. Drilled until automatic: 'Could you say that again, more slowly?', 'I'm sorry, I don't understand', 'How do you say __ in English?', 'Is this the right place for…?', 'I think I made a mistake — can I fix it?', 'Thank you for your patience.' Role-play: each phrase used in a real Paris micro-scenario. PARIS SIMULATION RERUN (25 min): Teacher reruns the 2 WEAKEST scenes from Aula 46 with Gabriela — same scenarios, same characters, but now Gabriela plays with her corrections integrated. Teacher raises the difficulty slightly: faster speech, unexpected turns, more complex vocabulary. Gabriela must hold the conversation for 2 full minutes per scene without freezing. Product: recorded rerun of both scenes — Gabriela compares with Aula 46 recording to hear her own improvement. WRAP-UP (10 min): Teacher conducts a 'Final Language Audit' — 10 rapid questions across all units (Aula 1–46). Gabriela answers spontaneously. Score noted for celebration in Aula 48. Teacher writes a personal note: 'What Gabriela can do today that she couldn't 47 lessons ago.'
temas.homework: Task 1 (30 min): Record a 4-minute audio titled 'Gabriela in Paris — The Full Story.' Tell the story of your imaginary 48 hours in Paris (Aula 46 scenarios) as a confident, fluent narrative. Use: past simple, past continuous, present perfect, at least 3 phrasal verbs, 2 passive sentences, and 2 opinion phrases. This is your FINAL PRACTICE before the big celebration. Send the recording. Task 2 (15 min): Write your personal 'Paris Confidence Manifesto' in the notebook — 5 sentences starting with 'I can…' based on everything you've learned in the program. Ex: 'I can check into a hotel and handle problems politely in English.' Task 3 (10 min): Re-read your Aula 1 diagnostic notes or early homework — write 3 sentences comparing who you were then vs. now. Bring this to Aula 48.

temas.tema: Gabriela Está Pronta — Grand Finale: Full Paris Simulation, Language Celebration & the Road Ahead
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: FULL INTEGRATION — every major structure from Aulas 1–47 in active production; NO new grammar introduced; showcase structures: to be (Aula 2), present simple (Aula 4), past simple + continuous (Aulas 14, 33), present perfect (Aula 15), future will + going to (Aulas 16, 21), modals (Aulas 8, 22), conditionals (Aulas 35, 47), passive voice (Aulas 45), phrasal verbs (Aula 44), opinion language (Aula 41), fluency strategies (Aula 42), used to (Aula 43), narrative tenses (Aula 33); vocabulary showcase: travel, food, culture, social, health, academic, emotional — all domains covered across 48 lessons; celebration phrases: 'I'm proud of myself', 'I've come a long way', 'I can't believe how much I've learned', 'I'm ready for Paris', 'This is just the beginning.' Proportion: 40% general (grammar integration showcase) + 60% focus (complete Paris immersion finale + personal reflection).
temas.atividadeEmAula: This is a 90-minute CELEBRATION + FINAL ASSESSMENT structured as a complete experience. OPENING CEREMONY (10 min): Teacher opens with a warm, personal speech acknowledging Gabriela's journey — from A1+ to confident communicator. Shows a visual 'Language Journey Map' with all 48 lesson titles. Gabriela reads aloud her 'Paris Confidence Manifesto' from homework. Teacher responds in English, Gabriela responds in English — full English environment from the first second. FINAL GRAND SIMULATION (40 min): The complete Paris scenario — 6 scenes, all linked as one continuous narrative. Scene 1: Airport arrival (5 min) — customs, luggage, taxi. Scene 2: Hotel (5 min) — check-in, room problem, resolution. Scene 3: Café + Museum (10 min) — ordering, opinions, passive descriptions. Scene 4: New friendship (8 min) — small talk, life stories, exchange program discussion. Scene 5: Emergency (6 min) — something goes wrong, Gabriela handles it with confidence. Scene 6: Goodbye + future plans (6 min) — Gabriela thanks the 'friend' she met, talks about coming back, expresses feelings about Paris. ALL scenes are recorded as one continuous audio document — 'Gabriela's Paris Recording.' REFLECTION + GROWTH AUDIT (15 min): Teacher plays back a 60-second clip from Gabriela's EARLIEST recording (Aula 1 or Aula 2) and a 60-second clip from today's simulation. Gabriela and teacher listen together. Gabriela identifies: (1) 3 things that are completely different now, (2) her proudest sentence from today, (3) the moment she felt most confident. Teacher gives personalized written feedback — 'What Gabriela can do today that she couldn't on Day 1.' LOOKING FORWARD (15 min): Teacher and Gabriela co-create a 'Post-Program Plan' — (1) How to maintain English until Paris 2027: series recommendations (with listening tasks), podcast list, 10-min daily speaking habit; (2) What to review before the trip: top 5 scenarios to drill; (3) Next steps if Gabriela wants to continue to B1/B2. Teacher presents a personalized 'Vocabulary Legacy Card' — the 30 most important words/phrases Gabriela mastered, handwritten or printed. CELEBRATION CLOSE (10 min): Gabriela delivers one final 2-minute freestyle speech in English — topic: 'What this program meant to me and what I'm going to do in Paris.' No script, no notes. Teacher listens, then gives a final one-sentence response in English: the most genuine compliment earned. Product: full simulation recording + reflection notes + Post-Program Plan + Vocabulary Legacy Card. Gabriela leaves the room ready for Paris.
temas.homework: This is the FINAL homework — optional but deeply encouraged. Task 1 (30 min): Write a letter to your future self — to be opened the night before you leave for Paris in 2027. Write it in English. Tell yourself: what you learned, how hard you worked, what you're capable of, and one thing you want to do in Paris that felt impossible when you started. Keep it in your notebook. Task 2 (ongoing): Start your 10-minute daily English habit today — pick ONE of these: watch one Friends scene with English subtitles, record a 1-minute voice note in English about your day, or read 5 sentences from a travel blog in English. Do it every day until Paris. Task 3 (for the trip): Create a 'Paris Phrase Card' — write your 10 most important phrases on an index card to carry in your bag during the trip. Include: greeting a stranger, asking for directions, ordering food, handling a problem, introducing yourself. These are yours. You earned them.

perfil._geracaoFoco: Comunicação oral com confiança — foco em conversação cotidiana, cultura pop e preparação para viagem/intercâmbio. Distribuição estimada: Fala 50% / Escuta 30% / Vocabulário temático 15% / Escrita 5%. Contextos prioritários: apresentação pessoal, rotina diária, opinar sobre séries e celebridades, simulações de viagem (França/Europa), diálogos de intercâmbio. Skills prioritários: fluência com frases prontas (pattern-based), vocabulário de alta frequência, produção oral guiada com scaffolding progressivo e redução da dependência do português.

curriculo.tema: Who Is Gabriela? — Diagnostic Session + Personal Introduction Baseline
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Diagnostic focus: present simple (to be + have), personal pronouns, basic personal information vocabulary; Key patterns: 'My name is…', 'I'm from…', 'I'm … years old', 'I live in…', 'I like…', 'I want to…'; Vocabulary: name, age, city, school, hobby, travel, dream, favorite; Diagnostic targets: pronunciation quality, hesitation patterns, L1 interference, comfort with speaking English spontaneously
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Ice-breaker: Teacher introduces themselves fully in English using a visual mind map on paper, then invites Gabriela to point to any bubble she understands. This reduces pressure and activates receptive vocabulary. Phase 2 (20 min) — Diagnostic speaking task: Teacher asks open questions ('Tell me about yourself', 'What do you like doing?', 'Why do you want to learn English?') and records notes on fluency, vocabulary range, pronunciation, and L1 switch frequency — NOT correcting, just observing. Phase 3 (20 min) — Guided self-introduction: Teacher provides a printed 'My Profile Card' with blanks (Name / Age / City / School / Hobbies / Dream Trip / Favorite Series) and Gabriela fills it in English with scaffolded support. She then reads it aloud. Phase 4 (15 min) — Goal-setting ritual: Teacher and Gabriela co-create a 'Victory Wall' poster listing her 3 big English goals anchored to Paris 2027. Written in English together. Posted visually. This creates emotional investment. Phase 5 (10 min) — Debrief + preview: Teacher shares one genuine strength observed (e.g., pronunciation) and one focus area. Gabriela records a 60-second baseline audio: 'Hi, my name is Gabriela. I'm from São Paulo. I like…' — this becomes her benchmark for progress.
curriculo.homework: 1) Record a 60-second audio in English saying: your name, your age, your city, your school, 2 hobbies, and one sentence about your dream trip to France. Save it — you'll compare this to your final recording in Aula 48. (15 min) | 2) Write your 'My Profile Card' cleanly in your English notebook with illustrations or stickers — make it personal and fun. (10 min) | 3) Watch the first 5 minutes of Friends S01E01 with English subtitles. Write down 3 words or phrases you understood. (15 min)

curriculo.tema: Hello, Nice to Meet You! — Greetings, Introductions & the Alphabet in Real Contexts
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb 'to be' (am/is/are) — affirmative, negative, question forms; personal pronouns (I, you, he, she, we, they); subject-verb agreement at sentence level; Vocabulary (10+ words): hello, hi, good morning, good afternoon, good evening, nice to meet you, what's your name, how are you, fine, great, spell, last name, first name; Expressions: 'Nice to meet you too', 'How do you spell that?', 'My last name is Pires — P-I-R-E-S'; Phonics focus: English alphabet pronunciation, especially letters often confused by Portuguese speakers (A, E, G, H, I, J, R, W, Y)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up: Teacher and Gabriela greet each other in 5 different ways (formal, casual, energetic, sleepy, excited) using the target expressions. Repetition through role-play variation keeps it fun. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces 'to be' using Gabriela's Profile Card from Aula 1. 'You are Gabriela. You are 15. You are from São Paulo.' Then flips: 'I am your teacher. I am…' Visual grammar on paper with color-coded pronoun-verb pairs. Phase 3 (20 min) — Alphabet drilling with a purpose: Teacher explains that in France, Gabriela will need to spell her name at hotels, airports, and ticket counters. Practice spelling G-A-B-R-I-E-L-A and P-I-R-E-S aloud, then spell 5 celebrity names she knows (e.g., Blake Lively, Penn Badgley from Gossip Girl). Makes drilling feel relevant. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play 'Check-in at a French hotel': Teacher plays receptionist. 'Good evening! Can I have your name please?' Gabriela responds, spells her last name, says her city, answers 'How are you?' This simulates a real Paris scenario using today's grammar. Phase 5 (15 min) — Drilling + wrap-up: Gabriela writes 5 'to be' sentences about herself and 3 about a Gossip Girl character. Read aloud. Teacher gives immediate positive corrective feedback.
curriculo.homework: 1) Practice spelling your full name, your best friend's name, and the name of your favorite café or place in São Paulo — aloud, in English letters. Record yourself doing it. (10 min) | 2) In your notebook, write 8 sentences using 'to be': 4 about yourself, 2 about a Friends character, 2 negative sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'English Alphabet Pronunciation' (any clear American English version, ~5 min). Note the 3 letters that sound most different from Portuguese.

curriculo.tema: This Is My World — Talking About People, Places & Things Around Me
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: demonstrative pronouns (this, that, these, those); indefinite articles (a/an); definite article (the) — introduction; plural nouns (regular -s/-es); 'to be' in questions and short answers ('Is this…?' 'Yes, it is / No, it isn't'); Vocabulary (12 words): friend, family, school, class, teacher, book, phone, city, neighborhood, street, building, place; Expressions: 'This is my friend…', 'That's my school', 'These are my things', 'Is this your…?'; Context: Gabriela's daily environment in São Paulo + objects she carries
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up with real objects: Teacher asks Gabriela to put 5 personal items on the table (phone, notebook, pen, water bottle, etc.). Practice 'This is my phone', 'That is your pen' — physical, tactile, zero abstract grammar. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces demonstratives and articles using a printed visual with Gabriela's name and photos of São Paulo landmarks (Paulista Avenue, MASP, her school neighborhood). 'This is São Paulo. That is Paris.' Grammar emerges from context, not a table. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'My World Map' activity: Gabriela draws a simple map of her week on paper — home, school, her favorite place in SP. Labels each in English using target vocabulary + articles. Teacher circulates and prompts: 'Is this your neighborhood? What's that?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Gossip Girl scene adaptation: Teacher creates a simple printed dialogue between Blair and Serena introducing their world ('This is my school. That's my best friend.') Gabriela reads Blair's lines, teacher reads Serena's. Then they swap characters. Debrief: which sentences did she feel confident with? Phase 5 (15 min) — Sentence construction race: Teacher calls out a Portuguese phrase, Gabriela writes/says the English version as fast as possible. Focus on 'this/that/these/those' + articles. 10 rounds.
curriculo.homework: 1) Take 5 photos of places or things in your daily life in São Paulo (school corridor, your bedroom, your lunch, etc.) and write 1 English sentence per photo using 'this is' or 'these are'. Save them in a note on your phone. (20 min) | 2) Write 10 sentences in your notebook — 5 affirmative, 3 negative, 2 questions — using 'to be' + demonstratives + articles. (15 min) | 3) Rewatch a scene from Gossip Girl (any scene from S01) and count how many times you hear 'this' or 'that'. Write the number in your notebook.

curriculo.tema: My Daily Life — Describing Routines in São Paulo with the Present Simple
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple — affirmative (I/you/we/they + base verb; he/she/it + verb-s); frequency adverbs (always, usually, sometimes, never, every day); time expressions (in the morning, at night, on weekdays); Vocabulary (12 words): wake up, get up, go to school, have breakfast, study, watch, sleep, start, finish, eat, take the bus/metro, exercise; Expressions: 'I usually wake up at…', 'I always have breakfast before school', 'On weekdays, I…', 'I never…'; Context: Gabriela's actual São Paulo school-day routine
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Routine brainstorm: Teacher asks 'What's the first thing you do every day?' in English, gestures, mimes. Gabriela guesses and responds. Build a quick list of her actual routine verbs — not invented, HERS. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Using a printed timeline (6am to 11pm), teacher models 'I wake up at 7am. I usually take the metro to school.' Then maps Gabriela's real routine onto the timeline using her own information from the brainstorm. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Gabriela's Day vs. Rory Gilmore's Day': Teacher creates a simple split chart. Left side: Gabriela's routine (student fills in). Right side: Rory Gilmore from Gilmore Girls routine (teacher provides in English). Gabriela compares: 'Rory always studies in the morning. I usually study at night.' Genuine comparison using real character she loves. Phase 4 (20 min) — Oral production: Gabriela describes her full day in English without looking at notes — teacher prompts with timeline images. Goal: 8+ sentences. Teacher notes hesitation and L1 switches for later feedback. Phase 5 (15 min) — Grammar focus: Third person singular -s. Teacher introduces: 'She wakes up. She studies. She watches Gilmore Girls.' Gabriela writes 5 sentences about Rory in third person. Debrief on the -s rule.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write your full daily routine in English in your notebook — at least 10 sentences, mixing frequency adverbs (always, usually, sometimes, never). Include morning, afternoon, and night. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'This is my day in São Paulo.' Describe your routine naturally, as if talking to a new friend abroad. (15 min) | 3) Watch the first breakfast scene in any Gilmore Girls episode. Write down 3 routine verbs you hear in English.

curriculo.tema: Do You Like It? — Expressing Likes, Dislikes & Preferences (Pop Culture Edition)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for expressing preferences — 'I like / I love / I enjoy / I don't like / I hate + noun/gerund (-ing)'; question forms with 'do': 'Do you like…?', 'What do you like?'; short answers: 'Yes, I do / No, I don't'; Vocabulary (12 words): series, episode, season, character, actor, celebrity, romantic, drama, comedy, thriller, celebrity, favorite; Expressions: 'I'm obsessed with…', 'I can't stand…', 'I'm a huge fan of…', 'What's your favorite…?', 'Have you seen…?' (receptive only); Context: Gossip Girl, Friends, Gilmore Girls, Notting Hill, celebrities Gabriela knows
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Like or hate?' warm-up: Teacher holds up printed images of shows/movies (Gossip Girl, Friends, a horror movie, a soap opera, action film) — Gabriela reacts in English only: 'I love this!', 'I don't like that.' Pure oral reaction, no writing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher draws the 'Preference Ladder' on paper: hate → don't like → it's okay → like → love → obsessed. Gabriela places her shows on the ladder using full sentences. Grammar emerges naturally. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Hot Takes' game: Teacher reads out celebrity or show names. Gabriela gives a 2-3 sentence opinion in English using target structures. 'I love Blair Waldorf because she is smart and stylish. I don't like Chuck Bass in season 1. He is mean.'
