Teacher drills sentence structure after each response, not interrupting flow. Phase 4 (20 min) — Roleplay: Gabriela meets a French student at an international school. Teacher plays the French student: 'Do you like French films? What series do you watch?' Gabriela must respond and ask back. 5-minute dialogue, then debrief. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes 10 preference sentences — 5 likes, 3 dislikes, 2 questions she would ask a new international friend.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a 'My Favorites List' in English in your notebook: favorite series (3 sentences), favorite movie (3 sentences), favorite celebrity (3 sentences). Use 'I love', 'I like', 'I don't like', 'I hate'. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio: 'My top 3 favorite series and why.' Speak naturally — no reading, just talk. (10 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of Friends with English subtitles. Count how many times you hear 'I like', 'I love', or 'I don't like'. Write the sentences in your notebook.

curriculo.tema: Numbers, Time & Dates — The Tools You Need to Survive in Paris
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: cardinal numbers (1–1000+); ordinal numbers (1st–31st); telling time in American English ('It's three fifteen', 'It's half past two', 'It's a quarter to five'); dates in American English format (March 15th, February 3rd, 2027); question forms: 'What time is it?', 'When is your flight?', 'How much is it?'; Vocabulary (12 words): o'clock, quarter, half past, noon, midnight, flight, arrival, departure, ticket, price, total, change (money); Expressions: 'Excuse me, what time is it?', 'My flight is on February 3rd', 'How much does this cost?', 'Here's my ticket'; Paris 2027 anchor throughout
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Number warm-up: Teacher calls out numbers in random order (up to 1000), Gabriela writes them in digits. Then reverses: teacher shows digits, Gabriela says aloud. Fast pace, fun. Phase 2 (15 min) — Clock drilling: Teacher draws analog clocks on paper with different times. Gabriela says the time aloud in American English. Focus on quarter past, half past, quarter to — often confused. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Paris Schedule' simulation: Teacher creates a printed 'Trip Itinerary for Gabriela Pires — Paris, February 2027' with 6 fake events (museum visit 10:30am, lunch 1:15pm, Eiffel Tower 4:45pm, etc.). Gabriela reads the itinerary aloud, answers questions: 'What time is the museum visit?', 'When does she have lunch?' Real-world task anchored to her trip. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play at a Paris ticket counter: Teacher is the ticket seller at the Louvre. 'Hello! One ticket please. How much is it?' Gabriela buys a ticket, asks the time of the next guided tour, and confirms her date of visit. Drills 'How much?', 'When?', 'What time?' in context. Phase 5 (15 min) — Date dictation: Teacher dictates 8 important dates (including February 3rd 2027 as her 'departure date'). Gabriela writes in American English format and reads back. Debrief: What felt easy? What needs more practice?
curriculo.homework: 1) Write your 'Dream Paris Itinerary' for 5 days in English — at least 2 activities per day with times and dates. Use American English date format. (25 min) | 2) Practice saying times aloud: set a timer for 5 minutes, look at a clock every 30 seconds and say the time in English. (5 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'Telling Time in English — American English' (~7 min). Note any time expression you hadn't heard before.

curriculo.tema: Where Are You From? — Countries, Nationalities & the World Around Gabriela
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'to be' for origin and nationality; prepositions of place for countries (from, in, at); question forms: 'Where are you from?', 'Where is she from?', 'What nationality are you?'; adjective-noun agreement awareness (no agreement in English vs. Portuguese); Vocabulary (12 words): country, nationality, capital, language, culture, Brazilian, French, American, European, citizen, passport, international; Target countries: Brazil, France, USA, UK, Italy, Spain, Japan, Australia; Expressions: 'I'm from Brazil', 'I'm Brazilian', 'She's French', 'Is he American?', 'Where in France are you from?'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Flag quiz' warm-up: Teacher shows 10 printed flag images. Gabriela names the country AND nationality adjective in English. Fun, fast, zero pressure. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces nationality adjectives using a printed world map. Focus on patterns: Brazil → Brazilian, France → French, USA → American, Italy → Italian. Highlights the difference from Portuguese (no gender agreement in English). Gabriela repeats and internalizes 10 key nationalities. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Celebrity Nationality Game': Teacher gives clues about a celebrity without saying their name. 'She is American. She is a famous actress. She was in Gossip Girl.' Gabriela guesses and responds: 'Is she Blake Lively? She's American!' Then Gabriela creates 3 clues for teacher to guess. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: At a Paris youth hostel, Gabriela meets international travelers. Teacher plays 3 different characters (one American, one Italian, one Japanese). Gabriela introduces herself and asks where they're from. Full dialogue: 5+ exchanges per character. Focuses on 'Where are you from? / I'm from… / What language do you speak?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Written production: Gabriela writes a short paragraph (6-8 sentences) introducing herself to a potential international pen pal: name, nationality, city, language, and why she's learning English.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write 10 sentences about 10 different celebrities or fictional characters: their name, nationality, country, and one fact. Use 'She is French. She is from Paris.' format. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio: 'Hi! I'm Gabriela. I'm Brazilian. I'm from São Paulo, Brazil. I speak Portuguese and I'm learning English because…' Speak naturally. (10 min) | 3) Look up the nationalities of the main cast of Gossip Girl (Blake Lively, Penn Badgley, Leighton Meester, Ed Westwick) — find out where they're REALLY from and write it in your notebook in English.

curriculo.tema: Can You Do That? — Abilities, Possibilities & Asking for Help
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verb 'can' — affirmative ('I can speak English'), negative ('I can't drive'), questions ('Can you help me?', 'Can I have…?', 'Can you repeat that?'); short answers: 'Yes, I can / No, I can't'; very basic 'could' for polite requests (receptive + formulaic production: 'Could you help me, please?'); Vocabulary (12 words): speak, understand, help, repeat, write, read, find, show, open, carry, call, need; Survival expressions: 'Can you help me?', 'I can't understand', 'Can you speak slower, please?', 'Can you repeat that?', 'I don't speak French, but I can speak English'; Travel survival focus throughout
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Can you…?' survey: Teacher asks Gabriela a rapid list of questions: 'Can you swim? Can you cook? Can you speak French? Can you play guitar?' She answers in full sentences. Fun, personal, fast. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces 'can' using a printed 'Skill Card' with Gabriela's name. 'Gabriela Pires can speak Portuguese. She can understand some English. She can't speak French.' Grammar is immediate and personal. Introduces can/can't in affirmative, negative, question with clear visual. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Survival Situations in Paris' activity: Teacher presents 5 printed mini-scenarios (lost in the metro, can't find the hotel, doesn't understand the menu). Gabriela must choose the right 'can' sentence to use in each situation. Discuss together. Focus on 'Can you help me?', 'Can you speak English?', 'Can you show me on the map?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Teacher plays a French passerby who speaks very fast. Gabriela must use survival English: 'Excuse me, can you help me? I can't find my hotel. Can you speak slower, please? Can you show me on the map?' Run the role-play 3 times with increasing complexity. Phase 5 (15 min) — Reflection + written output: Gabriela writes 8 'can/can't' sentences: 4 about her actual abilities, 4 survival phrases she would use in France. Read aloud with confidence.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a list in your notebook: 5 things you CAN do in English, 5 things you CAN'T do yet in English, 3 survival phrases using 'can' for your Paris trip. (15 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio scenario: 'I am in Paris and I am lost. I see a person and I ask for help.' Use 'can' at least 5 times. (15 min) | 3) Watch the Friends episode 'The One Where Nobody's Ready' (S03E02) — listen for the word 'can' and count how many times you hear it. Note 3 sentences.

curriculo.tema: Where Is It? — Directions, Locations & Getting Around Paris on Foot
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: prepositions of place (in, on, at, next to, across from, between, in front of, behind, near, far from); imperatives for directions (turn left, go straight, take the first right, walk past); question forms: 'Where is the…?', 'How do I get to…?', 'Is it far?'; Vocabulary (12 words): street, corner, block, turn, left, right, straight, cross, map, station, exit, landmark; Paris-specific vocabulary: metro, arrondissement, boulevard, rue (as receptive — teacher explains it's French for 'street'); Expressions: 'Turn left at the corner', 'It's next to the museum', 'Walk straight for two blocks', 'You can't miss it'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Directional warm-up: Teacher gives rapid direction commands in the classroom. 'Turn left. Walk straight. Stop. Turn right.' Gabriela follows physically — gets her moving and makes language embodied. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher draws a simple hand-drawn map of a Paris neighborhood on paper — names streets, adds landmarks (Eiffel Tower, a café, a metro station, a hotel labeled 'Hotel Pires'). Introduces prepositions of place and direction verbs with arrows on the map. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Find Gabriela's Hotel' map task: Teacher places Gabriela at 'Point A' on the hand-drawn Paris map. Reads aloud a set of directions. Gabriela follows on the map with her finger and marks where she ends up. Then reverses: Gabriela picks a destination and gives teacher directions. 3 rounds each. Phase 4 (20 min) — Outdoor or hallway simulation (if possible): Teacher sets up 4 'landmarks' in the room or hallway using labels (museum, café, hotel, pharmacy). Gabriela gives directions from one to another as if speaking to a tourist. If outdoor not possible, use the drawn map role-play. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes directions from the Eiffel Tower to the Louvre on the hand-drawn Paris map — at least 6 direction sentences. Read aloud.
curriculo.homework: 1) Draw your own simple map of your neighborhood in São Paulo — label at least 6 places in English. Write 5 directions from your home to your school in English. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Excuse me, how do I get to the Eiffel Tower from the Louvre?' — give the full answer yourself using the map from class. (15 min) | 3) Google Maps exercise: search 'walking directions, Paris, France' for any two famous landmarks. Read the directions aloud in English, adapting to your vocabulary.

curriculo.tema: What Do You Look Like? — Describing People, Characters & First Impressions
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: adjectives for physical description — order with 'have/has' and 'to be' ('She has long brown hair', 'He is tall and slim'); third-person singular review in descriptive context; question forms: 'What does she look like?', 'What does he look like?'; basic comparison: 'She is taller than…' (receptive, with limited production); Vocabulary (14 words): tall, short, slim, curvy, young, old, long, short (hair), curly, straight, dark, blonde, blue/brown/green eyes, stylish; Character vocabulary: confident, shy, funny, kind, mean, smart, dramatic; Expressions: 'She looks like…', 'He reminds me of…', 'She has a very strong personality'; Context: Gossip Girl cast + Gabriela's own description
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Guess who?' warm-up: Teacher gives 3 physical descriptions of Gossip Girl characters. Gabriela guesses who it is. Then Gabriela gives a description, teacher guesses. Pure listening + speaking activation. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces descriptive adjectives using printed photos of Gossip Girl cast (Blair, Serena, Chuck, Dan). Creates a simple 2-column chart: LOOK (physical) and PERSONALITY. Models sentences: 'Blair has dark hair. She is elegant and confident. She looks like a queen.' Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Character Profile Cards': Gabriela receives 4 blank cards and fills in descriptions for 4 characters using target vocabulary. She then reads each card aloud while teacher shows the photo — class 'confirms' if description matches. Teacher corrects word order issues gently. Phase 4 (20 min) — Self-description + comparison: Gabriela describes herself in English (appearance + personality) — at least 5 sentences. Then compares herself to a character: 'I have dark hair like Blair. But I'm more relaxed than her. I think I'm more like Rory from Gilmore Girls because…' Phase 5 (15 min) — 'Describe and draw': Teacher describes a mystery fictional character (not from a show Gabriela knows). Gabriela draws the character based on the English description only. Compare and laugh. Reinforces listening comprehension of adjectives.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a full physical + personality description of your favorite character from Gossip Girl OR Friends — at least 8 sentences. Use 'has' for physical features and 'is' for personality. (20 min) | 2) Record a 60-second audio describing yourself in English — appearance and personality. Pretend you're introducing yourself to a new friend in France. (10 min) | 3) Watch 5 minutes of any Gossip Girl episode. Write down 3 adjectives you hear used to describe a person.

curriculo.tema: Let's Eat! — Food, Menus & Ordering at a Restaurant (Paris Simulation)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: countable vs. uncountable nouns (a sandwich / some water / a piece of cake); 'would like' for polite ordering ('I would like…', 'I'd like…'); basic quantity expressions (a cup of, a glass of, a piece of, some, any); question forms in restaurant context: 'Can I have…?', 'What do you recommend?', 'What's in the…?'; Vocabulary (14 words): menu, order, waiter, bill, appetizer, main course, dessert, vegetarian, allergic, recommend, delicious, awful, tip, receipt; Food vocabulary: bread, water, coffee, salad, chicken, pasta, cake, juice; Expressions: 'I'd like the…, please', 'Can I see the menu?', 'What do you recommend?', 'The check, please', 'It's delicious!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Food reaction warm-up: Teacher shows 10 food images. Gabriela reacts in English: 'I love this!', 'I don't like that', 'I've never tried this' (introduce as fixed phrase). Fast, fun, expressive. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates a printed 'Café de Paris Menu' in English with Gabriela Pires as the customer name on the mock reservation. Introduces 'would like' as the polite way to order — explains it's more sophisticated than 'I want' and more common in restaurants. Models full ordering dialogue. Phase 3 (25 min) — Full restaurant simulation: Room is set up as a Parisian café. Teacher plays the waiter (with optional prop — apron or notepad). Gabriela is seated, receives the menu, orders a starter, main, and dessert, asks for recommendations, and asks for the bill. Run 2 full simulations — first with scaffolding (menu visible), second without notes. Focus on natural flow and politeness markers. Phase 4 (15 min) — Difficult moments drill: Teacher introduces 3 common problems: 'I'm allergic to nuts', 'I don't eat meat, I'm vegetarian', 'I ordered the salad, not the pasta.' Gabriela practices each complaint/request in English. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes her ideal 3-course Paris meal with full sentences: 'For my starter, I'd like… For my main course, I'd like… For dessert, I'd like… because it looks delicious.'
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a full restaurant dialogue in your notebook — at least 10 lines total (5 as customer, 5 as waiter). Include: greeting, ordering starter, main, dessert, a question about the menu, and asking for the bill. (25 min) | 2) Record the dialogue as if you're at a Parisian café — say BOTH parts, changing your voice. (15 min) | 3) Watch a YouTube video: 'How to Order Food at a Restaurant in English' (~8 min). Write 3 new expressions you didn't know.

curriculo.tema: Shopping in the City — Buying Clothes, Asking Prices & Making Choices
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'How much does it cost?', 'How much is it?'; this/that/these/those in shopping context (recycled from Aula 3); 'too + adjective' ('It's too expensive', 'It's too small'); 'not + adjective + enough' (receptive); comparative adjectives introduction: 'bigger', 'cheaper', 'nicer'; Vocabulary (14 words): size, price, discount, try on, fit, exchange, receipt, expensive, cheap, brand, style, cash, credit card, sale; Clothing vocabulary: dress, skirt, blouse, jacket, sneakers, accessories; Expressions: 'Can I try this on?', 'Do you have this in a smaller size?', 'It's too expensive', 'I'll take it!', 'Do you accept credit cards?'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Shopping or not?' warm-up: Teacher describes 5 items with prices (€5 souvenir vs. €500 dress). Gabriela reacts: 'That's cheap!', 'That's too expensive!', 'I love it but it's not in my budget!' Gets opinion vocabulary flowing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates a printed 'Paris Boutique' mini-catalog with 6 items, prices in euros, and sizes. Introduces 'how much', 'too expensive', 'try on', comparative adjectives. Models: 'Excuse me, how much is this dress? It's €120. Oh, that's too expensive. Do you have something cheaper?' Phase 3 (25 min) — Shopping role-play simulation: Teacher plays shop assistant in a Parisian boutique. Gabriela browses the 'catalog', picks items, asks prices, tries to negotiate, decides to buy or not. Three complete shopping interactions: 1) souvenir shop, 2) clothing boutique, 3) bookstore. Each builds in complexity. Phase 4 (15 min) — Problem-solving in shops: Teacher introduces 3 scenarios — wrong size, broken item, no cash payment. Gabriela must navigate each in English. 'Excuse me, I think this is broken. Can I exchange it?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a postcard-style note to a friend: 'I went shopping in Paris today! I bought… I didn't buy… because it was too… The best deal was…' (using present simple — 'I buy / I find' as narrative for now)
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a shopping dialogue in your notebook — at least 12 lines. Include: asking for prices, trying things on, asking for a different size, and making a decision to buy or not. (25 min) | 2) Look at an online shop website (in English — ASOS, for example). Find 5 items you like and write their description, size, and price in your notebook using full English sentences. (15 min) | 3) Record a 60-second audio: 'Today I went shopping in Paris. Here's what happened…' Describe a fictional shopping trip using your new vocabulary.

curriculo.tema: What Were You Doing? — Past Continuous & Storytelling About Yesterday in SP
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple — introduction with 'to be' (was/were) as stepping stone; past continuous — 'was/were + verb-ing' ('I was watching TV', 'She was studying'); time expressions: at + time, when, while; contrast: past simple vs. past continuous at sentence level (receptive: 'I was watching TV when my phone rang'); Vocabulary (12 words): yesterday, last night, this morning, ago, earlier, suddenly, then, after that, before, during, interrupted, while; Action verbs review: watch, study, eat, walk, talk, listen, wait, sleep; Expressions: 'Last night I was watching…', 'Yesterday I was studying when…', 'What were you doing at 8pm?'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Where were you?' game: Teacher asks Gabriela about specific times yesterday: 'At 7am, were you sleeping or eating breakfast? At 3pm, were you in school?' Gabriela answers with was/were. Activates the new form in a fun interrogation format. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces past continuous using a printed 'Yesterday's Timeline' with Gabriela's name. 'At 8am, Gabriela was having breakfast. At 10am, she was studying at school. At 9pm, she was watching Gossip Girl.' Visual timeline makes the progressive action concept clear. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Alibi game': Teacher says there was a 'mystery event' (a famous celebrity posted something controversial) and asks Gabriela to prove she wasn't involved by describing exactly what she was doing at each hour yesterday. Gabriela must create a believable past continuous alibi — 6 sentences minimum. Teacher cross-examines. Phase 4 (20 min) — Gossip Girl storyline retelling: Teacher provides 4 printed screenshots (or descriptions) of a Gossip Girl episode scene. Gabriela narrates what each character was doing: 'Blair was walking into school when she saw Serena. Chuck was sitting in his limo. Dan was reading a book.' Production in third person + past continuous. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a 6-8 sentence description of her evening yesterday using past continuous. Read aloud and teacher gives form-focused feedback.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a paragraph (8-10 sentences) about what you were doing at various times yesterday — include morning, afternoon, and evening. Use 'was/were + -ing' throughout. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Let me tell you about my Sunday. At 9am, I was…' Describe your whole day in past continuous. (15 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of Friends and write down 3 sentences where a character describes what they were doing ('I was just…', 'She was talking about…'). Note the exact sentences.

curriculo.tema: What Happened? — Past Simple for Telling Stories & Talking About the Weekend
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple — regular verbs (-ed: watched, studied, visited, walked, talked, listened); irregular verbs (high-frequency list: went, saw, had, ate, drank, met, bought, took, came, said, felt, got); negative: 'didn't + base verb'; questions: 'Did you…?', 'Where did you go?', 'What did you do?'; short answers: 'Yes, I did / No, I didn't'; Vocabulary (14 words): weekend, last week, last year, ago, visit, trip, museum, party, concert, festival, adventure, boring, amazing, unforgettable; Irregular verb list (receptive + active): go→went, see→saw, have→had, eat→ate, buy→bought, meet→met, take→took; Expressions: 'Last weekend I went to…', 'It was amazing!', 'I didn't expect that', 'Have you ever been to…?' (formulaic, receptive)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Did you…?' rapid fire: Teacher asks 10 yes/no questions about last weekend. 'Did you watch a series? Did you go to school? Did you eat pizza?' Gabriela answers with 'Yes, I did / No, I didn't.' Fast and activating. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates two printed 'Story Cards' — Gabriela's weekend and Blair Waldorf's imaginary weekend. Models: 'Last Saturday, Blair went to a fancy party. She wore a beautiful dress. She met a new boy. She didn't like him at first.' Introduces regular and irregular past forms in story context. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'My Weekend Story': Gabriela narrates her actual last weekend — teacher asks guiding questions and Gabriela produces the story. Teacher writes key verbs on paper as she speaks (correctly conjugated) for her to refer to. Goal: 10+ sentences in past simple. Teacher notes errors in past form without interrupting flow, addresses in Phase 5. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Story swap': Teacher narrates a fictional weekend story about Rory Gilmore in 8 sentences. Gabriela listens and answers 5 comprehension questions: 'Where did Rory go? Who did she meet? What did she eat?' Phase 5 (15 min) — Verb form focus: Teacher reviews the irregular verbs from today's story. Gabriela writes them in base + past form on a dedicated 'Verb Wall' page in her notebook. Drills 5 sentences with different irregular verbs.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write a 'Weekend Story' in your notebook — what you did last weekend, in at least 10 past simple sentences. Include: where you went, who you saw, what you ate, what you watched, how you felt. (25 min) | 2) Make a flashcard set (on paper or phone) with 15 irregular verbs: base form on one side, past simple on the other. Quiz yourself. (15 min) | 3) Record a 2-minute audio: 'Let me tell you about my last weekend in São Paulo.' Use at least 8 past simple sentences. No reading — just talk.

curriculo.tema: Have You Ever…? — Experiences, the Present Perfect & Talking About Life So Far
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present perfect — 'have/has + past participle' for life experiences; 'Have you ever…?', 'Yes, I have / No, I haven't'; 'never' with present perfect ('I've never been to France'); contrast with past simple (experience vs. specific time): 'I've been to Rio' vs. 'I went to Rio last year' (awareness level); Vocabulary (12 words): experience, ever, never, already, yet, before, once, twice, amazing, unforgettable, regret, bucket list; Past participles focus: been, seen, eaten, tried, visited, met, traveled, watched, read, heard; Expressions: 'Have you ever been to…?', 'I've never tried…', 'I've always wanted to…', 'It was my first time!', 'I've seen it twice!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Have you ever?' bingo: Teacher gives Gabriela a printed bingo card with 9 experiences ('eaten escargot', 'been to another country', 'met a celebrity', 'tried a new sport', etc.). Teacher asks questions: 'Have you ever…?' Gabriela marks the ones that apply and answers in full sentences. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces present perfect using a printed 'Gabriela's Life Experiences' visual. 'You have studied English. You have watched Gossip Girl. You have never been to France — but you're going in 2027!' Creates emotional hook. Explains form clearly: have + past participle. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Bucket list vs. Done it' activity: Gabriela creates two columns in her notebook — 'I've already done this' and 'I've never done this but I want to.' She writes 6 items per column using present perfect. Shares with teacher. Teacher prompts follow-up questions: 'Have you ever tried sushi? How many times?' Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Gabriela meets a French student at a cultural exchange event. Teacher plays the student: 'Have you ever been to France? Have you ever eaten a croissant? Have you ever watched a French film?' Gabriela answers and asks back. 8-exchange dialogue. Focus on natural rhythm of question + answer. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes her 'English Experience List' — 8 present perfect sentences: 5 things she has done, 3 things she has never done but wants to. Read aloud with pronunciation focus.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write 10 'Have you ever' questions you would ask a new French friend — then answer all 10 yourself. Use present perfect throughout. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'Things I've done and things I've never done.' Include at least 6 present perfect sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch the 'The One Where Everyone Finds Out' episode of Friends (S05E14). Count how many times you hear 'have' + past participle (e.g., 'I've told you', 'I haven't seen'). Write 3 examples.

curriculo.tema: Tell Me About Your Plans! — Future with 'Going To' & Talking About the Paris Trip
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: future with 'going to' — affirmative ('I'm going to visit Paris'), negative ('I'm not going to miss the Eiffel Tower'), questions ('What are you going to do in France?'); present continuous for future arrangements (receptive awareness: 'I'm flying to Paris on February 3rd'); time expressions: next year, in 2027, this summer, soon, in + month; Vocabulary (12 words): plan, trip, flight, hotel, book, visit, sightseeing, museum, tour, passport, luggage, itinerary; Paris-specific vocabulary: Eiffel Tower, the Louvre, Notre-Dame, Montmartre, croissant, boulangerie, arrondissement; Expressions: 'I'm going to…', 'We're planning to…', 'I can't wait to…', 'It's going to be amazing!', 'I'm so excited about…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Dream trip' activation: Teacher asks in English: 'Close your eyes. You're in Paris. What do you see? What do you do first?' Gabriela responds freely. Teacher notes vocabulary she doesn't know and teaches those 3-4 words immediately — just-in-time vocabulary learning. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher creates 'Gabriela Pires — Paris Trip 2027' planned itinerary and introduces 'going to' in context. 'On February 3rd, Gabriela is going to fly from São Paulo to Paris. On February 4th, she's going to visit the Louvre. She's not going to waste a single day!' Every example uses HER name and HER trip. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'Plan your Paris week': Gabriela creates her own 5-day Paris itinerary in English using 'going to' — at least 2 activities per day. Shares with teacher as an oral presentation: 'On Monday, I'm going to visit Montmartre in the morning. In the afternoon, I'm going to eat at a French café.' Teacher asks follow-up questions to extend production. Phase 4 (20 min) — Role-play: Gabriela calls a friend (teacher) to tell them about her Paris plans. 'Guess what? I'm going to go to Paris next year! I'm going to stay for 5 days. I'm going to visit…' Friend asks questions, expresses jealousy, asks for recommendations. Natural conversation flow. Phase 5 (10 min) — Written consolidation: Gabriela writes 8 'going to' sentences about her Paris trip — 6 positive, 2 negative ('I'm not going to forget my camera!'). Read aloud.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write your complete Paris Trip Plan in English — 7 days, at least 2 activities per day with full 'going to' sentences. Include: transport, accommodation, food, sightseeing, shopping. (30 min) | 2) Record a 2-minute audio: 'Let me tell you about my plans for Paris 2027!' Use 'going to' at least 10 times. Speak with excitement — this is YOUR trip! (15 min) | 3) Watch the trailer of Notting Hill on YouTube (in English) and write 3 sentences about what the characters are going to do, based on what you see.

curriculo.tema: Emergency Mode — Health Vocabulary, Asking for Help & Staying Safe Abroad
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'to feel' + adjective for health ('I feel sick', 'I feel dizzy'); 'have' for symptoms ('I have a headache', 'I have a fever'); 'need' + noun/infinitive ('I need a doctor', 'I need to sit down'); modal 'should' introduction (formulaic: 'You should see a doctor', 'You should rest'); imperatives for emergency ('Call an ambulance!', 'Don't move!'); Vocabulary (14 words): sick, headache, fever, dizzy, hurt, pain, allergic, doctor, hospital, pharmacy, emergency, medicine, insurance, lost; Expressions: 'I don't feel well', 'I need help', 'I'm allergic to…', 'Please call a doctor', 'Where's the nearest pharmacy?', 'I have travel insurance'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'How do you feel?' charades: Teacher mimes symptoms (dizzy, sick, in pain, tired, hungry). Gabriela guesses in English: 'You feel sick! You have a headache!' Then reverses. Embodied learning — makes health vocabulary physical and memorable. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces health vocabulary through a printed 'Emergency Card' titled 'Gabriela Pires — Emergency Information' with key sentences she'd need in France. Models each sentence aloud with clear pronunciation. Explains that this is one of the most important vocabulary sets for solo travel. Phase 3 (25 min) — Emergency simulation scenarios: 3 printed scenario cards. Scenario 1: Gabriela feels sick in a Parisian café — talks to the waiter. Scenario 2: She needs to find a pharmacy — asks a passerby for directions + explains symptoms. Scenario 3: She has a headache and goes to a pharmacy to buy medicine — speaks to the pharmacist. Each role-play runs for 4-5 minutes with teacher playing the other person. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'What should you do?' discussion: Teacher presents 5 situations (lost phone, fever, allergic reaction, missed flight, stolen wallet). Gabriela responds: 'I should call the embassy', 'I should go to the hospital', 'I should contact my travel insurance.' Introduces 'should' in a purposeful, high-stakes context. Phase 5 (15 min) — Emergency card creation: Gabriela creates her own 'Emergency Card' in English — her name, nationality, any allergies, emergency contact, insurance number (fictional). She writes it and reads it aloud.
curriculo.homework: 1) Create your personal 'Travel Emergency Card' in English in your notebook — include: name, nationality, blood type, allergies, emergency phrases, and 5 sentences for different emergency situations. (25 min) | 2) Record a 90-second role-play audio: 'I'm in a pharmacy in Paris. I don't feel well. Let me explain my symptoms.' Play both roles. (15 min) | 3) Research (in English): 'What to do if you get sick abroad?' Find 3 tips online and write them in your notebook in your own words.

curriculo.tema: Let's Compare! — Comparative & Superlative Adjectives Through Culture & Travel
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: comparative adjectives — short adjectives + -er ('bigger', 'cheaper', 'nicer', 'older'); 'more + long adjective' ('more expensive', 'more beautiful', 'more interesting'); 'than' in comparisons ('Paris is more romantic than São Paulo'); superlative adjectives — 'the + -est' ('the biggest', 'the most famous', 'the best', 'the worst'); irregular: good→better→best, bad→worse→worst; Vocabulary (12 words): famous, popular, crowded, expensive, modern, ancient, romantic, exciting, dangerous, peaceful, cosmopolitan, iconic; Expressions: 'In my opinion…', 'I think… is better than…', 'The most beautiful city I know is…', 'She's the best character in the show'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Which is better?' rapid reactions: Teacher shows pairs of images (São Paulo vs. Paris; Blair vs. Serena; Friends vs. Gossip Girl; Brazilian food vs. French food). Gabriela reacts immediately: 'Paris is more beautiful than São Paulo' / 'Blair is more dramatic than Serena.' Gets comparatives flowing without grammar explanation first. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces comparative and superlative structure using a printed chart with Gabriela's own comparisons from Phase 1. Adds the grammar labels AFTER she's already using the forms. Focuses on short vs. long adjective rule and irregular forms. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'São Paulo vs. Paris vs. New York' comparison activity: Teacher provides a printed fact sheet with 3 cities (population, famous landmarks, food scene, weather, cost of living). Gabriela writes 8 comparison sentences using comparatives and superlatives. Reads aloud and debates with teacher. Phase 4 (20 min) — Opinion debate: Teacher asks comparative opinion questions: 'Is Rory Gilmore a better character than Blair Waldorf? Is Gossip Girl more interesting than Friends? Is Paris more romantic than London?' Gabriela must defend opinions with complete sentences using comparatives. Teacher pushes back to generate more production. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a 'My Favorites Comparison List' — 6 comparative sentences and 3 superlative sentences about things she loves. Example: 'Friends is funnier than Gossip Girl, but Gossip Girl is more dramatic. The best character in Friends is…'
curriculo.homework: 1) Write 10 comparison sentences about cities, shows, food, or celebrities you know — use 5 comparatives and 5 superlatives. (20 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'São Paulo vs. Paris — a comparison.' Compare the two cities using at least 6 comparative and superlative sentences. (15 min) | 3) Watch 10 minutes of any travel vlog on YouTube about Paris (in English). Write down 3 comparison sentences you hear (e.g., 'Paris is more…', 'The best…').

curriculo.tema: Making Plans & Inviting People — Social English for International Friendships
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'Would you like to…?' for invitations; 'Let's + verb' for suggestions ('Let's go to the museum!'); 'What about + noun/-ing?' and 'How about + noun/-ing?' for alternatives; accepting: 'That sounds great!', 'I'd love to!'; declining politely: 'I'd love to but…', 'Maybe next time', 'I can't, I have to…'; 'have to' introduction (obligation): 'I have to be back by 10'; Vocabulary (12 words): invitation, suggestion, plan, meet, hang out, free, available, busy, cancel, confirm, venue, appointment; Expressions: 'Are you free this weekend?', 'Would you like to grab a coffee?', 'What are you doing tonight?', 'Let's meet at the Eiffel Tower at noon!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Accept or decline?' game: Teacher makes rapid invitations. 'Would you like to eat at McDonald's?' / 'Would you like to visit a museum at 6am?' / 'Would you like to get coffee?' Gabriela accepts or declines with full natural sentences. Immediate and energizing. Phase 2 (15 min) — Presentation: Teacher introduces invitation and suggestion structures using a printed 'Weekend in Paris' scenario. Models: 'Would you like to visit the Louvre tomorrow? What about lunch at a café near the Seine? Let's take a boat tour!' Introduces accepting/declining responses in pairs. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'Paris social weekend' role-play: Gabriela plays herself as a traveler in Paris. Teacher plays 3 different characters: a French student she met at the hostel, a Brazilian traveler she bumped into, and a local guide. Each character extends an invitation or suggestion. Gabriela must respond appropriately — accept, decline with reason, counter-suggest. 3 full mini-dialogues. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Making plans by message': Teacher gives Gabriela a printed 'fake WhatsApp exchange' in English between two travelers making plans. Gabriela reads aloud, then creates her own 8-line message exchange with a fictional French friend making plans for the next day in Paris. Phase 5 (15 min) — Written output: Gabriela writes a social invitation email (4-6 sentences) to a fictional French classmate: 'Hey! Would you like to…? We could… What about…? Let me know if you're free!' Uses all target structures.
curriculo.homework: 1) Write 3 different invitation scenarios in your notebook: one accepted, one declined politely, one with a counter-suggestion. Each scenario should have at least 6 exchanges. (25 min) | 2) Record a 90-second audio: 'I'm going to invite my new French friend to spend the day with me in Paris.' Plan the full invitation conversation and say it aloud. (15 min) | 3) Watch a Friends episode focused on social plans (e.g., 'The One with the Prom Video' S02E14). Note 3 expressions characters use to make or cancel plans.

curriculo.tema: Block 1 Review & Progress Check — Gabriela's Mid-Journey Milestone Celebration
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Review of ALL key structures from Aulas 1-19: to be (present + past), present simple, past simple, past continuous, present perfect, going to future, can/can't, comparatives/superlatives, modals (should, would like, have to), prepositions, articles; Vocabulary review: 120+ words introduced across 19 aulas — recycled through communicative tasks; Self-assessment focus: What can Gabriela do now that she couldn't do in Aula 1? What still needs work? Progress measurement against Paris 2027 goals; Expressions recycled: all key expressions from restaurant, directions, shopping, health, social plans
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — 'Remember this?' warm-up quiz: Teacher reads out 10 sentences with gaps from across the 19 aulas. Gabriela fills in from memory. Not graded — just diagnostic. Fun, light, confidence-building. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Paris Survival Mega Role-Play': A 20-minute immersive simulation that chains together 5 mini-scenarios from the block: arriving at the hotel (greetings, to be, spelling name) → asking for directions to a restaurant → ordering a 3-course meal → shopping for a souvenir → asking for help when she feels unwell. Teacher plays all characters. Gabriela stays in English throughout. Teacher notes what flows naturally and what breaks down. This is the primary assessment tool. Phase 3 (15 min) — Progress comparison: Teacher plays back (or reads aloud) Gabriela's Aula 1 baseline audio description. Then Gabriela records a NEW 90-second self-introduction in English. Together, they compare: vocabulary range, sentence complexity, confidence, L1 switches. Celebrate concrete progress. Phase 4 (20 min) — 'Vocabulary Victory Lap': Gabriela has 10 minutes to create a personal 'vocabulary map' on paper — all the topic areas covered (greetings, food, directions, health, travel) with her 5 favorite words per area. Then reads her map aloud to teacher. This is her personalized vocabulary reference for Block 2. Phase 5 (15 min) — Goal reset + Block 2 preview: Teacher and Gabriela review the Victory Wall from Aula 1. What has she already achieved? Teacher previews Block 2 themes (storytelling, opinions, media, intercâmbio language, more Paris prep). Gabriela writes one personal goal for Block 2 in English: 'In the next 16 lessons, I want to…' Ends with a celebration moment — Gabriela's choice (favorite scene from a series, a fun English game).
curriculo.homework: 1) Re-record your Aula 1 baseline audio — 2 full minutes this time. Talk about: who you are, your life in São Paulo, your hobbies, your plans for Paris, things you've done, what you're going to do. Compare it to your original recording and write 3 differences you notice. (25 min) | 2) Create your 'Block 1 Vocabulary Book' — go through your notebook from Aulas 1-19 and write all new words you learned organized by theme on a new clean page. Aim for 80+ words. (30 min) | 3) Choose your favorite English series moment from the past weeks and watch it again — this time WITHOUT any subtitles. Write what you understood (even if partial). Celebrate how far you've come!

curriculo.tema: Will You or Won't You? — Future with 'Will' for Predictions, Promises & Spontaneous Decisions About Life After High School
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: future with 'will' — affirmative ('I will study abroad'), negative ('I won't stay home'), yes/no questions ('Will you travel in 2027?'), short answers ('Yes, I will / No, I won't'); contrasting 'will' vs. 'going to' — spontaneous vs. planned decisions; vocabulary: probably, maybe, definitely, I think, I hope, I'm sure, I doubt, I promise; topic vocab: exchange program, gap year, scholarship, abroad, apply, enroll, deadline, semester; expressions: 'I'll call you back', 'I'll take it!', 'I think it will be amazing', 'I promise I'll practice every day'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows Gabriela a timeline of her life from now to 2027 (school graduation → Paris trip → possible exchange) and asks 'What do you think WILL happen?' She makes 5 spontaneous predictions using 'will'. Presentation (15 min): Contrast 'going to' (from Aula 16) vs. 'will' using real scenarios — 'I'm going to visit the Eiffel Tower' (planned) vs. 'I think the weather will be cold in February' (prediction). PPT/whiteboard with 3 categories: predictions, promises, spontaneous decisions. Practice (20 min): Drilling round — teacher says a situation card, Gabriela responds with 'will'. Ex: 'Your phone is ringing!' → 'I'll answer it!'; 'Someone is cold in Paris' → 'I'll get a jacket!'. Then gap-fill exercise: complete 10 sentences about Gabriela's Paris trip using will/won't. Production (20 min): Role-play — Gabriela is on the phone with her future host family in France. Teacher plays the French host mom asking about Gabriela's plans, habits, and what she will/won't do during the stay. Gabriela must use at least 8 'will' structures naturally across the conversation. Debrief (10 min): Discuss the difference will/going to — spot-check 3 sentences the student produced and correct together.
curriculo.homework: Write a short paragraph (8–10 sentences) titled 'My 2027 Predictions' — what Gabriela thinks will and won't happen during the Paris trip. Use: will, won't, probably, maybe, I think, I hope. Also, record a 90-second audio: 'A message to my future host family' — introduce yourself and say 3 things you will do and 2 things you won't do during the stay. Send the recording. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

curriculo.tema: At the Airport — Check-In, Security, Gates & Surviving Gabriela's First International Flight
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: review of modal verbs 'can/can't' (Aula 8) + new: 'may I…?' and 'could you…?' for polite airport requests; imperative for instructions ('Please remove your shoes', 'Place your bag on the belt'); vocabulary: passport, boarding pass, check-in counter, carry-on luggage, checked baggage, gate, departure, arrival, terminal, security checkpoint, customs, declaration form, flight attendant, overhead bin, seat belt, window seat, aisle seat, delay, on time; expressions: 'May I see your passport?', 'Could you open your bag, please?', 'Where is gate B12?', 'Is this seat taken?', 'My flight is delayed', 'How long is the layover?', 'I have nothing to declare'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher lays out airport props on the desk — printed boarding pass with Gabriela Pires's name, a 'passport', a luggage tag — and asks 'Where are you right now? What do you need to do?' Eliciting vocabulary. Presentation (15 min): Airport journey map on the board — each step labeled: arrive → check in → drop luggage → security → find gate → board. Introduce vocabulary at each stage with pronunciation drilling. Focus on polite request forms: 'May I…?' vs. 'Could you…?' Practice (20 min): Listening simulation — teacher reads 3 airport announcements aloud (gate change, boarding call, delay announcement) and Gabriela must answer comprehension questions. Then, written practice: fill in a simulated check-in form as Gabriela Pires (name, passport number, destination: Paris CDG, seat preference). Production (25 min): Full airport simulation role-play in 3 rounds — (1) check-in desk: teacher as agent, Gabriela checks in her bag and gets boarding pass; (2) security checkpoint: answer questions about items in her bag; (3) gate interaction: ask a fellow traveler about the gate and chat about the destination. Debrief: identify 2 moments where she hesitated and rehearse those lines again.
curriculo.homework: Listen to 2 real airport announcement recordings on YouTube (search 'airport announcements English') and write down 5 sentences you understood. Also, practice saying the full check-in dialogue alone at home and record yourself playing BOTH roles — agent and passenger Gabriela Pires. Recording should be 2–3 minutes. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

curriculo.tema: On the Plane & Arriving in Paris — Talking to Flight Attendants, Customs & Your First Moments in France
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'would like' for polite requests on the plane (review from Aula 11) + 'could you' + infinitive; present continuous for current actions ('We are landing in 20 minutes', 'The captain is speaking now'); vocabulary: turbulence, life jacket, emergency exit, tray table, recline, headphones, landing, customs officer, immigration, visa, purpose of visit, length of stay, I'm here as a tourist, accommodation; expressions: 'Could you bring me a blanket, please?', 'Excuse me, is there a vegetarian option?', 'I'm here for tourism', 'I'll be staying for 10 days', 'I'm staying at a hotel in Paris', 'Do I need to fill in a form?'; cultural note: difference between Schengen entry and non-EU passport protocols
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Recap of airport vocabulary from Aula 22 — quick flashcard quiz: teacher holds up a card, Gabriela says the word + a sentence. 5 words reviewed. Presentation (15 min): New scenario — inside the plane and at the immigration counter. Teacher presents vocabulary with a visual 'flight map' (boarding → takeoff → in-flight → landing → customs → exit). Pronunciation focus: 'turbulence', 'accommodation', 'immigration'. Practice (20 min): Dialogue completion — Gabriela receives a printed dialogue between a passenger and flight attendant with 6 blanks; she fills in using the target vocabulary, then reads it aloud with expression. Second activity: teacher reads a customs officer script and Gabriela answers as herself (purpose of visit, length of stay, where she's staying). Production (25 min): Two-part role-play. Part 1 — Gabriela is on the plane and needs to: ask for water, report her tray table is broken, ask what time they land. Part 2 — immigration at CDG airport: customs officer (teacher) asks all standard questions, Gabriela answers naturally using real information about her trip. Coach pacing and confidence. Debrief: highlight 2 strong answers and 1 area to improve.
curriculo.homework: Write a journal entry (8–10 sentences) as Gabriela on her first night in Paris — 'Today I arrived in Paris. My flight was…' Use: past simple + present perfect ('I have finally arrived!'). Also, find one YouTube video of a real immigration interview in English and note 3 questions the officer asked. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Milestone Check-In — Gabriela at the Halfway Point: What She Knows, What She Can Do & Where She's Going
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Review + integration of ALL major structures from Aulas 1–23: to be (present/past), present simple, past simple, present perfect, present continuous, future (going to + will), modals (can, could, would, may), comparatives/superlatives, prepositions, demonstratives, articles; vocabulary review across all topics: personal info, travel, health, food, shopping, directions, emotions, invitations, airport, Paris; self-assessment language: 'I can…', 'I'm still working on…', 'I need more practice with…', 'I feel confident when…', 'I get confused when…'; expressions for reflecting: 'Looking back…', 'I've improved in…', 'My goal is to…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher reads aloud 10 sentences from previous aulas (one from each major topic) — Gabriela rates her confidence on each from 1–5. This creates a live heat map of strengths and gaps. Presentation/Review (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Gabriela's Journey' visual timeline (Aulas 1–23) — each grammar point marked. Together they identify: what was easy, what was hard, what needs recycling. Practice (25 min): Integrated assessment — 3 tasks: (1) Oral task: Gabriela introduces herself fully in English — name, city, school, hobbies, future plans, Paris trip — without stopping for 90 seconds; (2) Grammar spot-check: 10 written sentences with targeted errors for her to correct; (3) Vocabulary recall: 15 flashcards from aulas 1–23, Gabriela defines or uses in a sentence. Production (20 min): Gabriela records a 'Progress Report Video' (teacher films on phone or she does at home): she speaks directly to camera in English for 2 minutes — what she has learned, what she can do now that she couldn't do in Aula 1, and what she wants to achieve by Aula 48. Debrief (10 min): Celebrate concrete wins; set 3 personal targets for Block 3.
curriculo.homework: Watch your own recording from Aula 1 (if available) and compare it to today's recording. Write 5 sentences in English: 'In Aula 1, I couldn't… but now I can…'. Also, review your personal vocabulary notebook and mark 10 words you feel 100% confident with and 5 you want to practice more. Estimated time: 25–30 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Let's Talk About Series! — Describing TV Shows, Giving Opinions & Using Gossip Girl and Friends as Grammar Tools
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for habitual actions + adverbs of frequency (review) + NEW: relative clauses with 'who' and 'that' for describing characters — 'Blair is a character who is very manipulative', 'Friends is a show that takes place in New York'; opinion language: 'I think…', 'In my opinion…', 'I believe…', 'I feel like…', 'What I love about it is…'; vocabulary: episode, season, plot, character, storyline, cliffhanger, binge-watch, streaming, drama, romantic subplot, villain, protagonist, supporting character, dialogue, scene, spin-off; expressions: 'Have you seen…?', 'You should watch it!', 'It's about…', 'The main character is…', 'It gets better after season 2', 'I've been watching it non-stop'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows stills from Gossip Girl, Friends, and Gilmore Girls (printed or shown on screen) and asks 'Tell me everything you know about this show — IN ENGLISH.' Gabriela free-talks for 2 minutes while teacher notes vocabulary gaps to address. Presentation (15 min): Introduce relative clauses with 'who' and 'that' using real show descriptions. Build on board: 'Gossip Girl is a show THAT follows the lives of wealthy teenagers in New York. Blair Waldorf is a character WHO is ambitious and fashionable.' Gabriela underlines the relative pronoun and the clause it introduces. Practice (20 min): Guided writing + speaking — Gabriela writes 6 sentences describing characters from her favorite shows using 'who' and 'that'. Then oral drill: teacher says a character name, Gabriela describes them with a relative clause in under 5 seconds. Round 2: opinion marathon — teacher asks 'What do you think of Serena van der Woodsen?' and Gabriela must answer with a full opinion sentence using target structures. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is recommending Gossip Girl to a new French friend she met in Paris who doesn't know the show. She must: describe the show (what it's about), introduce 3 characters using relative clauses, give her opinion, and convince her friend to watch it. Teacher plays the skeptical French friend. Debrief: review 3 relative clauses she produced and fine-tune.
curriculo.homework: Choose ONE episode of Friends or Gossip Girl (any episode in English with subtitles). Watch 10 minutes. Write a mini-review (6–8 sentences): describe the plot, one character using a relative clause ('who' or 'that'), and give your opinion. Also, make a list of 5 new English words you heard in the episode. Estimated time: 45–55 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Getting Around Paris — Metro, Maps, Bus & Asking for Directions Like a Local
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: imperative for giving directions (review from Aula 9) + NEW: 'You need to…', 'You should…', 'You have to…' for advice and instructions in navigation contexts; prepositions of movement: through, past, along, across, toward, away from; vocabulary: metro line, platform, ticket machine, single ticket, monthly pass, timetable, bus stop, transfer, interchange, exit, north/south/east/west, crosswalk, roundabout, landmark, map, neighborhood — Marais, Montmartre, Saint-Germain; expressions: 'How do I get to…?', 'Which line do I take?', 'Where do I change trains?', 'Is it far from here?', 'It's about a 10-minute walk', 'Get off at…', 'Take line 4 toward…', 'You can't miss it'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed Paris metro map on the table and asks Gabriela to find 5 famous landmarks she already knows (Eiffel Tower, Louvre, Notre-Dame, etc.) — in English only. Presentation (15 min): Introduce prepositions of movement with physical demonstration in the classroom — teacher walks 'through' the door, 'past' the desk, 'across' the room, narrating each move. Then: 'You should take line 1. You need to get off at Châtelet. Walk past the fountain and turn left.' Practice (20 min): Map drill — teacher gives Gabriela a 'You are here' starting point on the Paris map and a destination. She must give directions using imperatives + 'you need to / you should'. Two rounds with different starting points. Then: gap-fill dialogue between a tourist and a Parisian — complete 8 lines with direction vocabulary. Production (25 min): Full role-play simulation. Teacher sets up two chairs as 'metro seats'. Gabriela has just arrived at Gare du Nord and needs to get to the Eiffel Tower. Teacher plays: (1) a metro ticket agent — she must buy the right ticket; (2) a local Parisian she stops on the street for directions. Gabriela navigates both interactions without switching to Portuguese. Debrief: map any confusion about prepositions of movement.
curriculo.homework: Using Google Maps (or a printed Paris map), plan a route from the Eiffel Tower to Montmartre. Write the directions in English (6–8 steps) using imperatives and 'you need to / you should'. Then, record yourself giving those directions aloud as if you're explaining to a friend — aim for 60–90 seconds. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Talking About Feelings & Emotions — How Gabriela Feels About School, Travel & Life in a New Place
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'feel + adjective' (review from Aula 17) + NEW: 'I feel like + noun/gerund' ('I feel like crying', 'I feel like a stranger'); cause and effect with 'because', 'so', 'that's why'; linking words: although, however, but, on the other hand; vocabulary: excited, nervous, homesick, overwhelmed, relieved, proud, embarrassed, frustrated, confident, lonely, curious, anxious, grateful, nostalgic, motivated; expressions: 'I'm really excited about…', 'To be honest, I feel nervous when…', 'That makes me feel…', 'I feel more confident now', 'I was overwhelmed at first, but…', 'I miss home, but I love being here'; cultural vocabulary: culture shock, adjustment period, comfort zone, homesickness
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 10 'emotion cards' face-down on the table (each with an adjective). Gabriela picks one, acts it out without words — teacher guesses. Then switch. Vocabulary activated playfully. Presentation (15 min): Introduce the new emotion vocabulary in context: 'Imagine you're in Paris on day 1. You're at the hotel alone. How do you feel? Why?' Build sentences on the board: 'I feel overwhelmed because everything is in French. However, I'm excited because it's my dream.' Introduce linking words as connectors between emotions. Practice (20 min): Speaking chain — teacher gives a scenario card ('Your flight was delayed 4 hours'), Gabriela makes 3 sentences: how she feels + why + what she will do. 5 scenarios total. Written task: Gabriela fills in a 'Feelings Diary' — 5 situations from her life (school exam, talking in English, watching a new series) matched with 3 emotion words each + a sentence explaining why. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela has been in Paris for 3 days and video-calls her mom in São Paulo. Teacher plays the mom asking: 'How are you feeling? Are you homesick? Is it hard?' Gabriela must respond with full emotion sentences using linking words — no single-word answers. Debrief: celebrate emotional vocabulary range; highlight 2 especially strong sentences.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'Paris Day 3 Diary Entry' (10–12 sentences) in English — describe how you feel about being in Paris, what makes you nervous and what makes you excited, using emotion vocabulary and linking words (because, although, however). Also, look up one English song that talks about emotions and write down 3 lyrics that include an emotion word. Estimated time: 35–40 minutes.

curriculo.tema: At the Hotel — Checking In, Asking for Help & Handling Room Problems Like a Confident Guest
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'There is / There are' (affirmative, negative, questions) for describing room amenities; 'I'd like to…' + 'Could you…?' for polite requests; reporting a problem: 'The [noun] isn't working', 'There's no…', 'I think there's a problem with…'; vocabulary: reception desk, receptionist, reservation, room key, check-in/check-out, floor, elevator, single/double room, suite, Wi-Fi password, room service, minibar, air conditioning, heating, shower, towels, pillow, wake-up call, lobby, valet; expressions: 'I have a reservation under the name Pires', 'Could I have a room with a view?', 'The air conditioning isn't working', 'Could you send someone to fix it?', 'What time is breakfast?', 'Is there a safe in the room?', 'Can I have a late check-out?'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 8 small hotel-related objects or picture cards on the desk (towel image, key card, Wi-Fi sign, etc.) and Gabriela names each one and makes a sentence: 'There is a key card on the desk.' Quick-fire round — 60 seconds. Presentation (15 min): Hotel check-in dialogue on the board — full model conversation between Gabriela Pires and a Paris hotel receptionist. Teacher reads both roles with exaggerated politeness to show register. Highlight: 'There is/are', polite requests, problem-reporting structure. Practice (20 min): Gabriela rewrites 5 'rude' sentences into polite hotel English: 'Give me a towel' → 'Could you bring me a towel, please?' Then, gap-fill hotel dialogue — she completes a check-in script for a 4-star Paris hotel, including her name, reservation details, room preference, and one complaint. Production (25 min): Three-round hotel simulation — Teacher as hotel receptionist: Round 1: smooth check-in (Gabriela has a reservation, everything works); Round 2: problem scenario — the room smells, the Wi-Fi doesn't work, there are no towels; Round 3: check-out — pay the bill, ask for a receipt, request a taxi to the airport. Debrief: note 3 polite phrases that sounded natural and 1 area to polish.
curriculo.homework: Write 2 short emails (5–6 sentences each): Email 1 — before the trip, confirming your hotel reservation as Gabriela Pires. Email 2 — a complaint to the hotel after your stay about a problem with your room. Use 'There was no…', 'The [noun] wasn't working', 'I would like to…'. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Eating Out in Paris — Full Restaurant Experience: Menus, Orders, Allergies & Paying the Bill
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: 'would like' vs. 'want' in restaurant register (review + expansion); 'Could I have…?' / 'I'll have…' for ordering; quantifiers with food: some, a piece of, a glass of, a bottle of, a slice of (review countable/uncountable from Aula 11) + NEW: too much / too many / not enough; vocabulary: appetizer, main course, dessert, side dish, the specials, sparkling/still water, medium/well-done/rare (steak), vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, allergy, bill, tip, receipt, service charge, split the bill; expressions: 'I'm allergic to…', 'Does this contain…?', 'Could we have the menu, please?', 'I'll have the salmon, please', 'Could I get this without onions?', 'Can we get the check?', 'Is service included?', 'It was delicious, thank you!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher hands Gabriela a printed French restaurant menu (in English) — she has 2 minutes to choose a starter, main, and dessert and tell the teacher her choices with reasons: 'I'd like the French onion soup because…' Presentation (15 min): Expand on Aula 11's restaurant vocab with the full dining experience — from arrival to payment. Introduce 'too much / too many / not enough' with food context: 'There's too much salt', 'There aren't enough vegetables', 'I don't have enough money for the dessert'. Practice (20 min): Roleplay script reading — teacher and Gabriela do a waiter/customer dialogue from a printed script, switching roles. Focus on intonation and politeness. Then: allergy scenario drill — teacher announces 'You're lactose intolerant!' and reads 5 menu items; Gabriela must ask about each one: 'Does this contain dairy?' Production (25 min): Full restaurant simulation — teacher sets the scene (2 chairs, printed menu, pretend bill). Gabriela is dining with a French classmate (teacher plays both the friend and the waiter). She must: greet the waiter, order a full meal, handle an issue (wrong dish brought), ask about an allergy, and pay the bill. Debrief: role-play 'what went well' + replay 1 awkward moment more naturally.
curriculo.homework: Find a real Paris restaurant menu online (many have English versions) and write your ideal 3-course meal order as a complete dialogue — from greeting the waiter to paying the bill (10–12 lines). Record yourself speaking the customer's lines aloud. Also, write 3 sentences using 'too much/too many/not enough' about food from your day. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Visiting Museums & Monuments — The Louvre, Versailles & Talking About Art, History & Beauty in English
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past simple for narrating visits + present simple for general facts about places ('The Louvre is the world's largest museum', 'Napoleon built Versailles'); NEW: passive voice — simple introduction: 'The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci', 'The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889', 'It was designed by Gustave Eiffel'; vocabulary: painting, sculpture, gallery, exhibition, guided tour, audio guide, admission fee, masterpiece, artifact, portrait, landscape, architecture, medieval, Renaissance, Impressionism; expressions: 'It was absolutely breathtaking', 'I was amazed by…', 'Did you know that…?', 'The most impressive part was…', 'I can't believe how old it is!', 'It took my breath away'; cultural note: major Paris landmarks + brief context for each
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 images of Paris landmarks/artworks (Mona Lisa, Eiffel Tower, Versailles, Notre-Dame, Arc de Triomphe) and asks 'What do you know about this? Tell me ONE thing — in English!' Gabriela free-talks, teacher notes gaps. Presentation (15 min): Introduce the passive voice with the 5 images: 'The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889. It was designed by Gustave Eiffel. It was originally intended to be temporary.' Keep it accessible — no full passive grammar lesson, just the was/were + past participle pattern in context. Contrast with active: 'Gustave Eiffel designed it' → 'It was designed by Gustave Eiffel.' Practice (20 min): Sentence transformation — 10 active sentences about Paris monuments → Gabriela rewrites in passive. Then, oral quiz: teacher gives a passive sentence with a blank ('The Mona Lisa was painted by ___') and Gabriela completes it. 8 questions. Cultural knowledge + grammar combo. Production (25 min): Gabriela is a tour guide leading a group of Brazilian tourists through the Louvre. Teacher plays a clueless tourist asking basic questions ('Who painted this?', 'When was this built?', 'Why is it famous?'). Gabriela must answer using both active and passive structures confidently. Finish by having her describe her 'favorite thing she saw today' in 5 sentences. Debrief: coach 2 passive constructions that sounded unnatural.
curriculo.homework: Choose ONE Paris landmark or artwork. Research 5 facts about it in English. Write a 'Museum Card' description (8–10 sentences) using at least 4 passive voice sentences ('It was built in…', 'It was designed by…', 'It is visited by…'). Also, record yourself reading it aloud as if you're the museum audio guide. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Making Friends Abroad — Starting Conversations, Small Talk & Keeping a Friendship Going in English
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: question formation review — all tenses (present simple, past simple, present perfect, future) in question form; question tags for natural conversation: 'It's beautiful here, isn't it?', 'You've been to France before, haven't you?', 'You're not from here, are you?'; vocabulary: small talk, break the ice, common ground, get along (with), have a lot in common, keep in touch, follow on Instagram, exchange numbers, hang out, casual conversation; expressions: 'What brings you here?', 'How long have you been in Paris?', 'Where are you from originally?', 'Have you been to the Louvre yet?', 'We should hang out again!', 'Let's keep in touch!', 'I'll follow you on Instagram'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): 'Small talk blitz' — teacher and Gabriela stand up, teacher pretends to be a stranger at a Parisian café. Without preparation, Gabriela must start a conversation and keep it going for 90 seconds. Teacher notes where it breaks down. Presentation (15 min): Introduce question tags as 'conversation glue' — they keep dialogue flowing and show engagement. Board: affirmative + negative tag / negative + affirmative tag. Examples from Gossip Girl / Friends contexts: 'Blair is so dramatic, isn't she?', 'They haven't seen each other in years, have they?' Practice (20 min): Written question tag exercise (10 sentences to complete) — then oral practice: teacher makes 8 statements about Paris / the trip / series, Gabriela adds the correct question tag without hesitation. Then: 'Small talk bingo' — teacher gives Gabriela a bingo card with small talk topics (weather, hometown, first time in Paris, favorite food, series, plans). She must get a stranger (teacher) to mention each topic naturally through questions. Production (25 min): Full conversation simulation — Gabriela meets a British exchange student (teacher) at a Paris youth hostel common room. They must have a 4-minute natural conversation covering: where they're from, why they're in Paris, what they've done so far, and making plans to meet again. Debrief: highlight 3 especially smooth exchanges; work on 1 question tag that felt awkward.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'conversation script' for meeting someone new in Paris — create both sides of the dialogue (Gabriela + an exchange student from Australia). Use at least 5 question tags and 6 different question types (present simple, past simple, present perfect, future). Script should be 15–18 lines. Then read both parts aloud and record. Estimated time: 35–45 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Shopping in Paris — Boutiques, Souvenirs, Bargaining & Handling Money in English
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: quantifiers + comparative forms (review from Aula 18) for shopping comparisons — 'This one is more expensive than that one', 'Which one is cheaper?'; conditional type 1 introduction: 'If I buy this, I won't have money for dinner', 'If it's on sale, I'll get two'; vocabulary: boutique, souvenir, receipt, refund, exchange, fitting room, size (S/M/L/XL), sold out, in stock, discount, sale, price tag, cash, credit card, contactless payment, window shopping, browse; expressions: 'I'm just browsing, thanks', 'Do you have this in a different size?', 'Can I try this on?', 'Is this on sale?', 'Do you accept card?', 'Can I get a receipt?', 'I'd like to return this', 'Could I get a bag, please?'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 6 items (or item photos) on the table with price tags in euros. Gabriela has a 'budget' of €50 (a play-money envelope). She must decide what to buy and say why: 'I'll buy the scarf because it's cheaper than the bag and I love the color.' Activates comparatives and decision language. Presentation (15 min): Introduce Type 1 Conditional in shopping context: 'If I spend all my money on clothes, I won't be able to eat out.' Build 5 examples together on the board. Keep it practical — not abstract. Contrast with 'going to' and 'will' (already known). Practice (20 min): Gabriela has 3 scenarios (printed cards): (1) she finds a dress she loves but it's too expensive; (2) she wants to return a bag that broke; (3) she's looking for a specific souvenir and the shop doesn't have it. She prepares what to say for each and then role-plays with teacher. Written task: complete 5 Type 1 conditional sentences about shopping decisions. Production (25 min): Boutique simulation — teacher plays a Parisian shop assistant. Gabriela enters to shop for souvenirs and one item for herself. She must: browse, ask about sizes, try something on, handle a problem (wrong size), decide using a conditional, and pay. Teacher deliberately creates one 'complication' (card declined — she must handle it). Debrief: what vocabulary felt automatic? What needed prompting?
curriculo.homework: Imagine you have €100 to spend on souvenirs in Paris. Write a shopping list and for each item write one conditional sentence about your decision ('If I buy the perfume, I won't have money for the keychain'). Then, write a short dialogue (8–10 lines) between you and a shop assistant in a Paris boutique — include one problem. Estimated time: 30–35 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Telling Stories with Confidence — Narrative Tenses & How to Share What Happened Like a Natural Storyteller
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: integration of narrative tenses — past simple (main events), past continuous (background actions), present perfect (connection to now); NEW: past perfect — 'I had never seen anything like it before', 'By the time I arrived, the museum had already closed'; time expressions for storytelling: first, then, after that, suddenly, eventually, by the time, as soon as, while; vocabulary: storytelling signals — 'So, the thing is…', 'You won't believe this but…', 'And then, out of nowhere…', 'Long story short…', 'The funniest/scariest/weirdest part was…', 'And that's when I realized…'; narrative vocabulary: incident, adventure, unexpected, hilarious, awkward, embarrassing, terrifying, unforgettable
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher tells a short, dramatic 2-minute story about a (fictional) travel mishap in Paris — getting on the wrong metro, ending up in the wrong neighborhood — using all narrative tenses. Gabriela listens and identifies: past simple (main events), past continuous (background), past perfect (before the story started). Presentation (15 min): Focus on past perfect — the 'flashback' tense. Board examples anchored in Gabriela's world: 'I had already visited the Eiffel Tower when I realized I had left my phone at the hotel.' Concept check: what happened first? Timeline drawing on the board. Practice (20 min): Story reconstruction — teacher gives Gabriela 8 jumbled sentence strips from a travel story. She puts them in order, identifies the tense in each, and reads the full story aloud with natural pacing and emphasis. Then: fill in 5 past perfect sentences in a gap-fill about a Paris adventure. Production (25 min): 'Story Time' — Gabriela tells a 3-minute story (real or imagined) about something that happened to her during the week OR a scene from one of her favorite series, using all three narrative tenses plus at least 2 past perfect sentences. Teacher gives a story prompt if needed: 'Tell me about a time when something unexpected happened to you.' Record the story. Debrief: teacher plays back the recording and highlights 3 natural storytelling moments.
curriculo.homework: Write a story (12–15 sentences) titled 'The Day Everything Went Wrong in Paris' — fictional but believable. Use: past simple (main events), past continuous (background), past perfect (what happened before). Underline each tense in a different color. Also, find one 2-minute YouTube video of someone telling a travel story in English and note 5 time expressions they use. Estimated time: 40–50 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Talking About Health & Lifestyle — Eating Well, Exercising & Discussing Healthy Habits in English
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present simple for habits (review) + NEW: present perfect continuous — 'I've been exercising more lately', 'I've been eating healthier since I started school'; 'should / shouldn't' for advice: 'You should drink more water', 'You shouldn't skip breakfast'; vocabulary: balanced diet, nutrition, protein, carbohydrates, hydration, vitamins, portion size, junk food, processed food, meal prep, workout routine, cardio, strength training, stretching, sleep hygiene, mental health, stress, well-being; expressions: 'I've been trying to…', 'I cut down on…', 'I'm trying to eat cleaner', 'I work out 3 times a week', 'I've been feeling more energetic', 'I'm thinking about…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): 'Healthy or not?' — teacher reads 10 habits aloud ('Eating pizza every day', 'Walking 30 minutes daily', 'Sleeping 5 hours'). Gabriela gives a thumbs up/down AND a sentence: 'You shouldn't eat pizza every day because it has too much fat.' Activates 'should/shouldn't' and health vocabulary. Presentation (15 min): Introduce present perfect continuous using Gabriela's own interest in health: 'I've been working out more since January', 'I've been drinking more water lately.' Contrast with present perfect simple: 'I've exercised this week' (result) vs. 'I've been exercising for a month' (ongoing process). Board timeline with both. Practice (20 min): Conversation questions drill — teacher asks health-related questions, Gabriela must answer using present perfect continuous: 'How long have you been interested in healthy eating?' → 'I've been thinking about my diet for about a year.' 8 questions. Then: advice card activity — Gabriela receives 5 'patient profiles' with bad habits and writes a 'should/shouldn't' prescription for each. Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is being interviewed for a YouTube health channel called 'Teen Wellness Talk'. Teacher is the interviewer asking about her routine, habits, advice for other teens, and what she's been doing differently lately. Gabriela must speak for at least 3 minutes total, using PPC and should/shouldn't naturally. Debrief: how did the language feel? 2 strong moments + 1 correction.
curriculo.homework: Write your 'Weekly Health Report' in English (10–12 sentences): what you've been eating, how you've been exercising, how you've been feeling. Use present perfect continuous throughout. Also, write 5 health tips for a Brazilian friend going on a long trip to Europe — use 'you should / you shouldn't'. Estimated time: 30–40 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Talking About the Future — Dreams, Ambitions & Life After High School with Real Conditional Language
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: Type 1 Conditional review ('If I study hard, I'll get a scholarship') + NEW: Type 2 Conditional — hypothetical/imaginary: 'If I lived in Paris, I would study French every day', 'If I were fluent in English, I would apply to an international university'; 'I wish + past simple': 'I wish I spoke French', 'I wish I had more time'; vocabulary: ambition, career path, degree, major, apply, scholarship, gap year, exchange student, fluent, bilingual, achieve, goal, dream, aspiration, opportunity, university entrance, future plans; expressions: 'In the future, I'd love to…', 'My dream is to…', 'If everything goes well, I'll…', 'Ideally, I would…', 'I'm planning to…', 'I wish I could…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher gives Gabriela 3 dream scenario cards ('You are fluent in English', 'You live in Paris', 'You have a scholarship to a UK university') — she must say one sentence for each using 'If I… I would…' without preparation. Activates hypothetical thinking. Presentation (15 min): Contrast Type 1 (realistic) and Type 2 (hypothetical) on the board using Gabriela's real goals: 'If I practice English every day, I'll be fluent by 2027' (Type 1 — real possibility) vs. 'If I were already fluent, I would apply to Sciences Po in Paris' (Type 2 — imaginary, not yet real). Introduce 'I wish' as a shortcut for Type 2 feelings. Practice (20 min): Sentence transformation — 8 prompts to complete: 4 Type 1, 4 Type 2. Then: 'Dream Interview' warm-up — teacher asks questions, Gabriela responds mixing both conditional types: 'If you could study anywhere in the world, where would you go and why?' Production (25 min): Role-play — Gabriela is being interviewed by a journalist writing an article about 'Ambitious Brazilian Teenagers and Their International Dreams.' She must talk about her future plans, wishes, and hypothetical scenarios for 4 minutes, mixing Type 1, Type 2, and 'I wish' naturally.
